title: TOPO: immediate DomainTable unblock — topo_plans, topo_events, topo_event_links
description:
Per TOPO gap analysis 2026-07-02: add topo_plans, topo_events, and topo_event_links as DomainTables in SchemaConfig.topo() so TOPO can migrate its TopoPlan/TopoEvent model without waiting for the generic OHM ohm_intervals/ohm_plans primitive (OHM-dh9l / ADR-041).

This is the short-term pilot that unblocks TOPO while the core temporal model is being designed.

Acceptance:
1) SchemaConfig.topo() registers DomainTables: topo_plans, topo_events, topo_event_links.
2) Migration 0.x.y creates these tables if missing.
3) TOPO SDK can create a topo_plan, attach topo_events with start/end, and link events via topo_event_links.
4) At least one integration test demonstrates the 4-day maintenance window / annual outage example.
notes: Marked in_progress and assigned to hephaestus per Hephaestus dev-team report. Acceptance: register topo_plans/topo_events/topo_event_links DomainTables in SchemaConfig.topo(), migration, TOPO SDK create/attach/link, and 4-day maintenance window integration test.

title: TOPO: temporal event model (ADR-041) — ohm_intervals + ohm_plans core primitives
description:
P0 gap from TOPO gap analysis 2026-07-02. TOPO needs a first-class temporal event model in OHM: intervals with start_ts/end_ts, horizon (HISTORICAL/CURRENT/PLANNED/FORECAST), event_class + operating_state, and plan containers that group intervals by purpose (outage, PM schedule, campaign). TOPO can be unblocked immediately by adding topo_plans, topo_events, topo_event_links as DomainTables (ADR-040 pilot), but the generic OHM primitive (core ohm_intervals + ohm_plans or equivalent) is still required for cross-domain reuse.

Decision needed: whether ohm_intervals/ohm_plans become core tables or whether the first iteration ships as TOPO DomainTables and then generalizes.

Blocks: OHM-4qdk (plan container), OHM-ay5k (events), OHM-xggk (timeline rollup), OHM-vatf (temporal-aware Bayesian updates).

Acceptance:
1) ADR-041 written documenting core temporal model.
2) SchemaConfig.topo() registers topo_intervals, topo_plans, topo_event_links as DomainTables (immediate TOPO unblock).
3) Either core ohm_intervals/ohm_plans DDL lands, or a migration path from topo_* to core is documented.
4) SDK support: create_interval, create_plan, link_interval.
5) End-to-end test: 4-day planned maintenance window rolls up under an annual-outage plan and drives a Bayesian update.

title: ohmd SIGTERM shutdown hangs when worker threads are blocked (restart regression)
notes:
Likely cause: a worker thread is blocked on a long operation, so server.shutdown() waits indefinitely.
Blocks deployment of commit 5ea2353 (marketplace reason field) and any future code updates.

title: Configurable DuckLake table registry in sync/repair/recover methods
description:
OHM's DuckLake sync, repair_from_ducklake(), _recover_from_ducklake(), _auto_restore_if_empty(), _pull_from_ducklake_attached(), and check_ducklake_health() currently hardcode only [ohm_nodes, ohm_edges, ohm_observations]. If a domain uses additional tables (e.g., TOPO's 30+ tables), those tables are silently lost on crash/recovery.

Acceptance:
- SchemaConfig.ducklake_tables lists all tables to mirror (default: base 3; domains append their own).
- Optional per-table config: primary key, timestamp_col (fallback to updated_at/created_at).
- All DuckLake methods iterate schema.ducklake_tables instead of hardcoded list.
- check_ducklake_health() reports counts for every mirrored table.
- Integration test: simulate crash on a store with domain tables; repair_from_ducklake() restores all tables with matching row counts.

Depends on: domain DDL hook (previous P0). Blocks: TOPO migration.
notes: Implementation spec drafted by metis at /root/olympus/metis/memory/2026-06-30_topo_migration_spec.md. Proposes SchemaConfig.ducklake_tables property and refactor of all hardcoded [ohm_nodes, ohm_edges, ohm_observations] lists in ohm/graph/store.py to use the registry. Ready for dev review/implementation.
close_reason: Implemented in commit bb69d67: feat(ducklake): configurable table registry. Tests pass.

title: Domain DDL hook + migration sequencing in SchemaConfig.initialize_schema()
description:
OHM's SchemaConfig lets callers add node/edge/observation types, but there is no first-class hook for domain-specific SQL tables. TOPO needs tables like topo_prospects, topo_observations, topo_observation_assessments, etc. created alongside the base OHM schema in a single migration sequence.

Acceptance:
- SchemaConfig accepts a domain_tables: list[DomainTable] field with name, columns, primary key, indexes, and migration ordering.
- initialize_schema() creates base OHM tables first, then domain tables, then applies per-table migrations in declared order.
- Migrations are idempotent and versioned in ohm_meta.
- Existing TOPO tables can be declared and preserved during migration.
- Works in both OhmStore library mode and ohmd daemon mode.

Blocked by: none. Blocks: OHM-? (DuckLake registry).
close_reason: Implemented in commit 1342367: feat(schema): domain DDL hook + SchemaConfig.domain_tables. Tests pass (79 passed, 1 skipped in tests/test_domain_ddl.py + tests/test_ducklake_registry.py).

title: ohmd cannot be restarted via systemctl/SIGTERM (blocks code deployment)
description:
The live ohmd process (PID 1694538, /etc/ohm/ohmd.json, port 8710) cannot be restarted.

Symptoms:
- systemctl restart ohmd returns 0 but PID and start time remain unchanged.
- systemctl stop ohmd returns 0 but process continues running.
- systemctl kill -s SIGTERM ohmd returns 0 but process continues running.
- The process appears to ignore SIGTERM entirely.

Impact:
- Code updates cannot take effect on the live daemon without force-killing the process.
- The 2026-06-30 decision-node type fix (OHM-0abu) is deployed in /root/olympus/OHM/src but the live daemon is still running pre-fix code.
- scripts/deploy_ohmd.sh will fail at the restart/health-check step.

Likely cause:
- ohmd's shutdown handler catches SIGTERM but does not actually exit.
- May be related to the shutdown handler that wraps I/O in a 10s timeout (mentioned in override.conf OHM-8n9-fix).

Environment:
- PID 1694538, started 2026-06-30 14:38 EDT
- PYTHONPATH=/root/olympus/OHM/src
- /usr/local/bin/ohmd --config /etc/ohm/ohmd.json

Next step:
- Investigate signal handling in ohm/server/server.py or ohm/server/__main__.py.
- Reproduce in dev and ensure SIGTERM causes clean exit within TimeoutStopSec=15s.
- Until fixed, production code deployment requires manual SIGKILL, which is risky.
close_reason: Fixed in 86683d7: server.shutdown() now dispatched to daemon thread. tests/test_ohmd_shutdown.py passes 3/3. Live systemctl restart verified: PID changed from 1694538 to 1701033, daemon healthy on port 8710.

comments.author: root
comments.text: Ledger Roth twin skeleton is ready and passes local in-memory tests. Live deployment is waiting for this restart fix.

title: HTTP /node endpoint stores node_type='decision' as 'concept'
description:
The HTTP POST /node endpoint accepts node_type='decision' but persists the node with type='concept'. This breaks decision-specific endpoints such as /temporal/freshness, /temporal/mode-switch, and query_loop_status classification.

Repro:
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8710/node -d '{"label":"Test","node_type":"decision","id":"test_decision","created_by":"metis"}'

Expected: type='decision'. Actual: type='concept'.

Likely cause: handler not passing node_type, or validator coercing to concept without error.

Blocks Ledger pilot and any decision-aware temporal workflow.

Found during live daemon integration test 2026-06-30. See /root/olympus/metis/memory/2026-06-30_live_daemon_test_report.md
close_reason: Verified after restart: POST /node with node_type=decision now creates type='decision' (metis_restart_verify_decision_20260630). Test artifacts cleaned up via DELETE /node/*.

comments.author: root
comments.text:
Dev followup (commit 7f93066, OHM-0abu) fixed the underlying type-field alias issue. POST /node now accepts node_type='decision' correctly. Tests pass (test_type_field_aliases.py, 19 tests).

However, live verification is blocked because the ohmd process cannot be restarted via systemctl/SIGTERM. The live daemon is still running pre-fix code. Filed new P0 OHM-... for the restart blocker. Keep OHM-lc35 open until live daemon is restarted and verified.

title: Task routing: assigned OHM tasks do not appear in agent queues
description:
Agents check /tasks?assigned_to=<agent>&status=open on every session, but no tasks are returned even when work exists in Beads/OHM. This breaks the documented lifecycle where Atlas/Matt creates tasks and agents claim them.

Symptoms:
- Metis heartbeat: curl /tasks?assigned_to=metis&status=open returns [] every time.
- Beads has 19 open issues, including P1 features and P2 agent tasks.
- Nothing flows from Beads -> OHM task model -> agent action.

Likely causes (investigate):
1. Beads issues are not mirrored into OHM task nodes.
2. assignee field mismatch (e.g. metis vs metis@olympus.local vs agent-metis).
3. status mapping missing (Beads 'open' -> OHM 'open').
4. No automation/job syncs the two stores.

Acceptance:
- Creating a Beads issue with an assignee appears in /tasks for that agent within one sync cycle.
- Beads status changes update OHM task status bidirectionally.
- Agent can update OHM task status and see it reflected in Beads.
- Document the sync contract.
notes:
2026-07-02 (metis): Root cause confirmed. fetch_beads_issues() calls 'bd list --json', which returns records without 'assignee' or 'labels' fields. sync_beads_to_ohm_tasks() skips every issue because assignee is None. As a result POST /admin/sync-beads returns {created:0, updated:0, skipped:0, total:0} even though .beads/issues.jsonl has 50+ open issues. Fix: use 'bd list --json --all' or include assignee/labels in default JSON output, or read from JSONL fallback with field expansion.
2026-07-02 (metis): Fixed and verified live. Two parts:
1. Code: dcd474c enriched fetch_beads_issues() with assignee from .beads/issues.jsonl when bd list --json omits it.
2. Deployment: ohmd service cwd was '/', so it couldn't find .beads/issues.jsonl. Added WorkingDirectory=/root/olympus/OHM to systemd override. Restarted ohmd.
Verified: POST /admin/sync-beads returned {created:0, updated:10, skipped:39, total:49}. Hephaestus queue now shows OHM-sbtz P0 + OHM-9zae P1 + OHM-ezt5 P1 + OHM-tsxk P1 + OHM-bm5r P1.
2026-07-02 (metis): Child issues OHM-sbtz.1 (sync idempotency/dry_run) and OHM-sbtz.2 (task node validation) remain open P1 and are correctly filed under this parent. The original routing bug itself is closed.

title: Fix beads dependency table schema mismatch
description:
The rebuilt remote Dolt database has a `dependencies` table schema that the local `bd` binary does not recognize. `bd status`, `bd list --parent`, `bd create --parent`, and `bd dep` all fail with:

```
Error 1105: column "depends_on_id" could not be found in any table in scope
```

This blocks normal beads operations including closing shipped issues, linking parent/child tasks, and computing blocked IDs. Need to inspect the actual `dependencies` table columns and either (a) add a `depends_on_id` column/alias that `bd` expects, (b) migrate the table to the schema version expected by the installed `bd` binary, or (c) align the `bd` binary version with the remote schema.

Acceptance: `bd status`, `bd list --parent`, and `bd create --parent` all work without schema errors.
notes: Dev-team CI hygiene: OHM-zaow will stabilize the full pytest suite; that may also surface whether the schema mismatch affects test collection.
close_reason: Resolved in prior session. y9d4/g4bg: bootstrap recovery fixed schema. 1lj4: reconciliation done.

title: bd bootstrap broken with bd 1.0.5 — migration 0047 fails with 'table not found: wisps'
description:
bd 1.0.5 bootstrap (and bd init when sync.remote points at a remote with Dolt history) crashes on the second machine with:

Error: failed to open database: embeddeddolt: init schema: embeddeddolt: migrate: migration 0047_recompute_mixed_is_blocked.up.sql: Error 1146: table not found: wisps

Root cause is upstream bd bug #4176 (gastownhall/beads): wisps base-table creation lives in the main (synced) migration sequence, but the wisps tables are dolt_ignore'd. On a clone the main sequence runs first; 0047 references wisps unconditionally; the ignored sequence that would recreate the wisp tables runs after main and never gets a chance.

Workaround applied 2026-06-19:
1. bd init --reinit-local --discard-remote --destroy-token DESTROY-OHM --non-interactive (wipes local + remote refs/dolt/data)
2. bd import .beads/issues.jsonl (re-populates from git-tracked JSONL)
3. Metis's 3 new tasks (OHM-xwdq, OHM-1u78, OHM-lslz) lost from Dolt push — recreated manually (OHM-lslz epic + children)

Proper fix: upstream bd patch (move wisp table creation into ignored sequence, or make 0047 self-guarding). Track at https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/issues/4176.
close_reason: Resolved in prior session. y9d4/g4bg: bootstrap recovery fixed schema. 1lj4: reconciliation done.

comments.author: Matt Markham
comments.text: RESOLVED 2026-06-19: After Dolt pull from Metis's ref 3c854b98, dependency operations work correctly (bd dep add/remove/relate/link all succeed). The schema error was a transient state from the bootstrap recovery — Metis's pushed Dolt state appears to be self-consistent. The 'column depends_on_id not found' likely resolved itself when Metis's fresh Dolt data overwrote my locally-rebuilt state. Workaround confirmed: bd link <id1> <id2> --type parent-child works for linking children to an epic.

title: Remove OhmHandler.store class-level global — pass store via request scope
description:
HIGHEST severity cross-tenant data leak. Fix OhmHandler.store (src/ohm/server/server.py:2143) by removing the class-level assignment and passing the store via request scope (self.store on handler, captured in handler closure during server start, or via a TenantResolver).

Tests:
- tests/test_tenant.py: assert multi-tenant handler never queries without customer_id filter
- Add regression test: two tenants, A queries B's node id directly, expect 403/404

Files to touch:
- src/ohm/server/server.py (install_globals)
- src/ohm/server/handlers/*.py (replace OhmHandler.store with self.store)
- tests/test_tenant.py (new isolation tests)
notes:
Implemented factory pattern via make_configured_handler(store) in src/ohm/server/server.py. run_server no longer mutates OhmHandler.store as a class attribute; instead it constructs a per-class subclass that captures store via closure and binds it to self.store in __init__. ThreadedHTTPServer now uses make_configured_handler(store). 6 new tests in tests/test_tenant_isolation.py verify: factory returns distinct subclass, store bound as instance attribute, distinct stores produce distinct handlers, mid-flight class attr mutation does NOT corrupt in-flight handlers, current_store property returns the bound instance store in both single-tenant and multi-tenant-no-customer paths.

Pre-existing test failures (4 in test_server.py, test_integration_multitenant.py) confirmed to exist on pristine main — not introduced by this change. Out of scope for OHM-1s14.1: tokens, roles, config, rate_limit_store — those are tracked as OHM-1s14.2/3/4 and OHM-1s14.5 (isolation test suite).
close_reason: Implemented: make_configured_handler(store) factory binds store via closure + __init__; run_server no longer mutates OhmHandler.store; 6 new tests in tests/test_tenant_isolation.py. All pre-existing tests still pass (4 pre-existing failures confirmed on pristine main, unrelated). Multi-tenant integration scenarios (tenant A reading tenant B's resources) covered by OHM-1s14.5 isolation test suite.

title: Tenant-scope global audit (OHM-ym2f remediation)
description:
Audit finding from prior session (in bd memory only, never tracked as a bead): 5 module-/class-level globals in the OHM daemon store state without proper tenant scoping. Re-validated against current code in src/ohm/server/server.py.

## Findings (re-verified)

### HIGHEST — OhmHandler.store (full cross-tenant data leak)
- File: src/ohm/server/server.py:2143
- Code: \OhmHandler.store = store\ assigns the single OhmStore instance to a class attribute shared across ALL HTTP threads.
- In multi_tenant mode the store is supposed to scope queries to customer_id, but the global reference means any handler that forgets to filter by customer_id sees data from every tenant. This is a single source of cross-tenant data leak.
- Fix: remove the class attribute; pass store via self.store on the request (handler closure) OR introduce a TenantResolver that returns the correct store per request.

### HIGH — OhmHandler.tokens (flat namespace, agent-name collisions)
- File: src/ohm/server/server.py:2146
- Single dict \{token_hash: roles_dict}\ where keys are agent names not scoped by tenant.
- Two tenants that pick the same agent name collide on role lookup.
- Fix: scope tokens by customer_id: \{customer_id: {token_hash: roles}}\.

### HIGH — OhmHandler.roles (flat namespace)
- File: src/ohm/server/server.py:2148
- Same problem as tokens — \{agent_name: role}\ is global.
- Fix: \{customer_id: {agent_name: role}}\.

### MEDIUM — OhmHandler.config (single config dict)
- File: src/ohm/server/server.py:2144
- The full server config (including customer_tokens, multi_tenant flag, paths) is one global.
- Per-tenant overrides (rate limits, webhook secrets) cannot be expressed.
- Fix: per-tenant config overlay; top-level config provides defaults, TenantManager provides overrides.

### LOW — _rate_limit_store (IP-only keying)
- File: src/ohm/server/server.py:120, used at 1036–1059
- Module-level dict keyed by client_ip only. A noisy tenant can exhaust another tenant's quota headroom via shared IP (NAT / corporate proxy).
- Fix: key by (client_ip, customer_id) tuple.

## Already safe (verified 2026-06-18)

- \_webhook_registry\ — keyed by customer_id (line 142, 1892–1894)
- \_sse_subscribers\ — values carry customer_id (line 900)
- \_bayesian_network_cache\ — cache_key includes customer_id (line 334, OHM-g4os fix)
- \_build_customer_token_lookup\ — structured for multi-tenant via customer_id key

## Test plan

Add tests under tests/test_tenant.py that:
1. Start daemon in multi_tenant mode with two tenants (A, B).
2. Authenticate as tenant A's agent.
3. Attempt to read tenant B's nodes — must 403/404, not return data.
4. Verify rate limit counters are isolated per (ip, customer_id).
5. Verify role lookup for tenant A's agent name does NOT return tenant B's roles.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] No class-level / module-level mutable state used as a tenant-shared resource
- [ ] All cross-tenant access attempts rejected with audit log entry
- [ ] Tests pass for both single-tenant (no regression) and multi-tenant (isolation) modes
- [ ] ADR added or referenced documenting the tenant-scoping pattern
close_reason:
Audit epic complete. All 5 children shipped:
- OHM-1s14.1: store bound via factory closure (make_configured_handler)
- OHM-1s14.2: roles scoped by customer_id (_lookup_role helper)
- OHM-1s14.3: per-tenant config overlay (current_config property, allowlist)
- OHM-1s14.4: rate limit keyed by (ip, customer_id)
- OHM-1s14.5: multi-tenant isolation test suite (26 tests)

26 tests in test_tenant_isolation.py covering store closure, role lookup, scoped role integration, webhook registry, SSE subscribers, Bayesian cache, rate limit isolation, and config overlay. All pass. No regressions (3 pre-existing failures on pristine main, unrelated).

title: OHM Daemon Stability: DuckLake Sync Causing 500s and SEGV Crashes
description:
## Problem

The OHM daemon (ohmd) is experiencing three interacting issues causing 500 errors on writes and intermittent SEGV crashes:

### 1. DuckLake 'Unique file handle conflict' (Root cause of 500s)

The sync thread (`_ducklake_sync_loop`) calls `pull_from_ducklake()` every 60s, which opens a separate DuckDB connection to the DuckLake catalog file via `_pull_from_ducklake_legacy()`. But the main `self.conn` already has DuckLake attached as an alias (`ohm_lake`). When the sync thread opens `duckdb.connect(ducklake_path, read_only=True)`, DuckDB throws:

```
_duckdb.BinderException: Unique file handle conflict: Cannot attach 'ohm_lake' - 
the database file '/var/lib/ohm/ohm_lake.ducklake' is already attached by 
database '__ducklake_metadata_ohm_lake'
```

This cascades: the sync thread holds the write lock while failing, blocking concurrent POST /node requests for 12-70 seconds. Those requests time out and return 500.

Evidence: 9 out of 56 requests in the last hour returned 500. Write latencies of 47-112 seconds observed.

### 2. SEGV Crashes

Daemon SEGV'd at 05:25 today (auto-restarted by systemd). 18+ corrupted DB backup files in /var/lib/ohm/ dating back to May 19. DuckDB + DuckLake under concurrent access from sync thread + HTTP handlers hits a segfault condition.

### 3. Read-only Connection Fails Silently

`_read_conn` initialization fails because DuckDB requires matching configuration between read-only and read-write connections. All reads fall back to `self.conn`, increasing contention on the single write connection.

Evidence from startup log:
```
Read-only connection unavailable (reads will share write conn): Binder Error...
```

### 4. Sync Thread Degrades Continuously

DuckLake sync reports '169 orphaned rows' every 60 seconds. This never resolves and consumes resources on each cycle.

## Immediate Fix (A)

Disable DuckLake sync by setting `sync_interval_seconds: 0` in /etc/ohm/ohmd.json, then `systemctl restart ohmd`. This stops 500s immediately.

## Proper Fixes (B-E)

### B. Fix DuckLake sync to use existing attached alias

`_pull_from_ducklake_legacy()` should query `ohm_lake.*` tables through `self.conn` (where DuckLake is already attached) instead of opening a second connection. This eliminates the file handle conflict entirely.

In store.py line ~2568, replace:
```python
ducklake = duckdb.connect(ducklake_path, read_only=True)
```
with a query through `self.conn` using the already-attached `ohm_lake` alias.

### C. Fix read-only connection initialization

Add matching extension loads and config to `_read_conn` initialization so it matches the write connection configuration. DuckDB requires this for read-only connections to coexist.

### D. Crash resilience

- Add CHECKPOINT on startup before serving requests
- Consider WAL-mode auto-checkpoint settings
- 18 corrupted DB files suggest data loss risk on each crash

### E. Reduce write lock scope

`_write_lock` (RLock) serializes ALL writes. Under burst load (6+ agents), requests queue. Consider finer-grained locking per table or an async write queue with batch commits.

## Evidence

- 9/56 requests returned 500 in the last hour
- Write latencies: 12s, 37s, 47s, 58s, 70s, 112s
- Read latencies: 10-20ms (unaffected)
- 2 successful writes out of 5 attempts in one test (60% failure rate)
- 18 corrupted DB backups since May 19
- Daemon memory: 765MB RSS, peak 737MB
- Two instances running: main (8710) + ranch (8711)
- DuckLake sync errors every 60s: '169 orphaned rows'
close_reason: Resilience fixes applied: circuit breaker (max_retries), graceful sync degradation (push/pull isolated), time travel graceful degradation, WARNING-level logging for configured-but-unavailable DuckLake, redundant import cleanup. 40 DuckLake tests + 223 core tests passing.

title: Post-write suggestions on node creation
description: When an agent creates a node via POST /node or POST /scratch, include a suggestions object in the response with: (1) similar_nodes — top 3 semantic matches from embedding similarity, (2) shared_tags — existing nodes sharing tags, (3) orphan_warnings — if the new node has no edges, suggest connecting to similar nodes. One semantic search query per node creation, returned inline in the response. Socrates would have seen pattern-frame-control-and-gate the moment he created his manipulation pattern node.
design: Call semantic_search on the new node's content after creation. Cache embedding computation. Return alongside the created node data. Skip if Ollama unavailable (suggestions: []).
acceptance_criteria: POST /node and POST /scratch return suggestions object with similar_nodes (max 3), shared_tags (max 3), orphan_warnings (if no edges). Response time increase < 200ms.
close_reason: Closed

title: Epic: OHM Proactive Discoverability
description:
Move graph discovery from pull to push. When agents write nodes, they should see suggestions inline. When they create islands, they should be warned. When they're new, they should get oriented. The graph should feel like it's paying attention, not waiting for the right question.
Source: Socrates + Atlas feedback (2026-06-06). Both agents independently flagged discoverability as the core gap. Features existed — they couldn't find them.
notes:
Implemented OHM-tr71.1 (post-write suggestions). Committed as 9824824 and pushed to main. 13 tests passing (8 unit + 5 integration). Live-verified against daemon: 3 similar nodes returned on node creation, 3 shared tags on tagged nodes, orphan warning on unlinked nodes, neighborhood created_by filter working, search tip on empty results.
Implemented OHM-tr71.2 (edge suggestions) and OHM-tr71.3 (islands endpoint). Both live and tested. Edge suggestions return related_edges, edge_patterns, orphan_resolved. Islands endpoint returns 11 islands (≥2 nodes), 205 orphans, 75.4% coverage. Union-Find implementation. 27 tests total.
close_reason: Closed

title: OHM-a5rz.18: /search leaks fragments by default
description:
BUG: /search has no include_l0 parameter. It queries ohm_nodes WHERE deleted_at IS NULL with no type filter, so fragments appear in every search result. Stats and neighborhood correctly exclude L0 by default; search doesn't. ADR-019 says fragments are 'unreliable, excluded by default.'

Live reproduction: GET /search?q=GraphRAG returns the fragment node. Should return 0 results without include_l0=true.

Fix: Add type != 'fragment' to default search WHERE clause. Add include_l0 query param. When include_l0=true or absent (backward compat for explicit opt-in), include fragments. Default should exclude them — matching stats/neighborhood behavior.

Acceptance: GET /search?q=X returns 0 fragments by default. GET /search?q=X&include_l0=true returns fragments. Test coverage required.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 7e8236e. /search now excludes fragments by default. Added include_l0 query parameter. Also fixed validate_layer() to accept L0.

title: OHM-a5rz.17: connects_to edges never created in scratch()
description:
BUG: scratch() passes connects_to to create_node(), which validates the target node IDs exist but never creates L0 edges to them. The only way fragments get linked is via _auto_link_fragment (label-substring matching), which is unrelated to connects_to.

Live reproduction: POST /scratch with connects_to=['concept-and-gate-control-plane'] creates the fragment node but 0 edges to that target. neighborhood query at L0 depth returns empty edge set.

Fix: After create_node() returns in scratch(), iterate connects_to and create L0 CONTEXT_OF edges for each target. Edge creation must use create_edge() with layer='L0', edge_type='CONTEXT_OF', confidence=0.5, provenance='scratch_explicit'.

Acceptance: POST /scratch with connects_to creates L0 CONTEXT_OF edges to each target. SDK g.scratch(connects_to=[...]) same. Test coverage required.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 7e8236e. scratch() now creates explicit L0 CONTEXT_OF edges for connects_to targets. Returns explicit_links alongside auto_links. 4 query tests + 4 server tests passing.

title: Add g.scratch() to SDK
description: Add scratch(content, tags=None) method to HttpGraph SDK. Single HTTP POST to /scratch. Returns fragment dict. This is the agent-facing interface that makes L0 writes nearly free.
acceptance_criteria: g.scratch('hunch text') returns dict with id, content, agent_name, created_at. g.scratch('hunch', tags=['semi']) passes tags. Raises on empty content.
close_reason: Done: g.scratch() in both Graph and HttpGraph SDK. Live tested via HTTP. Same connects_to bug as .4.

title: Add POST /scratch endpoint
description: POST /scratch accepts {content: string} and creates ohm_nodes entry with type='fragment'. Agent name from auth token. No type selection, no confidence required. Auto-extract URLs and confidence patterns from content. Returns 201.
design: Handler wraps create_node() with node_type='fragment'. Value is friction reduction, not architecture. No new table.
acceptance_criteria: POST /scratch with content returns 201 with node. type='fragment', created_by from auth, label auto-gen from first 80 chars. URL extraction works. Empty content returns 400.
close_reason: Done: POST /scratch endpoint works. Live tested: curl POST returns 201 with fragment node. Auto-extracts URLs, auto-generates label, sets scratch=true. Bug found: connects_to param validated but edges never created — new issue filed.

title: Add L0 edge types to schema
description: Register L0 edge types in LAYER_EDGE_TYPES. L0 edges represent thinking-context connections, not semantic claims. Types: CONTEXT_OF (auto-linked), INSPIRED_BY (explicit), CONTRADICTS_FRAG, REFINES_FRAG.
acceptance_criteria: validate_edge_type('L0', 'CONTEXT_OF') returns True. Invalid L0 edge types (CAUSES, SUPPORTS) are rejected. Existing layer types unaffected.
close_reason: Done: L0 edge types (CONTEXT_OF, INSPIRED_BY, CONTRADICTS_FRAG, REFINES_FRAG) in LAYER_EDGE_TYPES['L0']. Verified in schema.py.

title: Add fragment node type to ohm_nodes
description: Register fragment as valid node type in schema validator. Fragments are ohm_nodes with type='fragment'. No new table. This is the minimal schema change that unlocks everything else.
design: 3-column effective schema: id, content, agent_name as created_by. All other fields use existing ohm_nodes columns with nullable defaults. No migration — just type registry update.
acceptance_criteria: validate_node_type('fragment') returns True. create_node(label='hunch', node_type='fragment') succeeds. Existing node types unaffected.
close_reason: Done: commit ebc83fe added 'fragment' to VALID_NODE_TYPES and EXEMPT_CROSS_LINK_NODE_TYPES. Verified live.

title: L0 Storage: ohm_nodes with type=fragment
description:
Decision: L0 fragments stored as ohm_nodes with type='fragment', not in a separate ohm_fragments table. Rationale: (1) reuses existing search, semantic search, and edge infrastructure, (2) avoids schema duplication, (3) fragments are nodes. ADR: OHM-l0.

## Context
Original design proposed separate ohm_fragments table. Self-review critique identified over-engineering — fragment links duplicate ohm_edges, search wouldn't cover new table, 16 columns for nearly-free write. Storing as ohm_nodes gives search for free.
design: See /root/olympus/OHM/docs/l0-design.md and l0-design-critique.md
close_reason: Done: fragment type uses ohm_nodes, no new table. ADR-019 design implemented. Verified in schema.py VALID_NODE_TYPES + EXEMPT_CROSS_LINK_NODE_TYPES.

title: L0 Thinking Layer
description: Add a thinking layer (L0) below L1 in OHM. L0 captures fragments, hunches, and raw associations that precede structured knowledge. Writes are nearly free; structure emerges from use. See /root/olympus/OHM/docs/l0-design.md and l0-design-critique.md.
design: Per self-review critique: fragments stored as ohm_nodes with type='fragment' (no new table). L0 edges use existing ohm_edges with layer='L0'. Ship /scratch endpoint first; everything else emerges from use.
close_reason: Closed

title: BUG: Migration failures silently swallowed — DB enters inconsistent state
description: In schema.py _apply_migrations, each migration statement is wrapped in try/except Exception: pass. This catches ALL exceptions, not just column-already-exists errors. A real schema corruption silently passes, returning a DB that may be missing columns, indexes, or constraints. The store is then used as if healthy. Critical because silent partial migrations can cause data loss or silent corruption.
close_reason: Fixed by OpenCode in d4617a7 — MigrationError raised instead of silent pass

title: SECURITY: SQL injection in quack_query() — sql and token interpolated into SQL
description: query_remote() in quack.py interpolates both the uri and sql parameters directly into an SQL string using f-string formatting. The sql parameter is arbitrary SQL provided by the caller. If any caller passes user-controlled input (HTTP param, body field), this is a full SQL injection vulnerability. Token is also interpolated which could leak credentials.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 400354c

title: SECURITY: X-Tenant-ID header allows any agent to access any tenant
description: The _customer_id property in server.py returns the X-Tenant-ID header value without any authorization check. Any authenticated agent with a valid agent token can set X-Tenant-ID to any customer_id and access that tenant's data. Tenant isolation is completely broken for agent tokens. This must be fixed before any customer data goes live.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 400354c

title: BUG: Migration failures silently swallowed — DB enters inconsistent state (Critical)
description:
In schema.py _apply_migrations (lines 998-1003), each migration statement is wrapped in try: conn.execute(stmt) except Exception: pass. This catches ALL exceptions, not just column-already-exists errors. If a migration fails for a serious reason (disk full, type mismatch, permission denied), the error is silently swallowed and the schema version is updated as if the migration succeeded. The database ends up in an inconsistent state: meta says version X, but DDL for version X was never applied.

Remediation: Only catch specific expected errors (e.g., CatalogException for duplicate columns). Raise MigrationError for unexpected failures. Do NOT update schema_version if any migration statement failed.
close_reason: Fixed: Migration failures no longer silently swallowed. Only catch specific errors (already exists, duplicate, not-implemented index). Unexpected failures now raise correctly.

title: SECURITY: SQL injection in quack_query() — sql and token interpolated into SQL (Critical)
description:
The query_remote() function in quack.py (lines 370-371) interpolates both the uri and sql parameters directly into a SQL query string using f-string formatting. The sql parameter is arbitrary SQL provided by the caller with no parameterization. The token is also string-interpolated.

Impact: Any code path calling query_remote() with user-influenced sql or token arguments can achieve arbitrary SQL execution.

Remediation: Use parameterized queries where DuckDB supports them, or use the create_secret() mechanism (already exists in the same file, line 377) to avoid embedding tokens in SQL strings. Validate sql against injection patterns before passing to quack_query().
close_reason: Fixed: SQL injection in quack_query() — sql param now validated: rejects single quotes and semicolons outside comments. URI and token already had validation.

title: SECURITY: X-Tenant-ID header allows any agent to access any tenant (Critical)
description:
The _customer_id property in server.py (lines 559-579) returns the X-Tenant-ID header value without any authorization check. Any authenticated agent with a valid agent token can set X-Tenant-ID to any valid customer ID and access that tenant's entire graph — read and write. The current_store property routes to the tenant's isolated DuckDB via tenant_manager.get_store(customer_id), but there is no mapping or verification that the agent is authorized for the specified tenant.

Impact: Full cross-tenant data breach (read + write) by any authenticated agent.

Remediation: Add an authorization mapping (agent_name -> [allowed_customer_ids]). Verify agent is permitted for the X-Tenant-ID tenant before routing. Admin tokens bypass the check. Alternatively, require customer tokens (not agent tokens) for tenant-scoped access.
close_reason: Fixed: X-Tenant-ID restricted to admin-role agents only. Non-admin agents get X-Tenant-ID ignored. _authenticated_agent stored in _authenticate() for role checks. Tests updated.

title: DuckDB concurrent access strategy for per-tenant connections
description: TenantManager LRU cache returns OhmStore instances to concurrent HTTP threads. DuckDB is single-writer — multiple threads writing to the same OhmStore.conn will serialize at the C level but Python-level TOCTOU races exist in write_node(), write_edge(), delete_node(). The current OhmStore._lock (RLock) only covers execute()/execute_one(); ~46 direct self.conn.execute() calls in store.py + server.py bypass it. Background threads (auto-embedding, DuckLake sync) also access conn without serialization. For multi-tenancy this gets worse: N tenants means N open connections each potentially hit by M concurrent threads. Need a decision and implementation: (a) per-tenant write serialization lock (extend _lock to cover all conn access), (b) connection-per-request with DuckDB read-only mode for GETs, (c) Quack per tenant for multi-writer, (d) DuckLake for tenants (each tenant syncs to its own lake). Option (a) is simplest but creates head-of-line blocking; option (d) matches ADR-012 architecture. This must be resolved before tss4.2 and tss4.4 ship.

title: Audit ALL process-global state for tenant safety (webhook registry, rate limit, metrics)
description: OHM-g4os fixes the Bayesian cache, but it is one instance of a class of bug. server.py has process-global state that is NOT tenant-scoped: _webhook_registry (server.py:88) — Tenant A registers a webhook and it fires on Tenant B's changes, a direct data leak; _rate_limit_store (server.py:67) — keyed by client IP not customer key, causing noisy-neighbor and shared-IP collisions; _metrics (server.py:79) — aggregate only. Any one of these silently crosses tenant boundaries with no error. This must be audited comprehensively before tss4.4 ships.

title: Validate customer_id against path traversal before filesystem use
description: customer_id is used to build a filesystem path (/var/lib/ohm/tenants/{customer_id}/ohm.duckdb in tss4.2). If unvalidated, customer_id='../../etc' or similar lets a caller read/write/delete arbitrary paths — a critical security hole in the provisioning and routing paths. This must be enforced at every entry point that accepts customer_id (provisioning API, X-Tenant-ID header, customer key resolution).

title: Fix Bayesian cache key — add customer_id to prevent cross-tenant inference bleed
description: The module-level _NETWORK_CACHE in bayesian.py is keyed by (edge_types, layers, root_nodes, preferred_edges, max_nodes, root_prior, leak, default_prob) with no tenant dimension. In multi-tenant mode, two customers with overlapping node IDs will collide in the cache and silently receive each other's Bayesian networks — wrong posteriors, wrong VoI rankings, wrong ATEs. This is the most dangerous correctness gap in the multi-tenancy plan because it produces no error, only wrong answers.

title: Server Request Routing — current_store property for tenant context
description: Route all OHM API requests to correct store (core or tenant) based on authenticated identity. Replace direct self.store references with self.current_store property. ~50-80 call sites in server.py.

title: TenantManager Module — provisioning, routing, caching, lifecycle
description: New module ohm/tenant.py for provisioning, routing, caching, and lifecycle management of per-customer OHM instances. Each customer gets an isolated DuckDB at /var/lib/ohm/tenants/{customer_id}/ohm.duckdb. LRU cache with configurable max size. Idle eviction. Secure deprovision. Seeds agent role nodes from domain+tier.

title: Server Auth — Customer API Key Support alongside agent tokens
description: Add customer API key authentication alongside existing agent token auth. Customer keys resolve to tenant instances. Agent tokens continue resolving to core OHM (unchanged behavior). _authenticate() returns (agent_name, customer_id_or_none) tuple.

title: OHM Multi-Tenancy: Single-engine, isolated customer instances, domain templates, cross-customer pattern learning
description: Single-engine, multi-tenant architecture. One ohmd, many isolated DuckDB instances. Domain templates via SchemaConfig JSON. Cross-customer pattern learning (opt-in). Lazy migrations. Enables TeamWork AI platform. See OHM_MULTI_TENANCY_BACKLOG.md for full spec.
notes:
Métis audit (May 25): 38/40 children closed. Architecture is solid — feature-flagged, isolated DuckDB, proper security. Remaining open: OHM-yl1f (encryption at rest — reopened, was closed prematurely, zero encryption code), OHM-kbwl (backup/restore — code exists in tenant.py, HTTP endpoints in server.py, just closed), OHM-tss4.15 (email channel, blocked on glni). New issues filed: OHM-axdj (home_services obs types), OHM-sgfr (construction obs types), OHM-5gi5 (healthcare obs types), OHM-ru6l (manufacturing topo inheritance), OHM-yv35 (concurrent write test), OHM-8d54 (meta.json hot write), OHM-tss4.1.1 (seed agents). Test results: 14/14 tenant, 30/30 auth, 21/21 API, 20/20 patterns, 12/13 integration (1 concurrent write failure).

---
Métis final audit summary (May 25):

CLOSED (code verified): 38/40 children closed. All P0 core items shipped (TenantManager, auth, routing, templates, quotas, migrations, feature flag, security review).

REOPENED: OHM-yl1f (encryption at rest) — closed prematurely, zero encryption code exists.

CRITICAL P0 SECURITY BUGS (from code review, pre-existing):
- OHM-tss4.19: X-Tenant-ID header allows any agent to access any tenant (full cross-tenant data breach)
- OHM-exur: Migration failures silently swallowed — DB enters inconsistent state
- OHM-5gpr: SQL injection in quack_query()

These 3 P0 bugs MUST be fixed before multi_tenant=True is safe in production.

TEMPLATE GAPS (7 new P2 issues filed):
- home_services: 4/8 observation types missing (axdj)
- construction: 3/7 observation types missing (sgfr)
- healthcare: 3/6 observation types missing (5gi5)
- manufacturing: only 2/18 topo types inherited (ru6l)
- seed agents: 0 nodes provisioned per domain (tss4.1.1, P3)

PERFORMANCE/BUGS (P2):
- yv35: Concurrent write test fails (HTTP connection reset, not data integrity)
- 8d54: meta.json hot-write on every request for quota tracking

TEST RESULTS: 14/14 tenant, 30/30 auth, 21/21 API, 20/20 patterns, 12/13 integration (1 concurrent write failure).
close_reason: 80% complete. Core multi-tenancy shipped. Remaining work tracked as individual bugs/features. Epic was a phantom blocker making backlog look larger than reality.

title: POST /tasks requires 'id' field - should auto-generate if missing
description: POST /tasks returns 400 'Missing required fields: id' when id is omitted. The id should be auto-generated like other node creation endpoints (POST /agent/synthesis auto-generates). Making id optional would improve DX for task creation.
close_reason: Closed

title: /adjustment crashes with Bayesian CPD error for most node pairs
description: GET /adjustment?cause=X&effect=Y crashes with: "Failed to build Bayesian network: ('CPD defined on variable not in the model', <TabularCPD representing P(career_paleontologist:2)>)" - appears to include stale/irrelevant CPDs in the model. The career_paleontologist variable is from Mara's college decision nodes and shouldn't be in every causal model.
close_reason: Closed

title: /source_reliability endpoint not found - returns 404
description: GET /source_reliability/metis and GET /source_reliability?agent=metis both return 404. The AGENTS.md and SDK docs reference this endpoint but it doesn't exist in the route table. Needs implementation.
close_reason: Closed

title: /compound_confidence/{node} endpoint not found - returns 404
description: GET /compound_confidence/hormuz_and_gate returns 404. Referenced in AGENTS.md as a feature but not implemented in route table.
close_reason: Closed

title: /suggest returns empty results for orphan_connect and cooccurrence methods
description: GET /suggest?method=orphan_connect returns 0 suggestions. GET /suggest?method=cooccurrence returns 0 suggestions. Only shared_tags works (20 results). With 50 orphans, orphan_connect should find suggestions based on tags/content.
close_reason: Closed

title: /suggest (shared_tags) returns results with empty fields
description: GET /suggest?method=shared_tags returns 20 results but from_id, from_label, to_id, to_label are empty strings in the raw JSON. The data is present (shared_tag_count works) but node details aren't being populated.
close_reason: Closed

title: POST /edge requires 'from', 'to', 'type' fields but docs say 'from_node', 'to_node', 'edge_type'
description: POST /edge returns 400 'Missing required fields: from, to, type' when called with from_node/to_node/edge_type fields. The field names are inconsistent with the rest of the API which uses from_node/to_node/edge_type in responses. Either accept both or standardize.
close_reason: Closed

title: POST /edge returns 404 for valid node when using full ID format
description: POST /edge with to='concept-demand-rationing' returns 404 'Edge to_node does not exist'. The node exists but under a different ID (demand_rationing_accelerating_bilateral_buyer_rela). Node ID lookup should be more forgiving or return a helpful message suggesting the correct ID.
close_reason: Won't fix: 404 is correct behavior when POST /edge uses a non-existent node ID. The node 'concept-demand-rationing' doesn't exist — the actual node has a different ID. This is expected validation.

title: Health graph score 0.73 but orphan rate still high (50/199 = 25%)
description: /health/graph returns health_score=0.73 with 50 orphans out of 199 nodes (25%). The health score formula may not properly weight orphan rate. Previous orphan rate was 0% after manual cleanup, grew back to 25%. Need orphan triage tooling.
close_reason: By design: health_score = 1 - (orphans+low_conf+stale)/total_nodes. The formula intentionally doesn't make orphan rate the sole determining factor — a graph with many high-confidence connected nodes can still score 0.73 with 25% orphans.

title: /health/agents returns agents with empty fields
description: GET /health/agents returns 9 agent entries but all fields (name, is_stale, last_seen) are empty/question marks. The /agents endpoint returns data with some fields but also mostly empty. Agent metadata isn't being populated properly.
close_reason: Closed

title: /suggest_causes returns college/career nodes in geopolitics context
description: GET /suggest_causes?node=hormuz_and_gate returns suggestions like 'Consider adding CAUSES edge from decision-college-2026 to path-hollins-interdisciplinary'. The suggest_causes algorithm isn't filtering by domain, producing irrelevant suggestions from unrelated subgraphs (Ariana's college decision nodes mixed with geopolitics).
close_reason: Data quality issue, not a code bug: suggest_causes uses graph topology which doesn't know about semantic domains. Mixed domains (college/career mixed with geopolitics) are expected when data from multiple domains co-exists. Future enhancement: domain filtering.

title: PATCH /node returns 405 Method Not Allowed
description: PATCH /node/hormuz_and_gate returns 405 with 'Method not allowed — use: DELETE, GET'. There's no way to update node content/metadata without deleting and recreating. Need PATCH support for updating node fields.
close_reason: Closed

title: /ate returns ATE=0.0 and risk_ratio=1.0 for Hormuz→demand-rationing
description: GET /ate?cause=hormuz_and_gate&effect=concept-demand-rationing returns ATE=0.0, indicating no causal effect detected. This is clearly wrong for this well-established causal relationship. The Bayesian model is only using 3 nodes (algorithmic_and_gate, journalistic_and_gate, demand-rationing), missing the actual causal chain. Model construction needs to be scoped to the relevant subgraph.
close_reason: Fixed: BFS hops increased 3→6 for longer causal chains; added connectivity diagnostic in compute_ate — when ATE=0 and cause not an ancestor of effect, returns error with explanation instead of misleading ATE=0.0/RR=1.0

title: /listen returns 0 events even for recent timeframes
description: GET /listen?since=2026-05-21T20:00:00 returns 0 events. The change feed appears to be empty/not working. This is likely because the events table was cleared during the daemon restart and hasn't been repopulated.
close_reason: Closed

title: POST endpoints return 401 with valid Bearer token via curl/requests but work via SDK
description: POST /tasks, POST /sync, POST /node, POST /edge, POST /outcome all return 401 with correct Bearer token when called via curl or Python requests library. However, POST works when called via urllib.request.Request (same method the SDK uses). Root cause: shell variable interpolation was mangling the token. NOT A BUG - works correctly with proper token handling. Closing as cannot reproduce.

title: WAL corruption causes data loss on hard shutdown — DuckDB WAL gets corrupted after SIGKILL, leaving DB empty
description:
When ohmd is killed with SIGKILL (OOM, power loss, docker kill), DuckDB's WAL file becomes corrupted. On restart, DuckDB refuses to open the database, effectively wiping all data. This has happened twice today. Recovery requires manually deleting the .wal file and re-seeding from DuckLake.

PARTIALLY FIXED (existing recovery code):
- db.py:52-73 catches IOException/InternalException on connect, attempts DuckLake recovery, falls back to WAL deletion
- store.py:172-177 additionally catches FatalException (unreliable — DuckDB FatalException may abort before Python catches it)
- store.py:195-315 _recover_from_ducklake() rebuilds from DuckLake mirror
- schema.py:687-690 PRAGMA checkpoint before each migration

REMAINING GAPS:
1. NO empty-table check: If DuckDB opens successfully but tables are empty (data in WAL was lost), system silently starts with empty graph. No code checks SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ohm_nodes == 0.
2. NO CHECKPOINT on shutdown: store.py close() (line 1265-1268) just calls conn.close() without PRAGMA checkpoint. Uncommitted WAL data is vulnerable.
3. NO CHECKPOINT on SIGTERM: server.py run_server() (line 2382-2393) has no checkpoint on shutdown signal.
4. FatalException handling is unreliable: store.py:174 catches it, but DuckDB FatalException may abort the process at C level before Python catches it.
5. PRAGMA checkpoint before initial DDL: schema.py:629-648 initialize_schema() has no checkpoint before the initial DDL statements (only before migrations).

Fix: (1) Add empty-table check + auto-restore on startup, (2) Add CHECKPOINT on shutdown in store.py and server.py, (3) Add CHECKPOINT before initial DDL in initialize_schema().

title: Bayesian: noisy-OR leak clamp [0.01, 0.5] destroys confidence modulation — high-confidence edges get same leak as low-confidence
description: In _build_noisy_or_cpd(), leak = leak_probability * (1 - avg_confidence) is clamped to [0.01, 0.5]. This destroys confidence modulation: an edge with confidence=0.95 gets leak = max(0.01, min(0.5, 0.15 * 0.05)) = 0.01, while confidence=0.5 gets leak = max(0.01, min(0.5, 0.15 * 0.5)) = 0.075. But confidence=0.3 also gets leak=0.045. The lower clamp means high-confidence edges can't distinguish themselves. Fix: remove lower clamp or make it configurable.

title: Monte Carlo: visited set undercounts failure probability for diamond/multi-path graphs
description: In monte_carlo_impact(), a visited set prevents nodes from being counted as failed more than once per simulation. For diamond graphs (A→B→D, A→C→D), this undercounts: P(D|A) should be ~0.99 but the visited set gives ~0.9. Fix: replace visited set with activated state tracking (independent per-edge Bernoulli sampling) per ADR-008.

title: VoI: compute_voi builds adjacency incorrectly — parent/child reversed
description: In compute_voi(), the edge loop builds the parents dict as: parents[from_node].append(to_node). This means parents maps from_node → [to_nodes], which is actually a forward adjacency (parent → children), not backward (child → parents). Then the code builds children dict as: children[child].append(parent) which inverts it. But then the BFS for finding ancestors uses children.get(node, []) — which is correct for backward traversal. However, the _path_confidence() helper then builds its own forward adjacency from children again, creating a double-inversion that may produce incorrect paths. The naming is confusing and the double-inversion is fragile. The adjacency should be built once with clear names: forward_adj (parent→children) and reverse_adj (child→parents).
design:
Refactor compute_voi() adjacency building for clarity:

Current (confusing):
  parents = {}  # Actually forward: from_node → [to_nodes]
  for row in edges:
      parents[from_node].append(to_node)  # This is forward, not backward!
  children = {}  # Actually reverse: child → [parents]
  for parent, child_list in parents.items():
      for child in child_list:
          children[child].append(parent)  # Inverting the misnamed dict

Fixed (clear):
  forward_adj = {}   # parent → [children]
  reverse_adj = {}   # child → [parents]
  for row in edges:
      from_node, to_node = row[0], row[1]
      forward_adj.setdefault(from_node, []).append(to_node)
      reverse_adj.setdefault(to_node, []).append(from_node)

Then:
- BFS for ancestors uses reverse_adj (walk backward from decision)
- _path_confidence uses forward_adj directly (no need to rebuild)
- Remove the double-inversion in _path_confidence()

Also fix _path_confidence() to accept forward_adj directly instead of rebuilding it from children dict.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Variable names match semantics: forward_adj = parent→children, reverse_adj = child→parents
2. No double-inversion: _path_confidence uses forward_adj directly
3. BFS ancestor traversal uses reverse_adj
4. VoI rankings are identical before/after refactor (behavior preserved)
5. Test: test_voi_adjacency_consistency() verifies forward/reverse are consistent
6. Test: test_voi_rankings_unchanged() verifies refactor doesn't change output
close_reason: FIXED: Adjacency now uses forward_adj/reverse_adj with clear naming (bayesian.py:1698-1716). No double-inversion. TestVoIAdjacencyDirection validates.

title: VoI: PERT p05/p50/p95 columns exist in schema but are unused by Bayesian engine
description: Schema v0.15.0 added probability_p05/p50/p95 and confidence_p05/p50/p95 columns to ohm_edges. The SDK and server accept these fields. However, build_bayesian_network() does NOT use them. The PERT-derived mean (p05 + 4*p50 + p95)/6 is never computed for CPT construction. The PERT-derived variance ((p95-p05)/6)^2 is never used for VoI uncertainty. Instead, VoI uses 1-confidence as uncertainty, which is a point estimate with no variance information. This means the entire PERT elicitation protocol from ADR-013 is wired up at the API level but produces no effect on actual Bayesian inference or VoI rankings.
design:
Wire PERT columns into build_bayesian_network():

1. In the SQL query that fetches edges, also SELECT probability_p05, probability_p50, probability_p95, confidence_p05, confidence_p50, confidence_p95.

2. For each edge, compute effective probability:
   If probability_p50 is set:
       p05 = COALESCE(probability_p05, probability_p50 * 0.8)
       p95 = COALESCE(probability_p95, min(1.0, probability_p50 * 1.2))
       effective_prob = (p05 + 4 * probability_p50 + p95) / 6.0  # PERT mean
   Else if probability is set:
       effective_prob = probability  # Point estimate (backward compat)
   Else:
       effective_prob = default_probability  # Fallback

3. For each edge, compute effective confidence:
   If confidence_p50 is set:
       c05 = COALESCE(confidence_p05, confidence_p50 * 0.8)
       c95 = COALESCE(confidence_p95, min(1.0, confidence_p50 * 1.2))
       effective_conf = (c05 + 4 * confidence_p50 + c95) / 6.0
   Else:
       effective_conf = confidence or default_probability

4. Use effective_prob * effective_conf as the edge weight in CPT construction (per ADR-008 fix from OHM-3xn).

5. Store PERT variance in edge dict for VoI: variance = ((p95 - p05) / 6) ** 2

Example: Edge with p05=0.1, p50=0.3, p95=0.7
- PERT mean = (0.1 + 4*0.3 + 0.7)/6 = 0.333
- PERT variance = ((0.7-0.1)/6)^2 = 0.01
- CPT uses 0.333 * confidence as edge weight
- VoI uses variance=0.01 as uncertainty signal
acceptance_criteria:
1. Edge with p05=0.1, p50=0.3, p95=0.7 produces CPT weight = 0.333 * confidence
2. Edge with only probability set (no PERT) uses probability directly (backward compat)
3. Edge with only confidence set (no probability, no PERT) uses confidence * default_probability
4. PERT variance is computed and stored in edge dict for VoI consumption
5. PERT mean is validated: p05 <= p50 <= p95, all in [0,1]
6. Test: test_pert_cpt_construction() with known PERT values
7. Test: test_pert_backward_compat() with old-style edges
8. Test: test_pert_validation() rejects p05 > p50
close_reason: FIXED: build_bayesian_network() now fetches and uses PERT p05/p50/p95 columns (bayesian.py:219-280). PERT mean computed via (O+4M+P)/6. TestPERTInBuildBayesianNetwork validates.

title: VoI: sensitivity uses path confidence instead of ATE as specified in ADR-013
description: ADR-013 explicitly states: 'sensitivity = ATE(ancestor → decision) or path-weighted confidence product'. The compute_voi() implementation uses _path_confidence() which does BFS and returns the minimum edge confidence along a single path. This ignores: (1) multi-path effects where multiple causal routes amplify impact, (2) confounders that bias the naive path estimate, (3) the actual do-operator semantics that ATE provides. The fix: compute actual ATE for high-VoI candidates using the existing compute_ate() function, falling back to path confidence only when the network is too large for ATE computation.
design:
Replace _path_confidence() with actual ATE computation in compute_voi():

1. For each candidate node, instead of:
     path_conf = _path_confidence(node_id, decision, children, edge_confidences)
     sensitivity += path_conf * utility
   Use:
     ate_result = compute_ate(conn, node_id, decision, layers=layers)
     sensitivity += abs(ate_result['ate']) * utility

2. Optimization: ATE computation is expensive (builds Bayesian network, runs do-operator × 2). Cache ATE results per (cause, effect) pair. For large networks (>20 nodes), fall back to path confidence as a fast approximation.

3. The path confidence fallback should use the PRODUCT of edge confidences along the path (not the minimum). The minimum is too conservative — it says 'the weakest link determines everything', but in reality each edge contributes independently. Product better approximates the joint probability.

Example: A → B → C → Decision
- Old: min(0.9, 0.8, 0.7) = 0.7 sensitivity
- New (ATE): actual do-operator result, e.g., 0.65
- Fallback (product): 0.9 × 0.8 × 0.7 = 0.504 (closer to ATE than min)

4. Multi-path handling: if multiple paths exist from node to decision, sum the ATE contributions (they represent independent causal mechanisms).
acceptance_criteria:
1. VoI sensitivity uses ATE when network ≤ 20 nodes
2. VoI sensitivity falls back to path-confidence product for large networks
3. ATE results are cached per (cause, effect) pair within one compute_voi() call
4. Multi-path nodes get higher sensitivity (sum of path contributions)
5. VoI rankings change from current (min-edge) to ATE-based ordering
6. Test: 3-node chain A→B→C, verify sensitivity for A uses ATE(A→C)
7. Test: large network (>20 nodes) uses fallback without error
8. Test: ATE cache prevents redundant Bayesian network builds
close_reason: FIXED: compute_voi() now uses compute_ate() as primary sensitivity method with path_confidence fallback (bayesian.py:1831-1854). TestVoISensitivityATE validates.

title: Bayesian: probability and confidence conflated in COALESCE
description: ADR-008 states that probability and confidence are separate attributes: confidence = belief in the edge's existence, probability = likelihood of the effect given the edge exists. However, build_bayesian_network() uses COALESCE(probability, confidence, default_probability) which treats them as interchangeable. If an edge has confidence=0.9 (high belief) but probability=0.1 (low likelihood), the COALESCE picks 0.1 (probability). But if an edge has only confidence=0.9 and no probability, it uses 0.9 as the probability — which is semantically wrong. Confidence should modulate the edge's weight in the CPT (e.g., effective_prob = probability * confidence) rather than substituting for probability.
notes: Investigated OHM-3xn. Confirmed HEAD already uses correct formula: COALESCE(probability, confidence * default_probability, default_probability) — conflating was already fixed in commits 57d1999/69edab2. Added ADR-008/ADR-009 doc blocks to bayesian.py docstring. All 21 Bayesian tests pass. Closing as completed by prior work.
close_reason: Closed

title: Bayesian: NEGATES edges ignored in network construction
description: ADR-009 defines NEGATES as an edge type for negative evidence (semantically distinct from CHALLENGED_BY). However, build_bayesian_network() only includes CAUSES, DEPENDS_ON, THREATENS, and EXPECTED_LIKELIHOOD edges. NEGATES edges are completely ignored, meaning negative causal evidence is lost in Bayesian inference. A NEGATES edge from A to B should reduce P(B=bad) when A is in the bad state (or increase it when A is good), which is the inverse of a CAUSES edge. The noisy-OR gate should be extended to handle NEGATES edges with inverted probability semantics.
close_reason: Closed

title: Bayesian: leak probability not modulated by edge confidence
description: The build_bayesian_network function has a comment stating 'Use confidence as leak probability if available, otherwise default. Higher confidence → lower leak (more of the probability is explained by parents)' but then unconditionally sets leak = DEFAULT_LEAK. The per-edge confidence values are never used to modulate the leak probability. This means all nodes use the same leak=0.15 regardless of how confident we are in their parent edges.
close_reason: Closed

title: store.py bypasses boundary enforcement on challenges (security bug)
description:
store.py's challenge_edge() does NOT call enforce_challenge_boundary(), while queries/__init__.py's create_challenge() correctly calls it. This means the daemon path (ohmd) allows challenging L1/L2 edges, violating ADR-003. The direct-connection path correctly blocks this.

Similarly, store.py's challenge_edge() skips enforce_support_boundary().

Fix: Add enforce_challenge_boundary() and enforce_support_boundary() calls to store.py's challenge_edge() method, mirroring the queries/ path.

Found by: Atlas OHM Review subagent
close_reason: Already fixed in commit 010b68b (OHM-evm). store.py challenge_edge() now calls enforce_challenge_boundary().

title: Critical: Server routes use ohmd as agent_name — boundary enforcement broken in production
description:
## Bug: All server writes attributed to 'ohmd' instead of authenticated agent

### Problem
The OHM daemon creates a single OhmStore with agent_name='ohmd' at startup. All HTTP handlers that call self.store.write_node(), self.store.write_edge(), self.store.write_observation(), self.store.update_edge_confidence(), and self.store.update_agent_state() use this single store — meaning ALL edges are created_by='ohmd' regardless of which agent authenticated.

### Impact
- Boundary enforcement is completely disabled in production. Since every edge belongs to 'ohmd', any agent can 'update' any edge.
- Challenge/support semantics are broken. challenge_edge creates new edges also attributed to 'ohmd'.
- Provenance is lost. You can't tell which agent created what.
- Agent state is broken. update_agent_state() writes to agent_name='ohmd' instead of the authenticated agent.

### Affected Endpoints
- POST /node, /edge, /challenge/{id}, /support/{id}, /observe/{id}, /state — all use self.store with agent_name='ohmd'

### Not Affected
- POST /register — correctly passes 'agent' to create_node/create_edge directly

### Fix (recommended: Option A)
Add agent_name parameter to OhmStore write methods:
  - write_node(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - write_edge(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - write_observation(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - update_edge_confidence(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - challenge_edge(agent_name=agent, ...)
  - update_agent_state(agent_name=agent, ...)
Then update server.py to pass the authenticated agent to each write method.

Option B (per-request wrapper) risks DuckDB connection issues.
Option C (set self.store.agent_name per request) is NOT thread-safe.

### Reproduction
```python
from ohm.store import OhmStore
s = OhmStore(':memory:', 'ohmd')
s.write_node(id='n1', label='test', type='note')
print(s.get_node('n1')['created_by'])  # 'ohmd' — should be the authenticated agent
```

title: BUG: store.py challenge_edge bypasses boundary enforcement
description:
CRITICAL FINDING FROM REVIEW: store.py's challenge_edge() method does NOT call enforce_challenge_boundary(). This means the daemon path (ohmd) allows challenges on L1/L2 edges, violating ADR-003.

queries/__init__.py create_challenge() correctly calls enforce_challenge_boundary() before creating the challenge edge. store.py challenge_edge() skips this check entirely.

This is a security AND behavior bug:
- L1 (Structure) edges should never be challenged — they're shared, authoritative facts
- L2 (Flow) edges should never be challenged — they're shared with attribution
- Only L3/L4 edges can be challenged — and only by agents other than the owner

When an agent connects via ohmd (HTTP), they bypass boundary enforcement. When an agent connects via CLI/SDK (direct DuckDB), enforcement works correctly.

Fix: store.py challenge_edge() must call enforce_challenge_boundary() before creating the challenge edge, same as queries/__init__.py create_challenge().

Acceptance:
- store.py challenge_edge() calls enforce_challenge_boundary()
- Challenge on L1/L2 edge via ohmd returns 403 Forbidden
- Challenge on L3/L4 edge via ohmd works correctly
- Test: POST /challenge/<L1-edge-id> returns 403
- Test: POST /challenge/<L3-edge-id> from different agent returns 201
close_reason: Fixed in 010b68b (commit 010b68). store.py challenge_edge() now calls enforce_challenge_boundary() before creating challenge edges. L1/L2 challenges correctly rejected.

title: Auth fail-open: ohmd allows writes when tokens{} is empty (dev mode bypass)
close_reason: Already implemented: POST requests require auth by default (raise AuthenticationError when no token). --no-auth flag and OHM_NO_AUTH env var already exist. Fail-open only when --no-auth is explicitly set.

title: Path traversal risk in /node/{id} and /edge/{id} endpoints
description: server.py extracts node_id and edge_id directly from URL paths (e.g., path[6:] for /node/{id}) without validation or sanitization. A request like /node/../../etc/passwd or /node/%00 could potentially cause issues. DuckDB parameterized queries protect against SQL injection, but the unsanitized IDs are passed to get_node()/get_edge() lookups. Need to: (1) validate that IDs match the expected format (alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores), (2) reject paths with directory traversal characters, (3) add URL decoding. The validation module already has validate_identifier() — use it in the server handlers.
close_reason: Closed

title: Auth: add --no-auth flag, fail closed by default for production
description:
From security audit (OHM-5of). Currently when self.tokens is empty (default), all requests get agent='ohm' with full access. This is fail-open.

POST routes now use _require_auth() which raises AuthenticationError — good.
GET routes still default to agent='ohm' when no tokens configured — acceptable for dev but not production.

Fix: Add --no-auth CLI flag and OHM_NO_AUTH env var. When set, allow unauthenticated access. When NOT set (default), deny requests without valid tokens.

Behavior:
- --no-auth or OHM_NO_AUTH=1: all requests allowed, agent defaults to 'ohm' (dev mode)
- Default (no flag): POST requires valid token, GET requires valid token OR no tokens configured (transition mode)
- Production: tokens configured, all requests require auth

Acceptance:
- ohmd --no-auth starts in dev mode (no auth required)
- Without --no-auth and with tokens configured, all requests require valid Bearer token
- Without --no-auth and without tokens, GET requests allowed, POST requests denied
- Tests verify fail-closed behavior
close_reason: Implemented in 6e1ba86. --no-auth flag added, fail-closed by default. OHM_NO_AUTH env var also supported. GET routes require auth when tokens configured.

title: Parameterized queries — replace f-string interpolation in SQL
description:
From security audit (OHM-5of). validation.py sanitizes identifiers before f-string interpolation, but this is defense-in-depth, not a replacement for parameterized queries.

Five locations use f-strings with validated-but-interpolated values:
1. layer in WHERE clauses (queries/__init__.py lines 53, 119)
2. since (timestamp) in change feed (queries/__init__.py line 312)
3. agent_name in agent state (queries/__init__.py lines 315, 354)
4. Dynamic WHERE in CLI filter (cli/__init__.py line 417)
5. Dynamic UPDATE in SDK (sdk.py line 144)

Principle: Use parameterized queries (? placeholders) wherever possible. Keep validation as defense-in-depth. f-string interpolation should only be for SQL keywords/structure that CANNOT be parameterized (like column names in dynamic ORDER BY).

Acceptance:
- All user-provided values use parameterized queries (?)
- f-string interpolation only for SQL structure that DuckDB can't parameterize
- validation.py remains as defense-in-depth (not removed)
- 134+ tests still pass
- No SQL injection possible even if validation is bypassed
close_reason: Implemented in 6e1ba86. All queries use ? parameterized placeholders. Only remaining f-strings are validation error messages, not SQL.

title: Security: SQL injection gaps, auth bypass, no TLS, no rate limiting
description:
Security audit findings. Multiple issues need fixing before ohmd is production-ready.

CRITICAL: SQL Injection Gaps

validation.py exists and is used for identifiers (node_id, edge_id, from_node, to_node) but NOT applied everywhere:

1. layer in f-strings (lines 53, 119): validated by validate_layer before interpolation, but should use parameterized queries
2. since (timestamp) in f-strings (line 312): validated by validate_timestamp, should be parameterized
3. agent_name in f-strings (lines 315, 354): validated by validate_identifier, should be parameterized
4. CLI dynamic WHERE clause (line 417): conditions are parameterized (=?), but WHERE structure is string concat
5. SDK dynamic UPDATE (line 144): column names from hardcoded strings, safe but fragile

Principle: Even when values are validated before interpolation, use parameterized queries wherever possible. Validation is defense-in-depth, not a replacement for parameterization.

HIGH: Auth Bypass in Default Mode

Lines 199, 307-308: When self.tokens is empty (default), auth is completely bypassed - any request gets agent='ohm'. Fresh install = no auth at all. If tokens dict is misconfigured, auth silently fails open. Fix: Auth should fail CLOSED. If no tokens configured, require explicit --no-auth flag or default to denying writes.

MEDIUM: No TLS/HTTPS

server.py uses plain HTTP (BaseHTTPRequestHandler). Tokens sent in plaintext. In production, run behind reverse proxy or add TLS support.

MEDIUM: No Rate Limiting

No rate limiting on any endpoint. A single client can flood the daemon.

LOW: Tokens in Plaintext Config

Tokens stored in ~/.ohm/ohmd.json as plaintext. Should be hashed (bcrypt/argon2) or stored more securely.

LOW: No CORS Headers

No CORS configuration. Needed if ohmd accessed from browser (marimo).

LOW: No Request Size Limit

_read_body reads Content-Length without a cap. Malicious client could send multi-GB body.
notes: PARTIAL FIX (2567515): POST endpoints now use _require_auth() which raises AuthenticationError if no token. _check_write_access() enforces read-only vs read-write roles. GET routes still default to agent='ohm' when no tokens configured — this is intentional for dev mode but needs explicit --no-auth flag for production.
close_reason: SQL injection (y2i.6), auth bypass (y2i.7), boundary bypass (evm) all FIXED. Remaining: TLS, rate limiting, request size, encrypted tokens. Creating separate issues for remaining work.

title: Add server tests — ohmd HTTP endpoint coverage
description:
Zero tests exist for server.py (ohmd). The daemon has 17 HTTP endpoints (GET: /status, /schema, /layers, /node/, /edge/, /neighborhood/, /path/, /impact/, /confidence/, /agent/, /agents, /listen; POST: /node, /edge, /challenge/, /support/, /observe/, /state) but none are tested.

This is critical for P0 — the production daemon needs test coverage before we add auth, error handling, and Quack.

Acceptance:

- Each GET endpoint returns correct status codes and JSON
- Each POST endpoint creates data and returns correct response
- 404 for missing nodes/edges
- Auth token validation (once OHM-y2i.2 is done)
- Use http.server test patterns (HTTPServer with test handler)
- All existing 144 tests still pass

title: Token auth: Bearer tokens, per-agent roles, /auth endpoint
description:
Implement Bearer token auth in ohmd. Each agent gets a token via 'ohm serve token <agent_name>'. Tokens are stored in ~/.ohm/ohmd.json. Every write endpoint requires a valid token. Read endpoints are open (no auth required). Token maps to agent identity for attribution. Support two roles: 'read-write' (full access) and 'read-only' (no writes). The ohm SDK should support passing tokens via environment variable (OHM_TOKEN) or constructor argument.

Acceptance:
- POST /node, POST /edge, POST /challenge, POST /support, POST /observe, POST /state require valid Bearer token
- GET endpoints are open (no auth required)
- Invalid token returns 401 with correlation_id
- Token maps to agent_name for edge attribution
- 'ohm serve token metis' generates and stores a token
- SDK supports token via OHM_TOKEN env var or connect(token=...) param
- 144 existing tests still pass
notes:
FOUND DURING REVIEW (2026-05-16):
- server.py already has _authenticate() stub and DEFAULT_CONFIG with tokens dict
- load_config reads ~/.ohm/ohmd.json or OHM_CONFIG env var
- Auth not enforced anywhere — _authenticate returns None
- Tokens dict in config is empty — needs token generation and storage
- Two roles needed: read-write (full access) and read-only (GET only)
- Write endpoints: POST /node, /edge, /challenge/, /support/, /observe/, /state
- Read endpoints: all GET routes (open, no auth needed)
- SDK needs token support: OHM_TOKEN env var or connect(token=...) param
- ohm serve token <agent_name> command needs implementation
close_reason: Implemented in 2567515. Bearer token auth with _require_auth() and _check_write_access(). Two roles: read-write and read-only. POST endpoints fail closed.

title: Health check and status endpoints
description: Add /health endpoint (returns 200 if daemon is alive). Enhance /status to include: database path, node/edge counts, active agents, uptime. Add /ready endpoint that checks DuckDB is accessible.
notes: DESIGN: Three endpoints: GET /health returns {'status': 'ok', 'uptime': <seconds>} with 200. GET /ready returns {'status': 'ready', 'database': <path>} after verifying DuckDB connection is alive. GET /status returns full status: node_count, edge_count, observation_count, active_agents, db_path, uptime, version. Implementation: thin wrappers calling OhmStore.status(). PM QUESTION: Should /health also check DuckDB connectivity (heavier but more accurate)? Should /status include per-agent breakdown?

title: Quack protocol integration for concurrent access
description:
Integrate Quack (DuckDB HTTP protocol) into ohmd for concurrent multi-agent access. Currently ohmd uses a simple HTTP server with in-process DuckDB — this is single-writer and blocks on concurrent access. Quack provides proper concurrent read/write with transaction isolation via the DuckDB HTTP protocol.

Approach: Replace the manual HTTP handlers in server.py with Quack for all graph query endpoints. Keep custom endpoints (auth, state, challenge semantics) as thin wrappers that call into Quack. The key insight is that Quack handles concurrent access; ohmd handles OHM-specific logic (boundary enforcement, challenge semantics, agent attribution).

Acceptance:
- ohmd starts with Quack backend for DuckDB
- Multiple agents can read/write concurrently without corruption
- Write operations still enforce boundary rules (challenge semantics, ownership)
- All existing CLI commands work through the Quack-backed daemon
- Benchmark: 10 concurrent agents writing, no data loss or deadlock
- 144 existing tests still pass
notes:
FOUND DURING REVIEW (2026-05-16):
- server.py currently uses http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler — single-threaded, synchronous
- All graph operations go through OhmStore which wraps DuckDB directly — single writer
- Quack provides HTTP protocol for DuckDB, handles concurrent read/write
- Key design: Quack replaces the raw DuckDB connection in server.py, not OhmStore itself
- OhmStore should stay as a convenience wrapper but connect through Quack
- SDK and CLI currently bypass server.py entirely — they connect to DuckDB files directly
- After Quack integration, SDK/CLI should still support direct file access (offline mode)
- Need to decide: does Quack run as a separate process or embedded in ohmd?

title: Error handling with exit codes and correlation IDs
description:
Implement the full exception hierarchy in server.py HTTP handlers. Each endpoint catches OHMError subclasses and returns appropriate HTTP status codes: 404 for NodeNotFoundError/EdgeNotFoundError, 403 for PermissionDeniedError, 401 for AuthenticationError, 400 for ValidationError, 500 for unexpected errors. Every error response includes a correlation_id (UUID4) for tracing. Add request logging with method, path, status, duration. Use the existing ohm.exceptions module — don't recreate it.

Acceptance:
- All HTTP errors return JSON with {error, message, correlation_id, status}
- 404 for missing nodes/edges
- 403 for boundary violations
- 401 for missing/invalid tokens
- 400 for bad input
- 500 for unexpected errors with safe messages
- Request log: method, path, status, duration, correlation_id
- 144 existing tests still pass
notes:
FOUND DURING REVIEW (2026-05-16):
- exceptions.py already has: OHMError, NodeNotFoundError, EdgeNotFoundError, PermissionDeniedError, AuthenticationError, ValidationError, DaemonNotRunningError, CorrelationError
- server.py currently has NO error handling — all errors return 500 as plain text
- server.py uses OhmStore (not queries/) — error handling needs to catch both store exceptions and query exceptions
- The _authenticate method exists but is a stub — returns None (no auth yet)
- server.py has _json_response helper already — extend it for error responses
- Stale TODO: cli/__init__.py references OHM-346.1 (closed issue) for daemon startup
close_reason: Implemented in 89e2848. Error mapping complete: 404/403/401/400/500 with correlation IDs and request logging.

title: P0: Production Daemon — ohmd with Quack, auth, and error handling
