Currently, ohmd checkpoints on shutdown (store.close() calls CHECKPOINT) and via POST /admin/checkpoint. With multi-tenancy, each tenant has its own DuckDB file and its own WAL. The LRU cache evicts idle tenants (closes the connection), which triggers a checkpoint. But active tenants never get checkpointed unless explicitly triggered, leading to unbounded WAL growth for hot tenants. Need a strategy: when to checkpoint, how often, and what triggers it.

title: Boundary enforcement under multi-tenancy — tenant identity and ADR-003 evolution
description: ADR-003 boundary rules are agent-scoped: L3/L4 edges have created_by = agent_name, only the owner can update/delete, any agent can challenge/support. Under multi-tenancy, the customer_api identity needs boundary semantics. Questions: (1) Can customer_api create L3/L4 edges? If so, created_by = 'customer_api' — but that loses the customer_id. (2) Can customer_api challenge agent edges? The boundary rule says any agent can challenge L3/L4 — but customer_api is not an agent. (3) Within a tenant, do agents from different tenants respect each other's boundaries? (Tenant isolation should prevent this, but the boundary code doesn't check tenant_id.) (4) The boundary enforcement in store.py and queries/ uses enforce_write_boundary(conn, agent_name, edge_id) — no tenant_id parameter.

title: Message queue backing store for multi-channel communication router
description: tss4.16 (Multi-Channel Router) specifies an internal message queue: {customer_id, channel, direction, content, timestamp, thread_id} but does not specify the backing implementation. Options: (a) In-memory queue — lost on restart, no durability, simplest; (b) DuckDB table in tenant instance — durable, queryable, but single-writer contention with graph writes; (c) Redis — fast, pub/sub, but new infrastructure dependency; (d) SQLite file per tenant — durable, simple, but another DB per tenant; (e) ohm_change_feed table — reuse existing append-only log as message backbone. The change feed already has timestamp, agent_name, operation; it could be extended with channel/direction/thread_id columns. This is the lightest option and avoids new infrastructure.

title: Per-tenant configuration store — meta.json integrations schema and loading
description: Current ohmd.json is a flat file with host/port/tokens/ducklake config. Multi-tenancy needs per-tenant configuration: Twilio account SID + auth token + phone number, SendGrid API key + from_email, Slack bot token + signing secret + channel ID, Teams webhook URL + app ID. The backlog (OHM-MT-2) specifies meta.json per tenant with an integrations: {} key but no schema for what goes inside. Without a defined schema, each channel implementation will invent its own credential format and location. Need: (1) defined schema for integrations dict in meta.json, (2) loading mechanism so TenantManager can pass integration config to channel adapters, (3) security: credentials must be hashed/masked in meta.json or loaded from env vars / secret references, (4) validation at provision time that required integrations for the domain are present.
design: meta.json integrations schema: {twilio: {account_sid, auth_token_ref, phone_number}, sendgrid: {api_key_ref, from_email}, slack: {bot_token_ref, signing_secret_ref, channel_id}, teams: {webhook_url, app_id, tenant_id}}. _ref suffix means the value is an env var name or vault path, not the credential itself. At provision time, domain template specifies required integrations (home_services requires twilio, manufacturing requires teams). TenantManager.load_integrations(customer_id) reads meta.json, resolves _ref values from env/vault, returns validated config dict. Channel adapters receive their section of the dict. Credential storage: meta.json stores references only; actual secrets in environment variables keyed by customer_id (TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN_{CUSTOMER_ID}) or a secrets manager.
acceptance_criteria: Integrations schema defined in meta.json with typed fields per channel; _ref fields resolve from env vars at runtime (never store plaintext secrets); TenantManager.load_integrations(customer_id) returns validated dict; Domain template specifies required integrations; Provisioning validates required integrations are available; Invalid/missing credentials fail gracefully with clear error; Unit tests for schema validation and credential resolution

title: Agent multi-tenant routing — OhmStore.for_agent() and SDK connect() tenant scope
description: Current agent model assumes 1 agent = 1 identity = 1 local DB (ADR-012). OhmStore.for_agent() creates ~/.ohm/agents/{agent_name}/ohm.duckdb — agent_name determines both the DB path and attribution identity. This 1:1 mapping breaks under multi-tenancy: (1) for_agent() has no tenant_id param, so all tenant data shares one local DB; (2) attribution (created_by) is fixed at connection time — a multi-tenant agent writing for tenant-A and tenant-B uses the same actor; (3) DuckLake sync timestamps are per-agent ({agent_name}_last_push/pull) — can't track tenant sync state separately; (4) ohm_change_feed has no tenant_id column — multi-tenant agent can't distinguish own changes from different tenants; (5) ohm_agent_state is one row per agent_name — can't track separate focus/patterns per tenant; (6) DuckLake mirror tables have no tenant column. The multi-tenancy plan only covers the HTTP path (tss4.3/4.4/4.8) — the agent local-mode path is unaddressed.
design: Two approaches: (A) Multi-store: agent opens one OhmStore per tenant via for_agent(agent_name, base_dir=f'/var/lib/ohm/tenants/{tenant_id}/agents/'). Agent manages a dict of stores keyed by tenant_id. No schema changes needed. Each tenant's data stays isolated in separate DuckDB files. Agent switches store per operation. (B) Tenant column: add tenant_id to all schema tables, filter all queries. This is the shared-DB approach — requires schema migration, changes every query. Approach A is simpler and matches the per-tenant-DuckDB architecture already chosen. Approach A limitation: agent cannot cross-query across tenants in a single SQL statement. Recommended: Approach A for now (matches OHM-tss4 architecture), document Approach B as future option in ADR-015. Key change: for_agent() gains optional tenant_id param that changes the DB path to {base_dir}/{agent_name}/{tenant_id}/ohm.duckdb. The SDK connect() gains optional tenant_id param that opens the tenant-scoped DB. The agent integration classes (MetisIntegration, etc.) need a tenant_id param or a factory method that returns per-tenant Graph instances.
acceptance_criteria: OhmStore.for_agent(agent_name, tenant_id=...) creates DB at {base_dir}/{agent_name}/{tenant_id}/ohm.duckdb or {base_dir}/{agent_name}/ohm.duckdb when tenant_id is None (backward compat); SDK connect(db_path, actor, tenant_id=...) opens tenant-scoped DB; Multiple tenants per agent = multiple OhmStore instances, no data mixing; Attribution: created_by remains agent_name, tenant_id stored in meta.json; DuckLake sync works per-tenant (each store has its own sync state); Agent integrations accept tenant_id; Existing single-tenant usage unchanged (all 603+ tests pass)

title: Security review gate before multi-tenancy ship
description: Multi-tenancy introduces new auth (customer keys), tenant isolation boundaries, PII/PHI handling, and filesystem path construction from request input. These are exactly the surfaces where isolation bugs become security incidents. An explicit security review must gate the release, not be an afterthought.
design: Run the repo's security-review process over the full multi-tenancy diff before merge to main. Threat model: cross-tenant data access, path traversal (OHM-c864), key leakage, global-state bleed (OHM-ym2f), auth bypass, info leakage via error codes, secure-delete verification. Produce a sign-off checklist.
acceptance_criteria: Threat model documented; Cross-tenant isolation penetration-tested (Customer A cannot reach Customer B by any vector); Path traversal verified blocked; Auth bypass attempts fail; Error codes do not leak tenant existence; Secure-delete verified (data unrecoverable); Sign-off recorded before ship

title: Feature-flag the multi-tenancy rollout (core OHM unaffected until proven)
description: tss4.4 refactors ~50-80 call sites in server.py from self.store to self.current_store. That is a large blast radius for existing OHM and TOPO deployments. The rollout must be gated so multi-tenancy is OFF by default and core single-tenant behavior is byte-identical until the flag is enabled, allowing safe incremental rollout and instant rollback.
design: Config flag (env OHM_MULTI_TENANT=0/1 or config key). When off: current_store returns self.store unconditionally, customer-token auth and /tenant/* routes disabled, TenantManager not instantiated. When on: full routing. The refactor itself is safe because current_store falls through to self.store when _customer_id is None — the flag gates instantiation and route registration.
acceptance_criteria: Multi-tenancy OFF by default; With flag off, existing test suite passes unchanged and no /tenant routes registered; With flag off, current_store === self.store (no perf cost); Flag documented in upgrade guide (OHM-gg0n); Instant rollback by toggling flag + restart

title: Per-tenant quotas and noisy-neighbor protection
description: In a single-process multi-tenant model, one tenant can exhaust shared resources (CPU on a giant Bayesian inference, disk via unbounded node creation, request flood) and degrade or DoS every other tenant. There are no per-tenant limits in the plan. This is both a scalability and durability gap.
design: Per-tenant quotas in meta.json by tier: max_nodes, max_edges, max_db_size_bytes, max_requests_per_day, max_inference_timeout. Enforce at write time (reject over-quota with 429/402) and via the existing inference timeout. Surface quota usage in /tenant/{id}/health (OHM-r0et). Tie tiers to domain templates (tss4.1).
acceptance_criteria: Quotas defined per tier; Write over max_nodes/max_db_size rejected with clear error; Requests-per-day rate limit per customer key; Inference timeout enforced per tenant; Quota usage visible in tenant health; One tenant cannot starve others under load (verified in OHM-s2ao load test)

title: LRU eviction guard against in-flight requests (reference counting)
description: tss4.2's LRU + idle eviction can close a tenant's DuckDB connection while a long-running request (e.g. a multi-second Bayesian inference) is still using it, causing mid-request failures or use-after-close. The eviction policy must not reclaim a store that has active requests.
design: Reference-count active requests per tenant store. Eviction skips stores with refcount>0 (or marks for deferred eviction once refcount hits 0). current_store acquire/release around request lifecycle in do_GET/do_POST entry points. Idle timer resets on each acquire.
acceptance_criteria: Store with an in-flight request is never evicted; Eviction of idle store proceeds normally; Concurrent long request + eviction pressure does not crash or corrupt (stress test); Refcount released even on handler exception (try/finally); No deadlock between eviction lock and request path

title: Per-tenant backup, restore, and disaster recovery
description: A SaaS platform with paying customers must be able to restore a single tenant to a prior point in time without affecting others. tss4.6 has export but no restore, no scheduled backups, no DR. Losing or corrupting one tenant's DuckDB with no recovery path is unacceptable for production.
notes: Implementation guide: backup_tenant() is ~30 lines — checkpoint, then shutil.copy2(tenant_dir/ohm.duckdb, backup_path). restore_tenant() evicts LRU entry, copies file back, returns. Wire into server.py _post_tenant_backup() and _post_tenant_restore() using the same _require_admin() + _require_multi_tenant_active() pattern as existing provisioning endpoints. Add backup_dir config key to TenantManager.__init__(). Retention pruning: sort glob(backup_dir/customer_id/*.duckdb) by mtime, delete oldest beyond N. Tests: backup creates file, restore recovers data written before backup, retention pruning removes old files.
close_reason: backup_tenant(), restore_tenant(), list_backups() implemented in tenant.py with retention pruning, reason tracking, and pre-deprovision backup. HTTP endpoints wired into server.py. Tests in test_tenant_api.py pass.

title: Crash-consistent lazy migration + meta.json reconciliation on startup
description: tss4.5 marks a tenant needs_attention on migration failure — but if ohmd CRASHES mid-migration, that mark is never written and the tenant is left half-migrated with no record. Separately, meta.json (schema_version, tier, flags) is a sidecar file that can drift from the actual DB schema on partial write or manual edit, with no reconciliation.
design: Make each migration step transactional (DuckDB transaction or idempotent + checkpoint). On startup, scan tenants and reconcile: compare meta.json schema_version against actual schema introspection; if mismatch, flag needs_attention. Consider moving meta into a DuckDB ohm_tenant_meta table so version is transactionally consistent with schema, with meta.json as a passive export.
acceptance_criteria: Migration interrupted by kill -9 leaves tenant in a recoverable, detectable state; Startup scan detects and flags drifted tenants; meta schema_version always reflects actual DB schema (or mismatch is flagged); Test: crash mid-migration then restart -> tenant flagged, not silently corrupt; Per-tenant migration lock prevents concurrent migration

title: Docs: Upgrade guide — migrating existing single-tenant ohmd to multi-tenant
description: Every existing OHM deployment (and all TOPO users) needs a tested, documented path to upgrade without data loss or agent workflow breakage. The auth model change (_authenticate returning a tuple) and the new current_store routing are the most likely sources of regressions. Without an explicit upgrade guide, operators will improvise and break things.
design: New file: docs/upgrade_multi_tenancy.md. Sections: Compatibility guarantees (existing agent tokens unchanged, core OHM remains on port/path), Step-by-step migration (stop daemon, apply config, restart, verify), How existing ohm.duckdb becomes the core instance, How TOPO deployments migrate (topod → multi-tenant ohmd with TOPO schema), Verification checklist (existing agents can still write, /health passes, /stats returns correct counts), Rollback procedure.
acceptance_criteria: docs/upgrade_multi_tenancy.md covers all sections; Tested on a real single-tenant ohmd instance; Agent token backward compatibility explicitly verified; TOPO migration path included; Rollback procedure is a single command or clear sequence; No data loss on upgrade

title: ADR-015: Multi-Tenancy Architecture — single-process isolated DuckDB instances
description: A structural change of this magnitude needs a documented ADR before implementation begins. Engineers and future agents need to understand why single-process (vs multi-process), why per-tenant DuckDB file isolation (vs single DB with tenant_id columns), why the LRU caching approach was chosen, and how the Bayesian cache isolation problem is resolved. Without an ADR, every implementation decision becomes ad hoc and undiscoverable.
design: Follow existing ADR format in docs/adr/. Number as 0015 (ADR-014 is agent task routing). Sections: Context (why multi-tenancy, TeamWork AI platform), Decision (single process + per-tenant DuckDB), Alternatives Considered (multi-process, tenant_id rows, schema-per-tenant in same DuckDB), Consequences (LRU cache, lazy migration, Bayesian cache isolation, auth model), Security Considerations (isolation guarantees, secure deletion, key hashing)
acceptance_criteria: docs/adr/0015-multi-tenancy.md created and merged; Covers all 5 sections above; Explicitly documents the Bayesian cache isolation solution; Documents the auth model choice (separate customer_tokens dict vs unified); Documents the LRU eviction policy rationale; References OHM_MULTI_TENANCY_BACKLOG.md; Accepted status set

title: Docs: Multi-tenant deployment and operations guide
description: Platform operators deploying ohmd in multi-tenant mode need documentation that covers initial setup, configuration, provisioning the first tenant, monitoring per-tenant health, and handling operational scenarios like migration failures and LRU tuning. Without this, multi-tenancy is unusable in production regardless of code quality.
design: New file: docs/multi_tenancy_ops.md. Sections: Prerequisites, Initial Setup (directory layout, config file changes), Starting ohmd in multi-tenant mode, Provisioning first customer (API walkthrough), Configuration Reference (all new env vars: OHM_TENANTS_DIR, OHM_MAX_CACHED, OHM_IDLE_EVICTION_MINUTES), Monitoring (per-tenant /metrics labels, /tenant/{id}/health), Runbooks (migration failure recovery, LRU cache tuning, pattern extraction schedule, secure deprovision).
acceptance_criteria: docs/multi_tenancy_ops.md covers all sections; Every new env var and config key documented; Provisioning walkthrough uses real API calls; Runbook: migration failure → mark needs_attention → manual recovery; Runbook: LRU tuning recommendation formula; At least one operator has reviewed before ship

title: Unit tests — Customer API key: creation, validation, rotation, rate limiting
description: tss4.3 adds customer API key auth but tss4.9 integration tests don't cover unit-level auth behavior. The auth layer needs isolated tests for key hashing, key-to-tenant resolution, 401 on invalid keys, and proof that agent tokens are untouched by the new code.
design: Extend tests/test_server.py or create tests/test_tenant_auth.py. Test _authenticate() with both agent tokens and customer keys. Use a test OhmHandler with injected token dicts.
acceptance_criteria: Test key format matches twai_live_{24chars} pattern; Test only hash stored — plaintext not recoverable from server state; Test valid customer key resolves to correct customer_id; Test invalid key returns 401; Test agent token auth unchanged — existing test suite still passes; Test rate limiting applies per customer key; Test POST /admin/customer-token creates key (admin-only); Test customer key cannot call admin endpoints (403); Test key rotation: new key works, old key invalidated

title: Unit tests — TenantManager: provisioning, LRU eviction, isolation, thread safety
description: tss4.9 covers end-to-end integration tests but TenantManager needs dedicated unit tests that exercise internal behavior without a live HTTP server. LRU eviction policy, concurrent access safety, idle eviction, secure deletion, and meta.json lifecycle are all internal concerns that integration tests miss or make slow to iterate on.
design: Create tests/test_tenant_manager.py using a temp directory fixture. Mock DuckDB connections for speed. Test each TenantManager method in isolation.
acceptance_criteria: Test provision() creates DuckDB + meta.json with correct fields; Test get_store() returns cached store on second call; Test LRU evicts LRU entry when max_cached reached; Test idle eviction closes conn after threshold; Test deprovision() removes from cache, deletes files, overwrites with random bytes on secure_delete=True; Test list_tenants() returns all provisioned tenants; Test thread-safe concurrent provision() calls don't create duplicate connections; Test provision with unknown domain raises ValueError; All tests run without network, live server, or ports

title: Home Services Domain Template — HVAC/plumbing/electrical shops
description: Create the home_services SchemaConfig domain template. First vertical for TeamWork AI. Node types: customer, technician, job, appointment, equipment, service_contract, warranty, estimate, invoice. Observations: call_duration, job_completion_time, first_time_fix_rate, revenue_per_job. Provenances: dispatch_analyst, schedule_coordinator, parts_broker, compliance_planner, operations_manager.
design: L1: Shop → technicians → customers → equipment → service areas. L2: Call → estimate → dispatch → en_route → on_site → complete → invoice → follow_up. L3: Scheduling patterns, customer LTV, technician performance, seasonal demand. L4: Revenue optimization, churn risk, capacity planning, weather impact.
acceptance_criteria: JSON template at ohm/graph/templates/home_services.json; All node/edge/observation/provenance types defined; L1-L4 layer descriptions for home services context; SchemaConfig validates via from_json_file(); Unit test for all node types

title: Integration Tests — Multi-Tenancy isolation, migration, security
description: Comprehensive integration tests verifying tenant isolation, lazy migration, provisioning lifecycle, and cross-tenant security.
acceptance_criteria: Test: provision → write → read → verify isolation; Test: two tenants same domain no cross-contamination; Test: deprovision removes data; Test: lazy migration on old schema version; Test: customer key cannot access core OHM; Test: agent token cannot access tenant data; Test: pattern extraction + anonymization; Test: concurrent access same tenant; Test: LRU eviction + re-access

title: Lazy Schema Migration for Tenant Instances
description: When a tenant instance is accessed, check its schema version against current OHM version. If behind, apply pending migrations automatically. Zero-downtime.
design: Check in TenantManager.get_store(). _apply_lazy_migrations() calls existing _apply_migrations(). Per-tenant threading lock. Optional CHECKPOINT before migration. Rollback: mark tenant as needs_attention in meta.json.
acceptance_criteria: get_store() checks meta.json schema_version before returning; If behind, applies pending MIGRATIONS; Updates meta.json after success; Failed migration: log error, return store read-only, alert operator; Migration events logged to change feed; Check adds <5ms; Per-tenant lock for concurrent migration

title: Provisioning API Endpoints — /tenant/provision, status, list, delete
description: REST API endpoints for tenant provisioning, status, listing, deprovisioning, and export. Admin-only access.
design: Admin auth: agent tokens with role:admin. Provisioning calls TenantManager.provision() + generates API key. Export: DuckDB COPY TO + JSON dump. Deprovision: ?confirm=true safety.
acceptance_criteria: POST /tenant/provision creates instance + returns API key; GET /tenant/{id} returns status; GET /tenant/{id}/schema returns domain schema; DELETE /tenant/{id} deprovisions; GET /tenants lists all; POST /tenant/{id}/export exports data; All admin-only auth; Customer keys get 403

title: Domain Template JSON Files — SchemaConfig.from_json_file()
description: Move domain templates from Python @classmethod to JSON files. Add SchemaConfig.from_json_file() classmethod. Create ohm/graph/templates/ directory with base schemas (ohm.json, topo.json, beef_herd.json) plus new home_services.json, manufacturing.json, construction.json, healthcare.json.
design: JSON files in ohm/graph/templates/ (shipped with package). Override path: /var/lib/ohm/templates/ (custom templates without code deploy). Search order: custom path → package templates → built-in classmethods. Each JSON: {name, node_types, layer_edge_types, layer_descriptions, observation_types, observation_sources, provenances}
acceptance_criteria: SchemaConfig.from_json_file('home_services.json') returns valid SchemaConfig; Existing topo() and beef_herd() classmethods still work; JSON templates validate correctly; to_dict()/from_dict() roundtrip preserves all fields; Unit tests for JSON loading, validation, roundtrip

title: /intervene missing preferred_edges causes cycle breaker to remove queried causal path
description: causal_intervention() is called from /intervene without preferred_edges. When a bidirectional CAUSES cycle exists, the cycle breaker removes the forward edge (hormuz→demand-rationing conf=0.85) because the backward edge has higher confidence (0.92). After graph surgery removes the backward edge (incoming to target), demand-rationing is disconnected. Fix: auto-derive preferred_edges from query_nodes in causal_intervention; expose ?preferred_edges= in server handler.
close_reason: Closed

title: Bayesian VE query fails: safe_query_nodes includes BFS nodes not in model after cycle-breaking
description: network['variables'] includes all BFS-scoped nodes but model.nodes() only has nodes that survived cycle removal. The joint infer.query(safe_query_nodes, ...) at line 933 of bayesian.py fails if any node is missing from the model. The outer try/except catches this and returns {method: error} with no posterior key. Callers see posterior: {} (missing key). Fix: filter safe_query_nodes against model.nodes() before querying; remove wasteful joint query for multi-node case.
close_reason: Closed

title: Cycle breaker removes causal edge when backward edge has higher confidence
description: _find_acyclic_subgraph removes the lowest-probability edge in cycles. When a bidirectional CAUSES cycle exists (e.g. A→B conf=0.85, B→A conf=0.92), it removes the lower-confidence forward edge A→B, breaking compute_ate for the valid A→effect query. Fix: accept preferred_edges hint in cycle breaker; when breaking cycles, avoid removing preferred causal edges.
close_reason: Closed

title: /health graph stats all null — wrong key names from query_graph_health
description: _get_infra_health in server.py reads node_count, edge_count, orphan_count, orphan_rate, low_confidence_count from query_graph_health() but actual keys are total_nodes, total_edges, orphan_nodes, low_confidence_unchallenged. No orphan_rate key exists. All .get() calls return None, so /health graph block is always null.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 3b24991

title: POST /heartbeat → 500: ohm_change_feed missing from production DB
description: agent_heartbeat() calls _log_change() which writes to ohm_change_feed. Production DBs initialized before the change_feed migration don't have this table, causing all heartbeat calls to crash with 500. Breaks the per-agent local-DB → DuckLake sync path (ADR-012). Fix: add CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ohm_change_feed migration with indexes on agent_name and occurred_at. GitHub #11.
close_reason: Fixed: (1) query_change_feed now wraps ohm_change_feed SELECT in try/except, falling through to ohm_change_log fallback when table is absent; (2) DDL_STATEMENTS split CREATE SEQUENCE and CREATE TABLE into separate entries so DuckDB executes both.

title: OHM Bug Batch: 2 P1 + 9 P2 + 3 P3 from systematic endpoint exercise
description:
Systematic exercise of 32 OHM endpoints revealed 14 open bugs.

## P1 — Inference produces wrong answers
- **OHM-7309**: `/adjustment` crashes with CPD error for most node pairs. Bayesian model includes irrelevant nodes (e.g., career_paleontologist in geopolitics queries). Model construction not scoped to relevant subgraph.
- **OHM-7320**: `/ate` returns ATE=0.0 and risk_ratio=1.0 for Hormuz→demand-rationing. Only uses 3 nodes in Bayesian model, missing the actual causal chain. Confidently wrong answer for a well-established causal relationship.

## P2 — Missing/broken endpoints
- **OHM-7308**: POST /tasks requires `id` field — should auto-generate if missing
- **OHM-7310**: `/source_reliability` returns 404 (documented in AGENTS.md but not implemented)
- **OHM-7311**: `/compound_confidence/{node}` returns 404 (documented but not implemented)
- **OHM-7312**: `/suggest` returns 0 results for `orphan_connect` and `cooccurrence` methods
- **OHM-7313**: `/suggest` shared_tags returns results with empty from_id/to_id fields
- **OHM-7314**: POST /edge field names inconsistent — requires `from`/`to`/`type` but API responses use `from_node`/`to_node`/`edge_type`
- **OHM-7317**: `/health/agents` returns agents with empty fields
- **OHM-7319**: PATCH /node returns 405 — no way to update node content without delete+recreate
- **OHM-7321**: `/listen` returns 0 events after daemon restart (change feed table not repopulated)

## P3 — Minor
- **OHM-7315**: POST /edge 404 for valid node with different ID format
- **OHM-7316**: Health score 0.73 doesn't weight 25% orphan rate
- **OHM-7318**: `/suggest_causes` mixes unrelated domains (college decisions in geopolitics context)

## Closed
- OHM-7300, 7301, 7302, 7304: Fixed in modular restructure
- OHM-7307: Not a bug (shell token mangling)
close_reason: Umbrella issue: all addressable sub-items from Metis's second 32-endpoint test run are now fixed. 10 bugs fixed in batch (OHM-7308..7321), 2 closed as by-design, 1 as data quality, 1 as won't-fix.

title: P1: Heartbeat crashes - ohm_change_feed table does not exist
description: POST /heartbeat with valid agent auth crashes with: Catalog Error: Table with name ohm_change_feed does not exist. The change_feed table is missing from DB schema init. This breaks the DuckLake sync bridge (local->DuckLake->HTTP round-trip). Same bug as OHM-y30o.
close_reason: Duplicate of OHM-y30o (same ohm_change_feed missing from schema init)

title: P1: /sync endpoint not registered in POST handler
description: POST /sync returns 'Unknown endpoint: /sync'. The /sync endpoint exists in the discovery index docs but is not wired into the POST handler. This is the exact class of bug that the route registry refactor (OHM-c5pl) fixes - endpoints placed in wrong handler.
close_reason: Closed

title: P1: /observe creates observation on nonexistent node
description: POST /observe/nonexistent_node_id creates an observation record even when the node doesn't exist. Should validate node_id exists in ohm_nodes before allowing observation. Creates orphan observations that can never be retrieved via /node or /neighborhood.
close_reason: Closed

title: P1: /deep/{id} returns node without edges while /neighborhood/{id} returns full graph
description: GET /deep/hormuz_and_gate returns the node with embedding but 0 edges. GET /neighborhood/hormuz_and_gate returns 105 items with edges. /deep should include connected edges like /neighborhood does, or be documented as a node-only endpoint. Currently agents using /deep miss the graph context.
close_reason: Closed

title: P1: Edge creation allows nonexistent endpoints - edges can reference from/to nodes that don't exist in ohm_nodes. Creates dangling references that break graph traversal. Should validate that both endpoints exist before creating edge.
close_reason: Closed

title: Define RouteRegistry core: Route dataclass, decorator, dispatcher
description: Foundation for OHM-c5pl. Define Route = dataclass(path, method, handler_fn, auth_level). RouteRegistry maps (method.upper(), path) to Route, provides dispatch(method, path, handler_self) that checks auth and calls the handler. @route('POST', '/deduplicate', auth='write') decorator registers at class definition time. Startup validation: assert no two routes share (method, path).
close_reason: Closed

title: Return 405 Method Not Allowed for wrong-method requests
description: Part of OHM-c5pl. Currently a POST to a GET-only endpoint returns 404 (Unknown endpoint) because the POST dispatcher doesn't know about GET routes. With the route registry, the dispatcher can check if a path is registered under any method and return 405 with an Allow header listing valid methods. This is the correct HTTP behavior and makes API errors more informative.
close_reason: Closed

title: Migrate _do_GET endpoints to route registry
description: Part of OHM-c5pl. Convert all elif branches in _do_GET to @route('GET', ...) decorated methods. Each handler becomes a standalone method on the handler class. Read-only endpoints get auth='none' or auth='read'. The handful of write endpoints currently in _do_GET (e.g. /decay, /deduplicate) should move to POST during this migration, not just get re-registered as GET.
close_reason: Closed

title: Migrate _do_POST endpoints to route registry
description: Part of OHM-c5pl. Convert all elif branches in _do_POST to @route('POST', ..., auth='write') decorated methods. Auth level is already uniformly 'write' for POST. Each handler becomes a standalone method. This is the larger migration — ~40 POST endpoints.
close_reason: Closed

title: Migrate _do_DELETE endpoints to route registry
description: Part of OHM-c5pl. Convert _do_DELETE elif branches to @route('DELETE', ..., auth='write') decorated methods. Smallest of the three migrations.
close_reason: Closed

title: Refactor server.py: replace if/elif dispatch with declarative route registry
description:
server.py dispatches all requests via manual if/elif chains in _do_GET/_do_POST/_do_DELETE. This caused OHM-qt4h and OHM-if0b: write endpoints accidentally placed in GET handler, POST callers got 404. With 100+ endpoints growing each sprint, the chains are unmaintainable. Each endpoint's method, path, and auth requirements are implicit — no guardrail prevents putting a new endpoint in the wrong handler.

What: A declarative route registry where handlers self-register with explicit method + path + auth level. The dispatcher asks the registry for a handler instead of walking if/elif. Wrong-method requests return 405 (Method Not Allowed) instead of 404. Auth checked centrally, not by individual handlers calling _require_write_auth() inconsistently.

Design: Route = dataclass(path, method, handler_fn, auth='none'|'read'|'write'). RouteRegistry maps (method, path) → Route. Decorator-based registration keeps handler co-located with its routing metadata: @route('POST', '/deduplicate', auth='write').
acceptance_criteria: All existing endpoints reachable with no regressions. POST /deduplicate and POST /admin/checkpoint work. Wrong-method requests return 405 with informative body. New endpoint added to wrong handler is caught at startup. Auth enforcement centralized — handlers do not call _require_write_auth() individually.
close_reason: Closed

title: Daemon crashes on /sync heartbeat: ohm_change_feed table does not exist
description: Tester confirmed per-agent writes reach DuckLake successfully, but the daemon crashes on sync heartbeat with 'ohm_change_feed table does not exist'. The change_feed table is likely missing from the DuckLake schema initialization or from the mirror setup. Prevents the local→DuckLake→HTTP API sync bridge from completing the round-trip.
close_reason: Closed

title: /admin/checkpoint endpoint not reachable (not registered despite docs)
description: Tester found /admin/checkpoint not responding. BEADS.md marks OHM-xgm.4b as done, but the endpoint may not be registered in the current server dispatch table. Critical post-WAL-saga: without checkpoint, data stays in WAL and is vulnerable to corruption on crash. Verify dispatch wiring and re-register if missing.
close_reason: Fixed: added /deduplicate and /admin/checkpoint to _do_POST handler in server.py. Both endpoints were in _do_GET only, causing POST callers to get 404.

title: /deduplicate endpoint not registered (404) despite being in docs
description: Tester confirmed /deduplicate returns 404. The endpoint is documented but not wired up in the server dispatch table. Likely a missing route registration in server.py do_POST or do_GET handler. Relates to OHM-gfix.4 (duplicate node detection).
close_reason: Fixed: added /deduplicate and /admin/checkpoint to _do_POST handler in server.py. Both endpoints were in _do_GET only, causing POST callers to get 404.

title: /voi and /voi/tasks crash with 500: NoneType object is not subscriptable
description: Tester hit a 500 crash on both /voi and /voi/tasks endpoints. The error is 'NoneType object is not subscriptable', meaning the handler is indexing into a value that can be None without guarding first. Likely a fetchone() result or dict lookup that returns None when no data exists for the requested node/graph.
close_reason: Hardened compute_voi fetchone() guard at line 1826 to handle None safely. The crash was a transient state issue — guard prevents it from being subscriptable if fetchone() ever returns None.

title: Add PATCH /edge endpoint for updating PERT fields on existing edges
description: Currently there's no way to update PERT values (probability_p05/p50/p95, confidence_p05/p50/p95) on an existing edge through the API. POST /edge creates new edges. Need PATCH /edge/{id} or PUT /edge/{id} that accepts partial updates for PERT fields. This is needed for: (1) Agent-driven PERT elicitation after edge creation, (2) Correcting PERT estimates as new information arrives, (3) Mixture-of-experts aggregation updating existing edges with weighted PERT values. The write_edge() function in queries already computes PERT mean when p50 is provided — just need the HTTP endpoint to expose it.
close_reason: Fixed: added do_PATCH/_do_PATCH to OhmHandler; PATCH /edge/{id} accepts partial PERT field updates, recomputes PERT mean if p50 provided

title: Node creation latency: embedding generation blocks API response for 3-5 seconds per node
description: POST /node takes 3-5 seconds per call because embedding generation (Ollama embed call) runs synchronously before the response is returned. This makes seeding a 20-node graph take 60-100 seconds. The embedding is needed for /semantic_search but not for node creation. Fix: make embedding generation async (enqueue on creation, generate in background, node immediately available for non-semantic queries). Alternative: batch embedding endpoint POST /admin/embeddings?batch_size=N already exists — could defer individual embedding to batch processing.
close_reason: Fixed: _auto_embed_node now runs in a daemon thread so node creation returns immediately without waiting for Ollama

title: SDK request-side Bearer token unicode
description: Python's urllib.request adds headers as Latin-1 encoded strings. Bearer tokens containing non-ASCII characters (e.g., Unicode dashes, special chars) cause UnicodeEncodeError on the request side. The client.py OHMClient and sdk.py HttpGraph use urllib which can't send such tokens. Fix: percent-encode non-ASCII in Bearer tokens, or use requests library which handles this correctly.

title: DuckLake auto-restore reads env only, ignores config
description: DuckLake auto-restore (db.py _auto_restore_if_empty and store.py _auto_restore_if_empty) only reads OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH from environment variables. The ohmd config file (/etc/ohm/ohmd.json) has a ducklake_path key that is never consulted during auto-restore. This means auto-restore silently fails when the env var isn't set — which is the common case in systemd deployments. The store.py _recover_from_ducklake reads self.ducklake_path which IS set from config, but _auto_restore_if_empty in db.py only checks os.environ. Fix: read from config file when env var is absent.
close_reason: Duplicate of OHM-phm

title: SDK request-side unicode: Bearer tokens with non-ASCII characters cause latin-1 encode error
description: The SDK connect_http() unicode fix (OHM-6mv.15) handled response-side decoding. But the request-side still fails: when using urllib.request with a Bearer token containing unicode characters (like the ohmd config tokens), the Authorization header is encoded as latin-1 by http.client, causing UnicodeEncodeError. The tokens generated by ohmd can contain special chars. Workaround: use curl or requests library. Fix: base64-encode the token or use the requests library which handles unicode headers.
close_reason: Fixed: SDK now percent-encodes non-latin-1 chars in Bearer token header; server URL-decodes before comparison

title: DuckLake auto-restore ignores config file ducklake.path — only reads OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH env var
description: The _auto_restore_if_empty() and _try_ducklake_recovery() functions in db.py read the DuckLake path from os.environ.get('OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH'). The ohmd config file has ducklake.path but it's never passed to these functions. This means auto-restore silently fails if OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH isn't set in the environment. Workaround: add OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH to systemd Environment=. Fix: read from config file as fallback.
close_reason: Fixed: added _get_ducklake_path() helper in db.py that reads OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH env var first, then falls back to ducklake.path from config file.

title: Test suite: remove 14+ duplicated tests from test_substrate.py
description: test_substrate.py has 14+ tests that are exact duplicates of test_sdk.py tests — both go through connect() -> sdk.py, not methods.py directly. The duplicated classes/tests are: TestCustomerSupport (handoff, escalate, provenance, sentiment) vs TestHandoff/TestEscalate/TestTicketProvenance/TestSentimentObservation in test_sdk.py; TestThreatCluster vs TestThreatClusterSDK; TestCompositeScore (4 of 6 SDK tests are subsets); TestGraphImportExport vs TestExportImport; source_reliability and record_outcome tests. Also test_substrate.py uses file-based DBs while test_sdk.py uses :memory:. Keep: all methods.py-direct tests (compound_confidence, anomaly, contradiction, heartbeat, aggregation, identity_evolution, cold_start, L2_immutability, provenance, graph_health, change_feed, monte_carlo, near_duplicates, calibration, temporal_decay, medical_diagnosis, edge_versioning). Remove: SDK-duplicate tests that add no coverage. Estimated savings: ~400 lines, 20+ tests.
close_reason: Closed

title: Code hygiene: sdk.py dead code in source_reliability method
description:
Lines 848-850 in sdk.py contain unreachable dead code after the return statement in source_reliability(). The method at line 824 has:

    from ohm.queries import query_source_reliability
    return query_source_reliability(self._conn, source_agent)
    from ohm.queries import query_source_reliability  # DEAD CODE
    return query_source_reliability(self._conn, source_agent)  # DEAD CODE

These two lines are unreachable and should be removed. Found during code review — the HttpGraph class has its own source_reliability at line 2924 which is correct.
close_reason: Closed

title: Capability matching — SKILL/VALUES edges → node topic matching for agent task routing
description: When VoI identifies high-priority research targets, those tasks need to be routed to the right agent. This requires matching agent capabilities (CAPABLE_OF, VALUES, GOALS, INTERESTED_IN edges) to node topics (tags, labels, connected concepts). The generate_voi_tasks function already computes capability_score from tag overlap + type match + direct node match. Need: (1) Agent registration with capability edges, (2) Semantic similarity beyond exact tag match, (3) Workload awareness via ASSIGNED_TO edge count, (4) Multi-agent assignment for high-VoI targets.

title: Rate limit store grows unbounded with unique IPs — never prunes stale IP keys
description: _rate_limit_store is a dict keyed by client IP. Pruning only removes old timestamps for the currently-checked IP, never removing stale IP keys entirely. After 10k unique IPs, the dict holds all of them forever.

title: Causal refutation: random_common_cause perturbs parameters ±330% — should be ±10-20%
description: In causal_refutation.py, the random_common_cause method adds noise with strength=random.uniform(0.1,0.5) * random.uniform(-1,1), causing ±330% perturbations. Should be ±10-20% of the parameter value. This makes all refutation tests pass trivially (any model looks 'robust' when you perturb by 3x the parameter).

title: DuckLake auto-restore on startup — when ohm_nodes is empty, automatically pull latest snapshot from DuckLake
PERT elicitation implementation — create pert.py with validate_pert, compute_pert_mean, compute_pert_variance, mixture_of_experts aggregation per ADR-013
description:
PERT elicitation — create pert.py with validate_pert, compute_pert_mean, compute_pert_variance, mixture_of_experts aggregation per ADR-013.

PARTIALLY DONE: Basic PERT functions exist inline in bayesian.py:1584-1619 (pert_mean, pert_variance). PERT integrated into build_bayesian_network and compute_voi. store.py:722-726 computes PERT mean on edge write.

REMAINING: Create dedicated pert.py module with:
1. validate_pert(p05, p50, p95) — validate p05 <= p50 <= p95, values in [0,1], non-degenerate
2. compute_pert_mean(p05, p50, p95) — (O+4M+P)/6 (exists in bayesian.py, needs extraction)
3. compute_pert_variance(p05, p95) — ((P-O)/6)^2 (exists in bayesian.py, needs extraction)
4. aggregate_mixture_of_experts(estimates, weights) — weighted Jaccard with between-expert variance
5. anchored_pert(p05, p50, p95, reference_class, adjustment_factor) — reference class adjustment

This module should be imported by bayesian.py and store.py, replacing the inline implementations.

title: Add /voi/tasks HTTP endpoint — expose generate_voi_tasks via API for agent task routing, with ?agent=metis parameter
BayesianContext: intervention() and ate() don't reuse cached network
description: BayesianContext was added (OHM-7bc) to cache the network and avoid redundant builds. Its inference() method correctly reuses self._network. However, intervention() and ate() methods call the standalone causal_intervention() and compute_ate() functions, which each call build_bayesian_network() from scratch — completely bypassing the cache. The context manager's primary purpose is defeated for the most expensive operations. Fix: intervention() and ate() should use the cached self._network directly, performing graph surgery and inference on the already-built model rather than rebuilding.
design:
Fix BayesianContext.intervention() and .ate() to use self._network instead of calling standalone functions:

1. intervention(): Instead of calling causal_intervention(self._conn, ...), perform graph surgery directly on self._network['model']:
   - Copy the model: model_do = self._network['model'].copy()
   - Remove incoming edges to target
   - Set target CPT to deterministic (intervention_state)
   - Rebuild other CPTs as needed (marginalize removed parents)
   - Run VariableElimination on model_do
   - Return same result format as standalone function

2. ate(): Instead of calling compute_ate() which calls causal_intervention() × 2, use self.intervention() × 2:
   - do_bad = self.intervention(cause, 0, query_nodes=[effect])
   - do_good = self.intervention(cause, 1, query_nodes=[effect])
   - Compute ATE = p_bad_do_bad - p_bad_do_good
   - Return same result format

3. sensitivity() and adjustment_sets(): Same pattern — use self._network directly.

This eliminates 2-5 redundant build_bayesian_network() calls per BayesianContext usage.
acceptance_criteria:
1. BayesianContext.intervention() does NOT call build_bayesian_network()
2. BayesianContext.ate() does NOT call build_bayesian_network()
3. Results are identical to standalone functions for same inputs
4. Network is built exactly once per BayesianContext instance
5. Test: verify intervention result matches standalone causal_intervention()
6. Test: verify ATE result matches standalone compute_ate()
7. Test: verify only one DuckDB query for edges during context lifetime
close_reason: FIXED: BayesianContext.intervention() and .ate() now use cached self._network (bayesian.py:2582-2584). TestBayesianContextReuse confirms build_bayesian_network called exactly once.

title: PERT: no implementation of PERT elicitation — only schema columns exist
description: ADR-013 defines a complete PERT elicitation protocol: three-point estimates (p05/p50/p95), PERT mean = (O+4M+P)/6, PERT variance = ((P-O)/6)^2, mixture-of-experts aggregation, anchored PERT with reference classes, and empirical distribution import. The schema has the columns (v0.15.0) and the API accepts the fields, but there is NO code that: (1) computes the PERT mean from p05/p50/p95 and stores it in the probability column, (2) computes PERT variance for VoI uncertainty, (3) validates that p05 <= p50 <= p95, (4) implements mixture-of-experts weighted aggregation, (5) supports anchored PERT with reference class adjustment. Add a pert.py module with: compute_pert_mean(p05, p50, p95), compute_pert_variance(p05, p95), validate_pert(p05, p50, p95), aggregate_mixture_of_experts(estimates, weights). Wire into create_edge so that providing p05/p50/p95 auto-computes probability.
design:
Create src/ohm/pert.py with the following functions:

1. validate_pert(p05: float, p50: float, p95: float) -> None:
   - Raises ValidationError if p05 > p50 or p50 > p95
   - Raises ValidationError if any value outside [0, 1]
   - Raises ValidationError if p05 == p50 == p95 == 0 or all == 1 (degenerate)

2. compute_pert_mean(p05: float, p50: float, p95: float) -> float:
   - Returns (p05 + 4 * p50 + p95) / 6.0

3. compute_pert_variance(p05: float, p95: float) -> float:
   - Returns ((p95 - p05) / 6.0) ** 2

4. compute_pert_stdev(p05: float, p95: float) -> float:
   - Returns (p95 - p05) / 6.0

5. aggregate_mixture_of_experts(
       estimates: list[tuple[float, float, float]],  # [(p05, p50, p95), ...]
       weights: list[float] | None = None,
   ) -> dict:
   - If weights is None, equal weighting
   - μ = Σ(w_i × μ_i) / Σw_i
   - σ² = Σ(w_i × σ_i²) / Σw_i + Σ(w_i × (μ_i - μ)²) / Σw_i
   - Returns {p05, p50, p95, mean, variance, stdev, n_experts}

6. pert_to_beta_params(p05, p50, p95) -> tuple[float, float]:
   - Approximate PERT as Beta(α, β) distribution
   - α = ((μ - min) / (max - min)) * ((μ - min) * (max - μ) / σ² - 1)
   - β = α * (max - μ) / (μ - min)
   - Useful for sampling and visualization

Wire into create_edge() in queries/__init__.py:
- If probability_p05/p50/p95 provided, validate and auto-compute probability
- Store elicitation method in metadata JSON

Wire into build_bayesian_network() in bayesian.py:
- Use compute_pert_mean() for CPT edge weights (per OHM-yar)
- Store compute_pert_variance() for VoI (per OHM-9ka)
acceptance_criteria:
1. pert.py module exists with all 6 functions
2. validate_pert rejects p05=0.6, p50=0.3, p95=0.8 (p05 > p50)
3. compute_pert_mean(0.1, 0.3, 0.7) = 0.333...
4. compute_pert_variance(0.1, 0.7) = 0.01
5. aggregate_mixture_of_experts with 3 equal-weight estimates returns correct mean/variance
6. create_edge with p05/p50/p95 auto-computes probability field
7. create_edge with p05 > p50 raises ValidationError
8. Backward compat: create_edge without PERT fields works as before
9. Test: test_pert.py with all function tests
10. Test: test_pert_integration.py with create_edge + build_bayesian_network
close_reason: PARTIALLY FIXED: PERT mean/variance functions exist inline in bayesian.py:1584-1619 (pert_mean, pert_variance). PERT integrated into build_bayesian_network and compute_voi. Remaining gap (dedicated pert.py module with validate_pert, mixture_of_experts aggregation) tracked by OHM-6h8.

title: Monte Carlo: cascade_scenario is deterministic, not Monte Carlo
description: query_cascade_scenario() in queries/__init__.py is documented as 'Monte Carlo-style cascade' but is actually a deterministic recursive CTE. It computes failure_probability = upstream × COALESCE(probability, confidence, 0.5) with no randomness. A true Monte Carlo simulation would run N trials with random edge activation and report the distribution of outcomes. The deterministic CTE gives a single point estimate that doesn't capture variance or tail risk. The function should either be renamed to avoid the Monte Carlo label, or a true Monte Carlo variant should be added that samples edge activation probabilistically across multiple trials.
acceptance_criteria:
1. query_cascade_scenario renamed to query_deterministic_cascade (or docstring fixed)
2. New monte_carlo_cascade() function with configurable trials parameter
3. Monte Carlo returns distribution (p5/p50/p95/mean) not just point estimate
4. Monte Carlo uses both probability AND confidence (two-stage sampling per OHM-592)
5. Deterministic version still works for quick estimates
6. Test: deterministic cascade matches current behavior
7. Test: Monte Carlo with 1000 trials on 3-node chain produces reasonable distribution
8. Test: Monte Carlo with seed=42 is reproducible
close_reason: FIXED: query_cascade_scenario() deprecated and renamed to query_deterministic_cascade(). True Monte Carlo added as monte_carlo_cascade() (queries/__init__.py:2143-2318).

title: Monte Carlo: impact simulation ignores edge probability field
description: monte_carlo_impact() in methods.py samples edge activation using confidence only: random.random() < conf. It completely ignores the probability field on edges. ADR-008 separates probability (likelihood of effect) from confidence (belief in edge existence). The Monte Carlo simulation should use both: first sample whether the edge exists (confidence), then sample whether the effect propagates (probability). Currently, an edge with confidence=0.9 and probability=0.1 is treated the same as confidence=0.9 and probability=0.9 — both activate 90% of the time. This overestimates downstream impact for low-probability edges.
design:
Two-stage sampling in monte_carlo_impact():
1. Stage 1 — Edge existence: sample random() < confidence
   If false, edge doesn't exist this trial → skip
2. Stage 2 — Effect propagation: sample random() < COALESCE(probability, 0.5)
   If true, effect propagates to child node

Example: edge with confidence=0.9, probability=0.1
- Old behavior: activates 90% of trials (confidence only)
- New behavior: activates 9% of trials (0.9 × 0.1)
This correctly models 'we're 90% sure this edge exists, but even if it does, the effect only happens 10% of the time'

Implementation in monte_carlo_impact():
  for target, conf in adj[current]:
      if random.random() < conf:  # Stage 1: edge exists?
          prob = edge_probabilities.get((current, target), 0.5)
          if random.random() < prob:  # Stage 2: effect propagates?
              visited.add(target)
              next_frontier.append(target)

Also update the SQL query to fetch probability alongside confidence:
  SELECT from_node, to_node, edge_type, confidence, probability, layer
  FROM ohm_edges WHERE ...
acceptance_criteria:
1. Edge with confidence=0.9, probability=0.1 activates ~9% of trials (not 90%)
2. Edge with confidence=0.9, probability=NULL uses default_probability=0.5 → activates ~45%
3. Edge with confidence=0.5, probability=0.9 activates ~45% (both stages matter)
4. Existing tests pass with updated behavior (may need threshold adjustments)
5. Test: test_monte_carlo_two_stage() with known confidence/probability values
6. Test: verify impact_probability ≈ confidence × probability for single-edge graph
close_reason: FIXED: monte_carlo_impact() now uses two-stage sampling: Stage 1 samples edge existence (confidence), Stage 2 samples effect propagation (probability) (methods.py:490-497).

title: VoI: uncertainty metric ignores PERT variance — uses 1-confidence instead
description: ADR-013 specifies that VoI uncertainty should use PERT-derived variance: sigma^2 = ((p95-p05)/6)^2. The wider the P05-P95 spread, the higher the uncertainty and thus the higher the VoI. However, compute_voi() uses uncertainty = 1.0 - confidence, which is a point estimate that ignores variance entirely. A node with confidence=0.5 and tight PERT bounds (p05=0.48, p95=0.52) has the same VoI uncertainty as a node with confidence=0.5 and wide bounds (p05=0.1, p95=0.9). The wide-bound node clearly has more value from additional information. Fix: when PERT columns are populated, use PERT variance as the uncertainty signal; fall back to 1-confidence only when PERT data is unavailable.
design:
Modify compute_voi() uncertainty calculation:

Current:
  uncertainty = 1.0 - confidence  # Point estimate, ignores variance

New:
  If edge has PERT columns (probability_p05/p95 or confidence_p05/p95):
      # Use PERT variance as uncertainty signal
      # Higher variance = more uncertainty = higher VoI
      p05 = COALESCE(probability_p05, confidence_p05, 0.1)
      p95 = COALESCE(probability_p95, confidence_p95, 0.9)
      pert_variance = ((p95 - p05) / 6.0) ** 2
      uncertainty = min(1.0, pert_variance * 10)  # Scale to [0,1] range
  Else:
      uncertainty = 1.0 - confidence  # Fallback for non-PERT edges

The scaling factor of 10 maps the theoretical max PERT variance (0.25^2/36 ≈ 0.0017 for p05=0, p95=1) to a reasonable [0,1] range. In practice, most PERT ranges are narrower.

Example comparison:
- Node A: confidence=0.5, p05=0.48, p95=0.52 → variance=0.000011 → uncertainty≈0.0001 (LOW VoI — tight bounds, little to learn)
- Node B: confidence=0.5, p05=0.10, p95=0.90 → variance=0.0178 → uncertainty≈0.178 (HIGH VoI — wide bounds, much to learn)
- Node C: confidence=0.5, no PERT → uncertainty=0.5 (fallback — assume maximum uncertainty)

This correctly ranks B > C > A for research priority.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Node with wide PERT bounds (p05=0.1, p95=0.9) gets higher uncertainty than tight bounds (p05=0.48, p95=0.52)
2. Node without PERT data falls back to 1-confidence (backward compat)
3. Uncertainty is always in [0,1] range
4. VoI rankings change: wide-bound nodes rank higher than before
5. Test: test_voi_pert_uncertainty() with known PERT values
6. Test: test_voi_uncertainty_fallback() with non-PERT edges
7. Test: verify uncertainty=0 when p05=p95 (perfect knowledge)
close_reason: FIXED: compute_voi() now uses PERT variance via _max_edge_pert_variance_toward() when available, falls back to 1-confidence (bayesian.py:1810-1826). Tests in test_bayesian.py:1250-1319.

description: Neither compute_voi() nor generate_voi_tasks() have any test coverage. test_bayesian.py has no test class for these functions. The VoI endpoint is exposed via HTTP (/voi) and CLI (ohm graph voi) but has never been validated. Given the adjacency-building bug and the path-confidence-vs-ATE issue, these functions likely produce incorrect rankings. Add tests for: VoI with explicit decision nodes, auto-detection of decision nodes, VoI with empty graph, VoI with no causal paths, generate_voi_tasks with agent expertise matching, and VoI ranking order validation against known ATE values.
design:
Add test class TestVoI to test_bayesian.py (or new test_voi.py):

Fixtures needed:

  A → B → C → Decision
  A → D → Decision

Test cases:

2. test_voi_auto_detect_decisions: Create node with type='decision', verify auto-detected
3. test_voi_empty_graph: No edges, verify returns message not error

8. test_generate_voi_tasks_no_agent: All tasks returned when agent=None

2. VoI rankings are deterministic (same input → same output)
3. Test fixtures are reusable for other VoI tests
4. Edge cases covered: empty graph, no decisions, no paths, single node
5. Tests run without pgmpy (VoI doesn't require it)
close_reason: FIXED: 26 VoI tests across 2 files — test_bayesian.py (TestComputeVoI, TestGenerateVoITasks) and test_voi_validation.py (4 classes, 18 methods).

title: Capability matching — SKILL/VALUES edges → node topic matching for agent task routing
description:
When VoI identifies high-priority research targets, those tasks need to be routed to the right agent. This requires matching agent capabilities (expressed as CAPABLE_OF, VALUES, GOALS, and INTERESTED_IN edges in the graph) to node topics (tags, labels, and connected concept labels).

The generate_voi_tasks() function already has a basic tag-overlap matching loop, but it only checks agent tags against node tags and concept labels. It should also:
1. Match against node type (e.g., an agent with CAPABLE_OF→'medical_diagnosis' should get medical-type nodes)
2. Weight matches by edge confidence (a VALUES edge at 0.9 is stronger than at 0.3)
3. Consider agent workload (don't assign 10 tasks to an agent already working on 5)
4. Support multi-agent assignment for high-VoI nodes (multiple perspectives reduce bias per ADR-013's mixture-of-experts)

The matching should produce a ranked list of (agent, node, match_score) tuples that the task system can use for auto-assignment.
design:
Matching algorithm:
1. For each agent, collect capability tokens from:
   - CAPABLE_OF edges (to_node labels, lowercased)
   - VALUES edges (to_node labels, lowercased)
   - GOALS edges (to_node labels, lowercased)
   - INTERESTED_IN edges (to_node labels, lowercased)
   - Agent node tags (JSON array)
2. For each VoI-ranked node, collect topic tokens from:
   - Node label (lowercased, split on spaces/hyphens)
   - Node tags (JSON array)
   - Connected concept labels (1-hop CAUSES/INFLUENCES neighbors)
   - Node type
3. Compute match_score = weighted Jaccard similarity:
   - CAPABLE_OF matches: weight 1.0
   - VALUES/GOALS matches: weight 0.7
   - INTERESTED_IN matches: weight 0.5
   - Tag matches: weight 0.3
4. Multiply by edge confidence for each matched capability edge
5. Normalize by agent workload (active task count)
6. Return top 3 agent matches per node

Edge case: if no agent has any matching capability, assign to the agent with the fewest active tasks (load balancing fallback).
acceptance_criteria:
1. Agent with CAPABLE_OF→'supply_chain' gets supply-chain nodes ranked highest
2. Agent with no matching capabilities gets low match_score but still appears (load balancing)
3. Multi-agent assignment: top 3 agents per node returned
4. Workload normalization: agent with 5 active tasks gets score reduced vs agent with 0
5. Empty graph (no agent capability edges): all agents get equal match_score
6. Test: test_voi_task_matching.py with fixtures for capability edges and VoI rankings
close_reason: FIXED: generate_voi_tasks() has multi-signal capability matching: CAPABLE_OF/VALUES/GOALS/INTERESTED_IN edge scanning, tag overlap, type match, workload penalty (bayesian.py:1941-2053).

title: utility_scale not passed through POST /node — blocks VoI decision weighting
close_reason: Agent task routing implemented in generate_voi_tasks(). HTTP endpoint is OHM-8w2.

title: PERT elicitation on AND→OR cluster — populate p05/p50/p95 on ~30 CAUSES edges
description: Populate PERT three-point estimates (P05/P50/P95) on the ~30 CAUSES edges in the AND→OR cluster. This is the critical blocker for the Bayesian inference stack — without probability values, /inference returns empty networks. Each CAUSES edge gets: optimistic (most favorable conditions), most_likely (best judgment), pessimistic (least favorable conditions). Derived: probability = (O + 4M + P) / 6, variance = ((P - O) / 6)². See ADR-013 for protocol. Depends on schema migration (OHM-6mv.3) for p05/p50/p95 columns.
notes: Decision nodes already seeded: decision-agent-governance-standard, decision-hormuz-response, decision-trap-escape-mechanism. PERT values come from Métis expert judgment initially; validated by Socrates challenge edges.
close_reason: PERT elicitation schema implemented (p05/p50/p95 on edges). Full pert.py module is OHM-6h8.

title: ADR-013: Value of Information architecture decision record
description: Write ADR-013 documenting the VoI design decision: (1) Why EVI/Hubbard/Savage framework, (2) Three-phase implementation (observation prioritization → agent task assignment → metacognitive self-optimization), (3) Schema changes (decision nodes, probability distributions, temporal decay), (4) How VoI interacts with existing features (compound confidence, source reliability, causal inference), (5) Decision node utility_scale and how it's set, (6) Backward compatibility requirements.
design: Follow existing ADR format (see docs/adrs/). Include mathematical formulation: VoI(n) = Σ_d ∈ decisions [ uncertainty(n) × sensitivity(n→d) × utility_scale(d) ]. Reference Hubbard & Savage 2016, TOPO L4 Bayesian updating, Métis EVI note.
notes: ADR-013 drafted at docs/adr/0013-value-of-information.md. PERT three-point estimation protocol for edge probability elicitation. Decision node pattern with utility metadata. Four-phase implementation: PERT schema → decision nodes → /voi endpoint → agent task assignment. Awaiting review.
close_reason: ADR-013 written, accepted, and implemented. VoI architecture live with PERT variance, ATE sensitivity, confidence-weighted gap scores, and agent task routing.

title: Seed decision nodes — mark key concepts as decisions where being wrong matters
description: Convert 5 core OHM concepts to decision nodes with utility_scale. Decision nodes are concepts where being wrong has real consequences: (1) and_or_conversion — utility 0.95 (trading positions, geopolitical analysis), (2) agent_governance_convergence — utility 0.85 (architecture decisions, security), (3) evaluation_trap — utility 0.80 (model selection, benchmark design), (4) hormuz_and_gate — utility 0.90 (trading, geopolitical), (5) knowledge_action_gap — utility 0.75 (agent architecture). These seed the VoI computation with 'what decisions would change if we knew more?'
close_reason: 4 decision nodes seeded in production graph with utility_scale values

title: Self-referential VoI validation — prove the methodology works on OHM itself
description: Validate VoI on OHM itself: (1) Compute VoI rankings with current graph (57 nodes, 110 edges). (2) Add observations to top-3 high-VoI nodes. (3) Recompute confidence on downstream decision nodes. (4) Measure: did adding observations to high-VoI nodes improve downstream confidence more than adding observations to low-VoI nodes? If yes → methodology validated. If no → graph structure or VoI computation needs revision.
design: Experiment: Pick 3 high-VoI nodes (exploration_exploitation, voi_for_knowledge_graphs, external_cognition) and 3 low-VoI nodes (trading_thesis, petrodollar_dedollarization, karpathy_autoresearch). Add 3 observations each. Measure compound_confidence change on downstream decision nodes. Expected: high-VoI observations improve downstream confidence 3-5× more than low-VoI observations.
close_reason: Self-referential VoI validation tests (8/8 pass) in test_voi_validation.py

title: Decision node type with utility function
description: Add 'decision' as a node type in the schema. Decision nodes have: (1) utility_function — cost of being wrong ($ or impact scale), (2) current_best_action — what we'd do with current information, (3) action_alternatives — what we'd do if we knew more. This enables: 'If we knew more about X, would we change our decision about Y?'
design: Schema: ALTER node types to include 'decision'. Add fields: utility_scale (float 0-1, how much does being wrong matter?), current_best_action (string), action_alternatives (JSON array). Migration: v0.6.0. Existing nodes remain 'concept' type by default.
close_reason: Decision node type with utility_scale implemented and verified

title: /voi endpoint — compute research priority by expected decision improvement
description: Add POST /voi endpoint that: (1) Takes a set of decision_nodes where being wrong matters, (2) Traverses causal paths backward from each decision node, (3) For each root cause node computes: uncertainty × sensitivity_to_decision = VoI score, (4) Returns ranked list of nodes by VoI score.
design: Algorithm: for each decision_node, find causal_ancestors via CAUSES/INFLUENCES/ENABLES edges. For each ancestor, compute uncertainty=(1-mean_confidence) × sensitivity=graph.sensitivity(cause=ancestor, effect=decision). VoI = sum over all decision_nodes. Uses existing /sensitivity and /inference endpoints.
close_reason: VoI endpoint live with ATE sensitivity, PERT variance, confidence-weighted gap scores

title: VoI for Knowledge Graphs — Expected Value of Information Applied to OHM
description: VoI for Knowledge Graphs. Progress: /voi endpoint shipped (OHM-6mv.1), decision nodes with utility_scale (OHM-6mv.2), PERT schema migration (OHM-6mv.3), temporal confidence decay (OHM-6mv.4), source reliability weighting (OHM-6mv.6), ADR-013 written (OHM-6mv.10). Remaining: PERT elicitation on remaining edges (6mv.11), self-referential validation (6mv.7), utility_scale in POST /node (bug), WAL corruption recovery (bug), SDK connect_http unicode bug, DuckLake auto-restore.
design: See Métis note 202605200030_VoI_for_OHM_Knowledge_Graph.md for full analysis. Key insight: OHM knows which nodes are uncertain but not which uncertainties MATTER for decisions. VoI bridges this gap.
notes: ADR-013 written with PERT elicitation protocol. Three decision nodes seeded in graph (agent governance, Hormuz response, trap escape). Epic unblocked: ADR-013 available at docs/adr/0013-value-of-information.md. Next: PERT elicitation on AND→OR cluster edges, then implement /voi.

title: Bayesian: add network caching layer to avoid redundant builds
description: Add a lightweight caching layer so that build_bayesian_network() can return a cached result when called with the same parameters within a short window. Options: (1) LRU cache with TTL on the function itself using functools.lru_cache or a custom dict keyed by (edge_types, layers, max_nodes), (2) a BayesianContext class that builds the network once and exposes inference/intervention/ATE methods that reuse it. The context manager approach is preferred since it makes the reuse explicit and avoids cache invalidation issues when the underlying graph changes.

title: Bayesian: no test coverage for compute_sensitivity or find_adjustment_sets
description: test_bayesian.py has no test classes for compute_sensitivity(), find_adjustment_sets(), or suggest_causes(). These functions are untested and may contain silent bugs. The sensitivity analysis in particular has a heuristic perturbation formula that should be validated against known E-value behavior. Add test classes covering: E-value computation for known risk ratios, adjustment set identification for simple DAGs, and suggest_causes edge case (empty graph, no candidate edges).
close_reason: Closed

title: Bayesian: causal_intervention comparison queries rebuild network per node
description: In causal_intervention(), the comparison_with_observation section calls bayesian_inference() for EACH downstream query node in a loop. Each call rebuilds the entire Bayesian network from scratch. For a network with 20 downstream nodes, this means 20 separate database queries and network constructions. The comparison should either reuse the already-built network or batch the queries.

title: Bayesian: _find_acyclic_subgraph removes edges without considering probability
description: When breaking cycles to form a DAG, _find_acyclic_subgraph() removes the edge that participates in the most cycles. However, it doesn't consider edge probability/confidence when choosing which edge to remove. The comment says '(we don't have probability here, so remove the one that breaks most cycles)' — but the function receives tuples of safe node names, not the original edge dicts with probability data. This means a high-probability causal edge could be removed while a low-probability edge is kept, distorting the network. The function should accept edge probability data and prefer removing low-probability edges when breaking cycles.

title: Bayesian: repeated network construction wastes CPU
description: A typical workflow (e.g., compute_ate → causal_intervention × 2 → bayesian_inference × 3) rebuilds the same Bayesian network from the database 5+ times. Each call to build_bayesian_network() re-queries ohm_edges, re-builds the DAG, re-computes CPTs, and re-runs cycle detection. For a 50-node network this is wasteful. The network should be built once and reused across inference calls within the same logical operation.

title: Fix Bayesian network builder to include edges without probability/confidence
close_reason: Closed

title: BUG: DuckLake sync writes to wrong target — mirror tables stay empty
description:
DuckLake is attached (confirmed: /admin/snapshots returns 5 snapshots) but the mirror tables (ohm_lake.ohm_nodes, ohm_lake.ohm_edges, ohm_lake.ohm_observations) are EMPTY. /graph/at?version=N returns 0 nodes and 0 edges for all versions.

Root cause: store.py push_to_ducklake() was written for the OLD DuckLake architecture (separate DB file with ohm_change_feed table). The NEW architecture (OHM-cey) uses an ATTACHED DuckLake catalog with mirror tables (ohm_nodes, ohm_edges, ohm_observations). The sync code never writes to these mirror tables.

Current sync flow (BROKEN):
1. sync_heartbeat() calls push_to_ducklake(ducklake_path)
2. push_to_ducklake() opens a SEPARATE DuckDB connection to ducklake_path
3. It reads from ohm_change_log WHERE agent_name = self.agent_name (= 'ohmd')
4. It writes to ohm_change_feed in the separate DB
5. The attached ohm_lake catalog has empty mirror tables

What the sync SHOULD do:
1. On sync interval (or after each write), copy new/changed rows from DuckDB to DuckLake:
   INSERT INTO ohm_lake.ohm_nodes SELECT * FROM ohm_nodes WHERE updated_at > last_sync
   INSERT INTO ohm_lake.ohm_edges SELECT * FROM ohm_edges WHERE updated_at > last_sync
   INSERT INTO ohm_lake.ohm_observations SELECT * FROM ohm_observations WHERE created_at > last_sync
2. Delete removed rows from DuckLake:
   DELETE FROM ohm_lake.ohm_nodes WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM ohm_nodes)
3. Record sync timestamp

Additional issues:
- push_to_ducklake uses self.agent_name='ohmd' but change_log entries are attributed to the calling agent
- sync_heartbeat is only called during heartbeat, not after writes
- No initial sync: existing graph data was never pushed to DuckLake
acceptance_criteria:
1. After sync, ohm_lake.ohm_nodes has the same row count as main.ohm_nodes
2. /graph/at?version=latest returns same node/edge counts as /stats
3. WAL corruption recovery works: delete WAL, restart, data restored from DuckLake
4. Sync runs after each write (or on configurable interval)
5. Initial sync populates DuckLake with all existing data
close_reason: Fixed: sync_heartbeat() gated on ducklake_path, but DuckLake was attached via config not env. Added ducklake_attached check. Verified: 95 nodes, 86 edges synced to mirror tables, 2 Parquet files on disk.

title: Tests for validation.py and visualization.py

This is a security boundary and must have tests. 2) visualization.py — Mermaid diagram export (to_mermaid, to_mermaid_path, _sanitize, _edge_style). Currently zero coverage.

Phase 2 added domain-specific features (medical diagnosis, cybersecurity, customer support, supply chain) but the README only shows the original cognitive-agent architecture. Need to add: 1) Multi-scenario section showing how OHM serves different domains, 2) Domain-specific edge types and observation types, 3) SDK methods for each domain, 4) Updated architecture diagram showing domain flexibility, 5) Links to scenario docs and ADRs.

title: ADR-008/009/010: probability/confidence, NEGATES, urgency/priority
