title: source→pattern should default to L2 citation edge, not L3 RELATED_TO
description: /edge/suggest-type currently returns L3 RELATED_TO for source→pattern. A source citing a pattern is better modeled as L2 REFERENCES or SUPPORTS_EVIDENCE so it doesn't get treated as an agent interpretation.
acceptance_criteria: Update suggest_edge_type() so source→pattern/concept/idea defaults to L2 REFERENCES with participates_in_inference=false. Add test for source→pattern.
close_reason: Shipped by Hephaestus 2026-07-02.

title: Copy-paste reasoning text in /edge/suggest-type for idea→task
description: For idea→task pairs, /edge/suggest-type returns the correct suggested type (REFINES) but the reasoning text says 'Pattern→task should use REFINES, not CAUSES...' even though the from_type is idea. This is a minor copy-paste error that erodes trust in the endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: Fix reasoning text to reference the actual from_type (idea). Add a test covering idea→task reasoning.
close_reason: Shipped by Hephaestus 2026-07-02.

title: Duplicate /challenge and /support calls create duplicate edges
description: Calling POST /challenge/{id} or POST /support/{id} twice for the same original edge creates two identical CHALLENGED_BY/SUPPORTS edges. /confidence then over-counts until POST /deduplicate is run. This is easy for agents to do accidentally.
acceptance_criteria: 1) /challenge and /support are idempotent per agent+edge, OR 2) auto-deduplicate on creation. 3) Add tests for duplicate challenge/support.
close_reason: Shipped by Hephaestus 2026-07-02.

title: Open Skills needs schema guide and template/query endpoints
description: The new skill and runbook node types (OHM-461f) are accepted by POST /node and filterable via /nodes?type=, but /schema node_type_guide is empty for them and there are no /templates or /queries endpoints. Agents have no guidance on how to use skill/runbook nodes semantically.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Populate node_type_guide for skill and runbook with usage examples and required/optional fields. 2) Add /templates or /node-types/{type}/template endpoints. 3) Add /queries endpoint listing useful queries for skill/runbook graphs. 4) Verify skill→runbook DEPENDS_ON is suggested by /edge/suggest-type.
notes: Confirmed open, correctly parented under OHM-461f, assigned to hephaestus. Per Hephaestus report this P2 task is not started and remains in backlog.

title: TOPO: DataProductRun execution tracking
description: P2 gap from TOPO migration analysis. TOPO records each notebook/analytical execution as a DataProductRun with run_id, status, outputs. OHM's ohm_data_products catalog (ODPS, ADR-027) is declaration-only; productVersion partially covers versioning but doesn't track individual runs. Either extend ohm_data_products with run rows or provide a topo-specific DomainTable for execution tracking. Needed for analytical reproducibility and lineage in TOPO.
notes: 2026-07-02 (metis): Reports/DataProducts are unblocked by the ADR-040 pattern (DomainTable convention). TOPO can add a topo_runs / topo_reports DomainTable for execution tracking and analytical artifacts without waiting for core ohm_data_products extensions.

title: OHM infrastructure: per-agent isolated local DuckDB caches (ADR-004)
description: P2 gap from TOPO migration analysis. OHM architecture shows per-agent local DB ↔ DuckLake ↔ per-agent local DB in README, but ADR-004 says the per-agent local DuckDB cache is architecture designed, not built. TOPO's ingest service and CLI agents each need their own isolation for writes. Until per-agent caches exist, all TOPO agents must share a single OhmStore connection, which won't scale. Required for any multi-agent write pattern.

title: TOPO: versioned analytical report artifacts (DomainTable)
description: P2 gap from TOPO migration analysis. TOPO has versioned analytical reports (sensitivity_analysis, rca_report, correlation_study) linked bidirectionally to graph entities. OHM has ohm_data_products which is conceptually similar but oriented toward operational data products, not analytical findings. Provide a topo_reports DomainTable and an SDK workflow for finalize → update L3 edge confidence feedback loop. Likely TOPO-side schema extension using OHM DomainTable substrate.
notes: 2026-07-02 (metis): Reports/DataProducts are unblocked by the ADR-040 pattern (DomainTable convention). TOPO can add a topo_reports DomainTable for analytical artifacts and wire finalize → L3 confidence feedback via existing OHM edges without waiting for core data-product extensions.

title: TOPO: RUL assessment storage hook via DomainTable
description: P1 gap from TOPO migration analysis. TOPO's rul.py is a complete Weibull 2P + Crow-AMSAA NHPP + change-point detection engine producing RulAssessment objects stored in topo_prospects. The statistical engine should stay in TOPO, but OHM needs a DomainTable DDL for topo_prospects and an SDK hook like graph.register_rul_assessment(node_path, assessment) to store RUL results and link them as L4 PREDICTS edges. Depends on OHM-ivlt (node_path) for addressing and the existing DomainTable / topo.json pipeline.
notes: 2026-07-02 (metis): Downgraded to P2 per TOPO gap analysis. RUL statistical engine stays in TOPO; only storage hook was requested. DomainTable for topo_prospects can be added once node_path/UNS addressing (OHM-ivlt) lands, but is not P0/P1 blocking now that assessment storage pattern is clear.

title: TOPO: timeline rollup query with horizon, date range, and ancestor grouping
description: P2 gap from TOPO migration analysis. TOPO's Timeline/TimelineEntry model rolls up child-node events to any ancestor via L1 CONTAINS edges, filters by horizon (HISTORICAL/CURRENT/PLANNED/FOCAST) and date range, and includes plan objects. OHM neighborhood() does not support date-range filtering or horizon discrimination. Add query primitives for bounded temporal rollups once OHM-ay5k (events) and OHM-4qdk (plans) exist.
notes: 2026-07-02 (metis): Re-parented under OHM-dh9l (ADR-041 temporal event model). Timeline rollup depends on the unified temporal-event model.

title: TOPO: node_path / UNS address as first-class graph identifier
description: P1 gap from TOPO migration analysis. TOPO uses UNS paths (pns.fm10, pns.kiln.main_drive) as both graph address and hierarchy key, with path-prefix matching for timeline and rollup queries. OHM nodes have id and label but no structured path field. Need a path column on ohm_nodes (or formal UNS address concept) so domains like TOPO, manufacturing, and building systems can use hierarchical addressing alongside semantic edges.
notes: 2026-07-02 (metis): Downgraded to P2 per TOPO gap analysis. Alias workaround (node_path via node id prefix or label) is viable for timeline/rollup queries in the near term; generic UNS address remains desired but not P0/P1 blocking.

comments.author: root
comments.text:
Matt asked whether production plans, inventory, capacity, and supply chain should also have twins/models. Yes — these are decision-rich, slower-dynamics domains perfect for OHM.

Proposed templates added to metis:20260630_OHM_Twin_Template_Catalog.md:
- production_plan_v1: schedule feasibility and profit
- inventory_position_v1: stock levels and reorder timing
- capacity_planning_v1: capacity vs. demand over horizon
- supply_chain_network_v1: multi-echelon flow risk and cost
- demand_forecast_v1: forecast accuracy and bias

These are natural snap-in twins (ERP/MRP/APS data sources). OHM stays as decision-intelligence layer; it pulls state from existing systems and proposes actions.

comments.author: root
comments.text:
Deep-dive addition: network-of-processes supply chain twin. A supply chain where nodes include industrial processes (refinery, cracker, polymer plant) and edges are multimodal logistics (ship/rail/truck/pipeline). This is a hierarchical composition of snap-in node twins under a network-level optimization/simulation twin.

Key elements:
- Node types: supplier/source, conversion node, buffer node, demand node, hub/cross-dock
- Edge types: source→conversion, conversion→conversion, conversion→buffer, buffer→demand, return/recycle
- Each node is a snap-in twin exposing /state, /predict, /constraints, /validate-action
- Network models: MEIO, discrete-event simulation, robust optimization, graph disruption propagation, transportation mode selection
- Disruption propagation simulated via POST /scenario with edge_overrides and node_interventions
- Decisions span operational (divert shipment), tactical (safety stock, supplier allocation), strategic (network design)
- Validation: backtest on historical disruptions, live shadow, stress tests, counterfactual regret

Open questions:
1. Decomposition vs. centralized optimization for large networks
2. Multimodal path representation
3. Co-product/by-product modeling
4. Right granularity for process nodes

Draft stored in metis memory 2026-06-30.md due to Zettelkasten flush restriction.

title: OHM per-agent read scopes and L0 cognitive privacy
description:
Not everything in OHM should be visible to every agent. L0 scratch fragments are explicitly provisional and should stay scoped to their author until promoted. Sensitive observations, source reliability internals, and strategic predictions may also need scoped access.

Scope model:
- public: visible to all agents
- agent-only: visible only to the creating agent and Atlas/coordinators
- team: visible to a defined group (e.g. research agents, trading agents)
- temporal: pinned to a specific knowledge version

Implementation areas:
- Add scope column to nodes/edges/observations
- Respect scope in /search, /neighborhood, /listen, /changes
- L0 fragments default to agent-only and are excluded from search unless include_l0=true
- Atlas/coordinator role can override for triage
- Document which layers (L0-L4) default to which scopes

Why this matters:
- Lets agents think out loud without polluting shared knowledge.
- Prevents premature exposure of uncertain claims.
- Enables strategic privacy where needed.

Acceptance:
- Scope enforced on all read endpoints.
- L0 scratch is private-by-default.
- Regression tests for scope leakage.
- Complements OHM-wvz8 (read scopes child).

title: OHM verification auto-detection: prompt agents to verify dateable claims
description:
ADR-018 requires recording outcomes for causal claims, but the process is currently manual and opt-in. This feature auto-detects when a dated claim can be verified and prompts the relevant agent(s) to record an outcome.

Detection rules:
- Claim has a predicted date or window (e.g. 'Hormuz reopens within 30 days')
- Claim has a measurable predicate (price threshold, event occurrence, policy change)
- Window has passed or event has occurred in a monitored source
- No outcome recorded yet

Prompt behavior:
- Add task or nudge: 'Your claim X is now verifiable. What was the outcome?'
- Include links to the claim and any new relevant observations
- Allow TRUE / FALSE / AMBIGUOUS / DEFERRED with reason

Why this matters:
- Closes the verification loop without relying on agents to remember every prediction.
- Source reliability becomes data-driven, not anecdotal.
- Challenge ratio and verification rate metrics become accurate.

Acceptance:
- Background job or hook scans verifiable claims daily.
- Prompts generated as tasks or nudges.
- Agents can record outcome in one action.
- Monitored sources configurable (RSS, web, ingestion pipeline).

title: Backfill OHM challenges missing challenge_reason
description:
45 open CHALLENGED_BY edges have challenge_reason=NULL. ADR-018 and the verification pipeline depend on explicit rationale for challenges; null reasons degrade the challenge ratio metric and make it impossible to evaluate whether a challenge was substantive.

Context:
- 3 high-confidence null-reason challenges were already patched manually on 2026-06-24.
- Remaining 45 were assigned to Socrates but not completed.
- Metis can batch-backfill these by reading the target edge and inferring the reason from edge type + confidence gap.

Acceptance:
- All open CHALLENGED_BY edges have non-null, non-empty challenge_reason.
- Reasons are specific (cite the edge type mismatch or overclaim).
- No challenges are modified without an explicit rationale.
- Add a constraint or lint to prevent future null-reason challenges.
close_reason: Implemented in commit 01a87eb: feat(challenges): backfill null-reason challenges + lint guard.

title: Add agent 'what changed since I last checked' discovery endpoint
description:
Agents currently poll /listen, /contradictions, /anomalies, /stale, /suggest separately to reconstruct what happened since their last session. A single endpoint should return a personalized delta for an agent.

Proposed endpoint: GET /changes?agent=metis&since=2026-06-26T00:00:00Z

Returns:
- new_nodes_created_by_or_relevant_to_agent
- new_edges_touching_agent's_claims
- new_observations_on_watched_nodes
- contradictions_involving_agent_nodes
- challenges_to_agent_edges
- tasks_assigned_or_status_changed
- stale_nodes_needing_refresh

Use cases:
- Faster session startup without 6 separate polls.
- Surface surprises: 'Your claim X was challenged' or 'Node Y got a new observation'.
- Enable proactive agent behavior.

Acceptance:
- Endpoint returns structured JSON with sections above.
- Supports since timestamp or 'last session' lookup per agent.
- Respects per-agent read scopes if/when those exist.
- Performance: <500ms for metis-sized footprint.

title: OHM schema: add hypothesis-tree node/edge types for iterative research
description:
Arbor treats research as a hypothesis tree where each node binds a hypothesis, artifact version, experiment evidence, and distilled insight. OHM's graph already has observations, edges, concepts, and source reliability. Add explicit hypothesis-tree primitives so OHM can represent an iterative research process, not just a static knowledge base.

Proposed additions:
- node_type: hypothesis (with status: proposed / tested / verified / pruned)
- node_type: experiment (links to artifact commit/ref, dev score, test score, duration)
- edge_type: TESTS (experiment → hypothesis)
- edge_type: REFINES (child hypothesis → parent hypothesis)
- edge_type: SUPPORTS_EVIDENCE / CONTRADICTS_EVIDENCE (experiment → concept/edge, like observation but tied to an experiment node)
- observation.obs_type: experiment_result with fields for dev_metric, test_metric, worktree_ref

Queries:
- find_active_hypotheses(project_id)
- best_verified_artifact_path(hypothesis_id)
- propagate_experiment_result(parent_hypothesis_id) — update ancestor confidence from child evidence

Acceptance:
- Schema migrations for new node/experiment types and edge types.
- Unit tests creating hypothesis trees and propagating evidence.
- Document how HTR maps onto OHM's existing L0-L4 layers (L2/L3 evidence, L3/L4 hypotheses).
design: Arbor's HTR is the action counterpart to OHM's epistemic graph. This task makes the bridge explicit. Depends on current node_type/edge_type extensibility; if hard-coded, refactor first.
notes: 2026-06-21 08:15 EDT: Done. Implemented schema, migration 0.34.0, helper queries, tests. 303 passed. Committed and pushed to GitHub as 4fb4245.

title: Arbor evaluation: run HTR optimization on an OHM harness task
description:
Evaluate the Arbor framework on a real OpenClaw/OHM harness-engineering task to see whether the 2.5x held-out-gain claim transfers from paper benchmarks to our codebase.

Scope:
- Pick a bounded, metric-driven task with a clean eval (e.g., improve pytest runtime / coverage, optimize a query helper, tighten a price-alert/trading harness).
- Provide Arbor with an initial artifact, a benchmark script, and a goal.
- Run its native CLI with a modest budget (smoke → pilot).
- Compare its final artifact against a baseline (current code) and against the same budget spent with a plain coding agent.

Acceptance:
- A reproducible Arbor run config checked into a scratch branch.
- Dev/test split evaluation and held-out metric recorded.
- Report: what worked, what failed, runtime cost, merge readiness, and whether Arbor should become a regular tool.

Risks:
- Autonomous code edits in the repo; must use Arbor's isolated git worktrees and never merge without review.
- Long-running experiments may hang; use smoke/pilot budgets and timeouts.
- Paper benchmarks may not generalize to our less-metric tasks.
design: Complements OHM's epistemic graph: Arbor produces experiments and improved artifacts; OHM records the outcomes as observations. If the evaluation succeeds, the next step is an OHM-Arbor bridge task.
notes: 2026-06-21 08:15 EDT: Unblocked by OHM-ss22. Schema now in GitHub main (4fb4245). Pilot design doc at metis:zettelkasten/notes/202606210800_OHM_Arbor_Pilot_Design.md. Next: generate synthetic graph, run baseline, execute Arbor AO agent.

title: Temporal Decay Phase 4: Weibull Generalization — replace discrete decay profiles with continuous shape parameter
description:
Replace the 5 discrete decay profiles (perishable, fast-perishable, durable, binary, appreciating) with a continuous Weibull shape parameter. The current system works but has 5 special cases; Weibull with shape κ subsumes all 5 into a single continuous function.

Design:
- Generalize confidence_at() to use Weibull formula:
  - shape == 0: binary step function (valid until explicitly superseded)
  - shape < 0: appreciation formula
  - shape > 0: confidence = value * exp(-(age/scale)^shape), where scale = half_life_days / ln(2)
- Add weibull_shape column to observations with defaults per obs_type:
  - measurement: 1.0 (perishable)
  - sentiment: 1.5 (fast-perishable)
  - verification: 0.7 (durable)
  - outcome: 0 (binary)
  - source: 1.0
  - pattern: -1 (appreciating)
- Migrate Phase 1 half_life_days values; preserve existing effective-confidence behavior.
- Later refinement: learn shape and scale from supersession data via scipy.stats.weibull_min when enough events accumulate.

API changes:
- GET /observation/{id}/confidence?at=... returns weibull_shape, half_life_days, effective_confidence, and decay_function string.
- /admin/learned-half-lives shows Weibull shape alongside half-life when data exists.

Acceptance:
- weibull_shape column exists with correct defaults per obs_type
- confidence_at() uses Weibull formula
- Phase 1 behavior preserved (migration maps profiles correctly)
- Learned half-lives endpoint includes Weibull shape
- All Phase 1 profiles produce identical results with Weibull parameters
design: Dependencies: Phase 1 half_life_days column must exist first. Phase 3 learned half-lives become learned Weibull parameters. Estimated effort: 9-14h.

title: DEDUP: Content Hashing and Alias Resolution — idempotent ingestion and entity deduplication
description:
OHM has no content hashing or entity deduplication. When the ingestion pipeline fetches the same article from two RSS feeds, or when two agents create nodes for the same concept with slightly different IDs, we get duplicates. No mechanism detects that concept-demand-rationing and concept-demand-rationing-escalation might be the same node. Re-running the pipeline creates duplicates.

Design:
1. Content hashing: add content_hash field to source nodes and queue items. In Stage 1 (fetch), compute SHA-256 of fetched content; if hash matches an existing item, skip (idempotent ingestion).
2. Alias resolution: add ohm_aliases table (alias_norm, node_id, created_at). On node creation, normalize the label, check aliases first, and return existing node_id if found; otherwise create node + insert alias.
3. Merge operation: POST /admin/merge with canonical_id, duplicate_ids, and strategy (keep_higher_confidence / keep_newest / keep_most_observed) to merge edges/observations and delete duplicates.

API surface:
- GET /resolve?query=hormusz — alias resolution search
- POST /admin/merge — merge duplicate nodes
- GET /admin/duplicates?threshold=0.85 — list suspected duplicates

Success criteria:
- Content hashing on ingestion pipeline (Stage 1 fetch)
- Idempotent re-ingestion
- Alias table created and populated
- Alias resolution on node creation and search
- Duplicate detection (cosine similarity >= 0.85)
- Merge operation (edges + observations + delete duplicate)
design: Dependencies: ADR-013 (source citation / source_url) for content hash tying; shell-hook ingest (OHM-aznh) for running hash in pre-ingest hook.

title: Add performance tests for semantic search and HD fingerprints
description:
With ~2,325 nodes now embedding-backed, we need baselines for semantic search and HD fingerprint queries. Add performance tests under a `tests/performance/` directory (or mark with `@pytest.mark.performance`):

1. `semantic_search` latency at 100/1,000/2,000 nodes.
2. HD fingerprint similarity query latency and memory.
3. Manifold density compute cost per result (depends on OHM-nnrw).
4. `source_diversity_score` at depth=3 on a dense node.

Acceptance:
- Tests fail if latency exceeds thresholds (initial: p95 < 200ms for single query, < 2s for batch).
- Performance suite can be run independently: `pytest -m performance`.
- Results are printed in a format that can be tracked over time.

title: Add HTTP integration tests for internalized verification endpoints
description:
The internalized verification features are unit-tested but lack dedicated HTTP integration coverage. Add tests for the server handlers:

1. `POST /nodes` with `source_tier` and confidence — verify 201/400 behavior.
2. `POST /agent/synthesis` returns `source_diversity_score` (depends on OHM-8q5d).
3. `GET /nodes/{id}` returns TELOS signing fields (`write_signature`, `signing_key_id`, `signed_at`).
4. `POST /nodes/{id}/verify` or equivalent returns verified/not-verified.
5. Read-scope enforcement: agent A with `source_tier: [peer_reviewed]` cannot read UGC nodes.
6. Temporal pinning: `GET /snapshot?at=...` excludes soft-deleted nodes.

Acceptance:
- New file `tests/test_server_internalized_verification.py` or extend `test_server.py`.
- Tests run against the daemon or a test server instance.
- All new endpoints have at least one happy-path and one denial test.

title: Reduce OHM orphan rate via automated triage
description:
OHM's graph health endpoint reports 428 orphans (18.2% orphan rate) out of 2,346 nodes. Many of these are likely L0 fragments or one-off observations that should either be linked, promoted, or compacted.

This task is to reduce the orphan rate by:
1. Distinguishing L0 fragments (expected high churn) from L1-L3 orphans.
2. Running the existing `/suggest` and `/islands` endpoints over orphans.
3. Auto-linking high-confidence suggestions in batches.
4. Adding a heartbeat nudge when orphan rate exceeds a threshold.

Acceptance:
- Orphan rate drops below 10% for non-fragment nodes.
- A daily triage bead/list is produced with top 20 orphan-link suggestions.
- Heartbeat nudges include orphan-rate trend.

title: Open Skills portable skill contract integration
description:
Integrate Nate Jones' Open Skills model into OHM and OpenClaw: portable skill.mmarkdown contracts with trigger, scope, tools, boundaries, output, and verification evidence. This maps directly to OHM's internalized verification redesign (source tiers + outcome tracking) and the three-layer memory model (prompt vs memory vs skill).

Key deliverables:
- Skill node schema: trigger, scope (personal/project/universal), required tools, boundaries, output format, verification evidence list.
- Runbook node schema: ordered DEPENDS_ON chain of skill nodes.
- Session-to-skill extractor hook: after substantial agent sessions, detect recurring procedures and propose skill candidates.
- Verification contract enforcement: skill execution produces observations linked to required evidence types.
- OpenClaw skill loader compatibility: generate Cursor rules / claude.md / Codex instructions from OHM skill nodes to prevent drift.

Connects to: OHM-wvz8 (hyperdimensional membership), OHM-wvz8.1 (source tiers), OHM-wvz8.3 (embeddings now complete), OHM-jbsr (oppositional review), OHM-yk7z (tastebud fingerprinting), OHM-xtzk (ripen-then-decide triage).

Source: https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/claude-codex-agent-skills and https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9PUaEj0pMYE

Pocock's workflow layer complements Open Skills' portability layer. Converged OHM synthesis created linking both.

comments.text: Temporal pinning lets agents query the graph 'as of' a specific time without being overwritten by newer consensus. Per-agent read scopes let different agents see different confidence/verification layers. Both prevent the present consensus from erasing earlier, possibly more accurate, textual/observable evidence.

title: OHM-a5rz.28: Cluster detection for fragment promotion candidates
description:
Replaces original OHM-a5rz.14 with concrete design. When 3+ fragments share 2+ auto-link targets, they form a cluster worth examining for promotion to L1 concept.

Implementation: query fragment nodes, group by shared CONTEXT_OF targets, identify clusters of 3+ fragments sharing 2+ targets. Surface these as promotion candidates via GET /fragments?clusters=true.

Does not auto-promote — just surfaces candidates. An agent decides whether the cluster is meaningful.

Acceptance: GET /fragments?clusters=true returns groups of fragments that share multiple CONTEXT_OF targets. Each cluster includes fragment ids, shared targets, and cluster size.
close_reason: Implemented in commit a685f23. GET /fragments?clusters=true returns fragment clusters with themes. query_fragment_clusters() in queries. Live verified (no clusters yet — only 5 fragments, needs 3+ per cluster).

title: OHM-a5rz.27: Fragment TTL and soft eviction
description:
Fragments are meant to be cheap and plentiful. Without eviction, the graph fills with noise. Design a TTL system:

- Default TTL: 30 days for fragments (configurable)
- After TTL, fragment is soft-deleted (deleted_at set) but not removed
- Soft-deleted fragments excluded from all queries (already handled by deleted_at IS NULL filter)
- Before eviction, check: does this fragment have L0 edges? If yes, extend TTL by 30 days (connected fragments are worth keeping)
- Before eviction, check: was this fragment promoted? If yes, never evict (it birthed a concept)

Implementation: background job in ohmd that runs hourly. SELECT fragment nodes where created_at < now() - interval '30 days' AND deleted_at IS NULL. Check edge count and promotion status before soft-deleting.

Acceptance: Fragment older than 30 days with 0 edges is soft-deleted. Fragment with edges gets TTL extended. Promoted fragment never evicted.
close_reason: Closed

title: OHM-a5rz.26: Fragment promotion to L1 concept
description:
When a fragment accumulates enough evidence (3+ CONTEXT_OF edges, or 2+ other agents reference it), it may deserve promotion to a proper concept node. This is the L0→L1 transition.

Implementation: add POST /fragments/{id}/promote endpoint. Copies the fragment to a new concept node (type='concept', layer='L1'), creates a REFINES_FRAG L0 edge from concept to original fragment, and marks the fragment with metadata.promoted_to = concept_id.

Not automatic — an agent must decide a fragment is worth promoting. But the endpoint makes the action nearly free.

Acceptance: POST /fragments/{id}/promote creates a concept node with same content. Fragment metadata shows promoted_to. L0 REFINES_FRAG edge links concept → fragment.
close_reason: Implemented in commit 11df946. POST /fragments/{id}/promote creates concept node from fragment with REFINES_FRAG edge back. Live verified: promoted warsh_and_gate_cascade fragment to concept.

title: OHM-a5rz.25: Cross-agent fragment resonance via shared auto-links
description:
Replaces original OHM-a5rz.13 with concrete design. When two agents write fragments that auto-link to the same node, that's resonance — independent agents noticing the same thing from different angles.

Implementation: after auto-link creation, check if any other fragment nodes link to the same target. If >= 2 fragments from different agents link to the same node, create an L0 RESONANCE edge between the fragments (not the agents).

Query: GET /fragments?resonance=true returns fragments ordered by resonance count (shared targets).

Acceptance: Agent A scratches 'Hormuz oil supply tight', Agent B scratches 'Warsh rate hike driven by energy'. Both auto-link to concept-and-gate-control-plane. A RESONANCE edge is created between the two fragments.
close_reason: Implemented in commit 11df946. _create_resonance_edges detects when fragments from different agents share context targets, creates RESONANCE L0 edges. /fragments?resonance=true sorts by resonance count. Live verified.

title: OHM-a5rz.24: Fragment density metric and dashboard

Kuzu has 2.68 links/note. OHM has 1.77 edges/node. To track whether L0 cheap writes are building meaningful density, we need a fragment-specific density metric:

- fragments_total: count of fragment nodes
- fragments_with_links: count with >= 1 L0 edge
- fragment_density: avg L0 edges per fragment
- auto_link_precision: ratio of auto_links that are semantically relevant (requires manual tagging initially)

Add to /stats?include_l0=true response. Track over time to see if semantic auto-linking (OHM-a5rz.19) improves density vs substring matching.

Acceptance: /stats?include_l0=true includes fragment_density section.
close_reason: Implemented in commit 11df946. /stats?include_l0=true now returns fragment_density dict with fragments_total, fragments_with_links, total_fragment_edges, and density ratio. Live verified: fragment_density=1.2.

title: DEDUP: Content Hashing and Alias Resolution — idempotent ingestion and entity deduplication
description:
## Problem

OHM has no content hashing or entity deduplication. When the ingestion pipeline fetches the same article from two RSS feeds, or when two agents create nodes for the same concept with slightly different IDs, we get duplicates. No mechanism to detect that concept-demand-rationing and concept-demand-rationing-escalation might be the same node. Re-running the pipeline creates duplicates.

## Inspired By

GBrain has two systems we lack:
1. Content hashing: SHA-256 of page content, skip ingestion if hash unchanged. Makes ingestion idempotent.
2. Alias resolution: Free-text alias layer normalizes entity references. Alice Chen, Dr. A. Chen, achen@example.com all resolve to the same person page.

## Design

### 1. Content Hashing for Ingestion

Add content_hash field to source nodes and queue items. In Stage 1 (fetch), compute SHA-256 of fetched content. If hash matches existing item, skip (idempotent). Makes the entire pipeline safe to re-run.

### 2. Alias Resolution

Add ohm_aliases table (alias_norm, node_id, created_at). When creating a node:
- Normalize label (lowercase, strip punctuation, collapse whitespace)
- Check aliases first, return existing node_id if found
- If new, create node + insert alias

Handles: "Hormuz AND-Gate" == "hormuz_and_gate" == "Strait of Hormuz AND-gate"

### 3. Merge Operation

POST /admin/merge with canonical_id, duplicate_ids, strategy (keep_higher_confidence / keep_newest / keep_most_observed). Merges edges, observations, deletes duplicates.

### API

GET /resolve?query=hormusz — Alias resolution search
POST /admin/merge — Merge duplicate nodes
GET /admin/duplicates?threshold=0.85 — List suspected duplicates

### Dependencies

- ADR-013 (source citation) — content hash ties to source_url
- OHM-aznh (shell hooks) — hashing should run in pre-ingest hook

### Success Criteria

- [ ] Content hashing on ingestion pipeline (Stage 1 fetch)
- [ ] Idempotent re-ingestion (same content = skip)
- [ ] Alias table created and populated
- [ ] Alias resolution on node creation and search
- [ ] Duplicate detection (cosine similarity >= 0.85)
- [ ] Merge operation (edges + observations + delete duplicate)
- [ ] Zero duplicate nodes after merge of existing graph
close_reason: Closed

title: TRAJ: Temporal Regression Detection — flag observations that reverse trend direction, like gbrain find_trajectory
description:
## Problem

OHM has observations with timestamps but no temporal regression detection. When an observation's value reverses trend direction (e.g., "deal probability rising" then "deal probability falling"), we have no automated mechanism to flag it. For AND-gate tracking, this is critical: a layer that was trending toward resolution suddenly reversing is more important than a static low probability.

## Inspired By

GBrain's find_trajectory tracks metric claims over time and auto-flags regressions. gbrain eval trajectory shows chronological history with regressions highlighted. gbrain founder scorecard produces four-signal rollups (claim_accuracy / consistency / growth_trajectory / red_flags). This is built on their Facts fence with typed metric claims (metric: mrr, value: 50000, unit: USD, period: monthly).

## Design

### Observation Time Series

For numeric-valued observations, compute:
1. Trend direction (rising/falling/flat)
2. Regression detection (value reverses direction)
3. Acceleration (rate of change itself changing)
4. Consistency (how often different sources agree)

### API

GET /trajectory/{node_id}?metric=probability&kind=observation&since=2026-01-01

Response includes data_points with date/value/source/sigma, trend direction, regressions array (with from_value, to_value, direction, previous_trend, significance), consistency score, and red_flags.

### AND-Gate Specific

For AND-gate nodes, trajectory also tracks:
- How many layers are at P=0, P<0.1, P<0.5, P>0.5 over time
- Whether the AND-gate is becoming more or less likely to resolve
- Whether any single layer's regression is dragging the whole gate

This maps directly to Hormuz: deal probability rising then falling = deal-hope inversion.

### Dependencies

- OHM-tnwa (gap analysis) — trajectory feeds into gap detection
- Observations with numeric values and timestamps (existing)

### Success Criteria

- [ ] /trajectory/{node_id} endpoint returns time series with trend and regression flags
- [ ] Regression detection correctly identifies direction reversals
- [ ] AND-gate specific trajectory (layer probability tracking over time)
- [ ] Red flag generation for significant regressions
- [ ] Integration with gap analysis (stale observations as gaps)
close_reason: Closed

title: DOCTOR: Graph Health Scoring with Dependency-Ordered Remediation — gbrain doctor --remediate equivalent for OHM
description:
## Problem

OHM has no automated health scoring or remediation. When the graph degrades (dead-end nodes accumulate, challenge ratio drops, verification rate stalls), there's no system to detect and fix it. We run `discover.py orphans` and `discover.py suggest` manually during heartbeats. There's no single health score, no dependency-ordered fix plan, no cost cap.

## Inspired By

GBrain's `gbrain doctor --remediate --target-score 90 --max-usd 5` walks a dependency-ordered plan (sync before extract, embed after consolidate), re-checking score between every step, refusing to spend past the cost cap. It's a closed-loop health system.

## Why This Compounds

Without self-healing, the graph degrades over time. Dead-ends accumulate, verification rate drops, challenge ratio stays at 2.7%. The graph gets wider but not denser. Doctor makes the graph *self-improving* rather than *slowly-degrading*.

Currently: 675 nodes, 1109 edges, 145 dead-ends, density=1.64, challenge_ratio=0.027. A health score would surface this degradation and a remediation plan would fix it.

## Design

### Health Score (0-100)

Computed from weighted sub-scores:

| Component | Weight | Current Metric | Target |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------|
| Dead-end ratio | 25% | dead_end_count / total_nodes | <10% |
| Edge density | 20% | edges / nodes | >2.0 |
| Verification rate | 20% | verified_causal / total_causal | >50% |
| Challenge ratio | 15% | challenges / total_L3 | >10% |
| Observation coverage | 10% | nodes_with_obs / total_nodes | >30% |
| Staleness | 10% | avg days since last obs | <7 days |

### Remediation Plan (Dependency-Ordered)

```
1. Sync ingestion pipeline (fetch → triage → source)     [zero tokens]
2. Run orphan detection + auto-suggest                      [zero tokens]
3. Embed stale nodes (re-embed if content changed)           [low cost]
4. Run verification scan (ADR-018)                           [medium cost]
5. Challenge dubious edges (requires OHM-cuu0 private vault) [medium cost]
6. Synthesize orphan clusters (≥3 connected orphans)         [high cost]
7. Promote private vault items (confidence ≥ 0.8)            [zero tokens]
```

Each step re-checks the health score. If target reached, stop. If cost cap hit, stop and report what was accomplished.

### API

```
GET  /admin/health                    — Current health score + breakdown
POST /admin/health/remediate          — Run remediation plan
       ?target_score=90               — Stop when score reaches N
       ?max_cost_usd=5                — Stop when token cost exceeds $N
GET  /admin/health/remediate/plan      — Preview plan without executing
GET  /admin/health/remediate/status    — Current remediation run status
```

### Implementation

```python
def compute_health_score(store: OhmStore) -> dict:
    stats = store.stats()
    dead_end_ratio = stats["dead_end_count"] / max(stats["total_nodes"], 1)
    edge_density = stats["total_edges"] / max(stats["total_nodes"], 1)
    verification_rate = stats.get("verification_rate", 0)
    challenge_ratio = stats.get("challenge_ratio", 0)
    
    scores = {
        "dead_end": max(0, 100 - dead_end_ratio * 500),
        "edge_density": min(100, edge_density * 50),
        "verification": verification_rate * 100,
        "challenge": min(100, challenge_ratio * 1000),
        "observation": (stats["nodes_with_observations"] / max(stats["total_nodes"], 1)) * 100,
        "staleness": 100  # computed from obs timestamps
    }
    
    weights = {"dead_end": 0.25, "edge_density": 0.20, "verification": 0.20, 
               "challenge": 0.15, "observation": 0.10, "staleness": 0.10}
    
    overall = sum(scores[k] * weights[k] for k in scores)
    return {"overall": overall, "components": scores, "weights": weights}
```

### Dependencies

- OHM-tjzh (cross-link constraint) — directly improves dead_end and edge_density scores
- OHM-tnwa (gap analysis) — feeds staleness detection
- ADR-018 (verification loops) — feeds verification_rate
- OHM-cuu0 (vault isolation) — enables safe challenge remediation
- OHM-vj3i (trajectory) — regression detection feeds staleness score
- OHM-g0kv (dedup) — dedup reduces dead-end count

### Success Criteria

- [ ] /admin/health endpoint returns composite score + breakdown
- [ ] Remediation plan generator (dependency-ordered steps)
- [ ] Dry-run mode (preview plan without executing)
- [ ] Cost cap enforcement (stop at max_usd)
- [ ] Target score achievement (stop when reached)
- [ ] Health score improves from baseline to ≥70 within 2 weeks of remediation
close_reason: Closed

title: INGEST.12: Update ADR-016 with hook lifecycle diagram and hook spec
description:
## Task

2. Hook spec: event types, payload schemas, exit code semantics

5. Built-in hooks: cross_link_check, source_url_required

title: INGEST.11: Migrate cross-link enforcement to a built-in pre_ingest hook (proof of concept)
description:
## Task
Migrate cross-link enforcement (_enforce_cross_link_requirement in graph.py) to a built-in pre_ingest hook as proof of concept.

This proves the hook architecture works by converting the most complex existing pre-write check into a hook.

## Approach
1. Register a built-in 'python:ohm.hooks_builtin.cross_link_check' hook on server startup
2. Remove the inline _enforce_cross_link_requirement() call from _post_node
3. The hook performs the same check: derived claim types must have connects_to referencing existing nodes
4. Hook returns exit_code=1 + stderr with the same error JSON structure

## This is a P2 refactor
Only do this AFTER INGEST.1-6 are complete and tested. It's a proof-of-concept for migrating existing checks to hooks, not required for the hook system to work.

## Files
- src/ohm/hooks_builtin.py: cross_link_check() implementation
- src/ohm/server/handlers/graph.py: remove inline call, rely on hook
- src/ohm/server/server.py: register built-in hook on startup
- tests/test_hooks.py: verify cross-link still enforced via hook

## Acceptance
- POST /node with derived type and no connects_to still returns 422
- POST /node with connects_to still returns 201
- _enforce_cross_link_requirement() removed from handler code
- Built-in hook registered automatically on server start
close_reason: Inline _enforce_cross_link_requirement + ADR-013 source_url check removed from _post_node. Built-in hooks (cross_link_check, source_url_required) registered on startup via _register_builtin_hooks(). 5 server tests.

title: INGEST.10: Wire pre_query/post_query hooks into GET handlers — query param rewrite and response decoration
description:
## Task
Wire pre_query and post_query hooks into GET handlers.

## pre_query
- Runs BEFORE the query handler executes
- Receives: {event: pre_query, path, query_params}
- Can modify query_params by writing JSON to stdout
- Non-zero exit: return 403 Forbidden (query blocked by hook)

## post_query
- Runs AFTER the query handler executes
- Receives: {event: post_query, path, query_params, response_body}
- Can decorate response by writing JSON to stdout (merged under hook_decorations)
- Non-zero exit: logged but does NOT block the response

## Wiring points
- In _do_GET() in server.py, after auth + before handler dispatch for pre_query
- After handler returns, before _json_response for post_query

## Files
- src/ohm/server/server.py: wire in _do_GET()
- tests/test_hooks.py: test pre_query modifies params, post_query decorates response

## Acceptance
- pre_query hook can modify query params
- pre_query non-zero exit returns 403
- post_query hook stdout merged into response
- No hooks means normal operation unchanged
close_reason: pre_query hooks (403 on reject, query_params override via JSON stdout), post_query hooks (hook_decorations merge, failures logged not blocked), _json_response buffering, 6 server tests

title: INGEST.9: Python callable hooks — support callables alongside shell commands for in-process hooks
description:
## Task
Support Python callables alongside shell commands for in-process hooks.

## python: prefix
If hook command starts with 'python:', import and call the function directly:
- 'python:ohm.hooks_builtin.cross_link_check' -> from ohm.hooks_builtin import cross_link_check
- Callable signature: def hook(payload: dict) -> tuple[int, str, str] -> (exit_code, stdout_json, stderr)
- stdout_json: JSON string that gets parsed for response decoration
- stderr: error message for abort

## Built-in hooks module
Create src/ohm/hooks_builtin.py with reusable hook implementations:
- cross_link_check: validates connects_to for derived claim types (replaces inline enforcement)
- source_url_required: validates source_url for source node type (replaces ADR-013 inline check)
- rate_limit: simple per-agent rate limiter (configurable via metadata)

## Files
- src/ohm/hooks.py: implement python: prefix in run_hook()
- src/ohm/hooks_builtin.py: new module with cross_link_check, source_url_required
- tests/test_hooks.py: test python: prefix, test built-in hooks

## Acceptance
- python: prefix imports and calls the function
- Built-in cross_link_check returns exit_code=1 for derived claims without connects_to
- Built-in source_url_required returns exit_code=1 for source nodes without source_url
- Import errors return exit_code=1 with error message
close_reason: python: prefix hooks with __conn injection, hooks_builtin.py with cross_link_check/source_url_required/rate_limit, 13 new tests

title: INGEST.8: Hook sandbox — restricted environment (no network, read-only fs except tenant dir, CPU/wall-clock limits)
description:
## Task
Sandbox hook execution to prevent arbitrary code from compromising the system.

## Restrictions
1. No outbound network: use OS-level restrictions (subprocess preexec_fn on Linux, or restricted token on Windows)
2. Read-only filesystem except tenant data directory
3. CPU limit: enforce via timeout_ms (already implemented)
4. Memory limit: set via subprocess resource limits (rlimit on Linux, job object on Windows)
5. No environment inheritance: pass only OHM_HOOK_EVENT, OHM_HOOK_ID, OHM_CUSTOMER_ID

## Implementation approach
- Linux: preexec_fn sets resource limits (RLIMIT_AS, RLIMIT_NPROC=0, RLIMIT_NOFILE=64)
- Windows: CREATE_RESTRICTED_TOKEN or job object with limits
- Fallback: if sandboxing unavailable, log warning and run unsandboxed (dev mode)

## Security model
- Hooks registered by tenant agents can only affect tenant data
- System-level hooks (admin-only) run with full privileges
- All invocations logged regardless of sandbox status

## Files
- src/ohm/hooks.py: add _sandbox_preexec() to run_hook() subprocess call

## Acceptance
- Hook subprocess cannot open outbound network connections (Linux: RLIMIT_NPROC, or documented as best-effort on Windows)
- Hook subprocess has restricted env vars only
- Timeout still enforced
- Fallback works when sandboxing unavailable
close_reason: Closed

title: INGEST.7: Hook invocation logging — ohm_hook_log table for audit trail (exit code, stderr, duration)
description:
## Task
Add ohm_hook_log table for audit trail. Log every hook invocation.

## Schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ohm_hook_log (
    id          VARCHAR PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    hook_id     VARCHAR NOT NULL,
    event       VARCHAR NOT NULL,
    payload     JSON,
    exit_code   INTEGER,
    stdout      TEXT,
    stderr      TEXT,
    duration_ms FLOAT,
    timed_out   BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    triggered_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

Index: idx_hook_log_hook ON ohm_hook_log(hook_id)
Index: idx_hook_log_time ON ohm_hook_log(triggered_at)

## Integration
HookRunner.run_hook() inserts into ohm_hook_log after each invocation.

## Files
- src/ohm/graph/schema.py: DDL + migration (v0.22.0)
- src/ohm/hooks.py: insert into ohm_hook_log in run_hook()
- tests/test_hooks.py: verify log row created after hook execution

## Acceptance
- ohm_hook_log table exists after schema init
- Every hook invocation creates a log row
- Log row contains exit_code, stderr, duration_ms, timed_out
- GET /hooks/log endpoint (optional, can be separate issue)
close_reason: ohm_hook_log DDL + migration v0.22.0, _log_invocation in HookRunner.run_hook(), 5 hook tests + 5 schema tests

title: SECURITY FIX: Negative Content-Length bypass in _read_body() — server reads unbounded body
description:
## Vulnerability

`_read_body()` in `server/server.py` read `int(self.headers.get('Content-Length', 0))` and only checked `if length > MAX_BODY_SIZE`. A negative Content-Length (e.g., `Content-Length: -1`) passed this check and was passed to `self.rfile.read(length)`. In CPython, `io.read(-1)` reads until EOF — effectively reading an unbounded body and bypassing the MAX_BODY_SIZE limit entirely. This allows a client to exhaust server memory.

## Fix Applied

Added `length < 0` check before the size limit:
```python
if length < 0 or length > MAX_BODY_SIZE:
    self._send_error(413, 'Request body too large')
    return None
```

## Commit

Fixed in session 2026-06-04 as part of holistic security review.
close_reason: fixed: added `length < 0` guard in _read_body()

title: BUG: Concurrent writes data race — test_concurrent_writes_with_lock_no_corruption fails (55 != 50)
description:
## Symptom

`test_concurrent_writes_with_lock_no_corruption` sends 50 concurrent POST /node requests and expects exactly 50 nodes in the graph. It finds 55 — 5 extra nodes, meaning the write-lock is not preventing duplicate writes under concurrency.

This is a pre-existing failure confirmed by running against the unmodified codebase.

## Root Cause Hypothesis

The per-tenant `threading.Lock` in `TenantManager` protects the DuckDB connection from concurrent _access_, but the lock may not cover the full read-check-write sequence. If two threads each read 'node X does not exist', both pass the existence check, and both write — creating a duplicate.

The most likely offender is the `POST /node` handler: it checks for an existing node by ID before inserting, but this check+insert is not atomic under the lock if the lock is acquired and released between the check and the write.

## Fix

Ensure the check-then-insert sequence is wrapped in a single lock acquisition. Or use DuckDB's `INSERT OR IGNORE` / `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` to make the write idempotent at the database level — this is the simpler and more robust fix.

## Impact

This blocks Quack activation (ADR-016 production readiness criteria: `test_ten_agents_concurrent_writes_no_data_loss` also fails with HTTP 500s).
close_reason: Closed

title: BUG: GET /granger → HTTP 500 — Granger causality endpoint crashes
description:
## Symptom

`test_granger_endpoint` fails: the test sends a valid GET /granger request and receives HTTP 500. This is a pre-existing failure confirmed by `git stash` + test run against the unmodified codebase.

## Likely Cause

The Granger causality implementation in `src/ohm/inference/` likely requires `statsmodels` or `numpy` for the VAR model, and either:
- The dependency is missing from `pyproject.toml`
- The implementation raises an unhandled exception (division by zero, empty time-series, etc.)
- The endpoint handler doesn't catch the specific exception type raised

## Investigation

1. Run `python3 -c 'from ohm.inference.granger import granger_causality'` — if ImportError, the dependency is missing
2. Check server logs for the 500 traceback
3. Check `pyproject.toml` for statsmodels in dependencies

## Fix

Add missing dependency and/or add exception handling in the Granger handler that returns a structured error response instead of propagating the exception.
close_reason: Already fixed: test_granger_endpoint passes (4 granger tests, all pass). Likely resolved by a prior change.

title: SECURITY: Rate limiter uses raw socket IP — bypassed behind reverse proxy
description:
## Problem

The rate limiter in `server/server.py` keys on `self.client_address[0]` — the raw TCP socket IP. When OHM runs behind nginx, Caddy, or any reverse proxy, every request arrives from the proxy's loopback address (127.0.0.1). All tenants share one rate-limit bucket and a single agent can trivially exhaust it for everyone.

More importantly: a malicious agent can send unlimited requests by routing through the proxy, completely bypassing the per-IP limit.

## Fix

Read the real client IP from `X-Forwarded-For` or `X-Real-IP` when present:

```python
def _get_client_ip(self) -> str:
    xff = self.headers.get('X-Forwarded-For')
    if xff:
        return xff.split(',')[0].strip()  # leftmost = original client
    xri = self.headers.get('X-Real-IP')
    if xri:
        return xri.strip()
    return self.client_address[0]
```

**Security note**: Only trust these headers if OHM is behind a trusted proxy. Add a config flag `TRUSTED_PROXY_IPS` (default empty = don't trust forwarded headers). When empty, fall back to socket IP. When set, only trust X-Forwarded-For from those IPs.

## Current Code

`server/server.py`: `_rate_limit_store` uses `self.client_address[0]` as the key in the `_check_rate_limit()` method.
close_reason: Closed

title: BUG FIX: GET /voi HTTP 500 — compute_voi() missing include_soft_evidence and soft_edge_types params
description:
## Symptom

`test_voi_reports_mixed_sensitivity_methods` failed: GET /voi returned HTTP 500. The `_get_voi` handler in `server/handlers/inference.py` was passing `include_soft_evidence=True` and `soft_edge_types=[...]` to `compute_voi()`, but `compute_voi()` in `inference/bayesian.py` did not accept those parameters. Python raised `TypeError: compute_voi() got an unexpected keyword argument 'include_soft_evidence'` which the handler caught as an unhandled exception and returned 500.

## Fix Applied

Added `include_soft_evidence: bool = False` and `soft_edge_types: list[str] | None = None` to `compute_voi()`'s signature.

## Files Changed

- `src/ohm/inference/bayesian.py` — `compute_voi()` signature
close_reason: fixed: added missing params to compute_voi()

title: N+1 UPDATE+SELECT pattern in admin source_url bulk update
description: admin.py:115-141 iterates updates with individual UPDATE + verification SELECT per row (2n round-trips for n updates). Fix: executemany() for UPDATE, skip verification SELECT (use affected row count).
close_reason: Closed

title: Unbounded _bayesian_network_cache with no eviction
description: bayesian.py:47 _bayesian_network_cache module-level dict grows without bound. In long-running ohmd, many param combinations = unbounded memory. Fix: LRU eviction with configurable max size (e.g. 50 entries).
close_reason: Replace unbounded dict with _LRUBayesianCache (maxsize=50). Evicts oldest entry when exceeded. Preserves dict interface for existing callers (clear, in, len, []).

title: import_graph uses individual INSERT per row instead of batch
description: sdk.py:2549-2603 import_graph() does individual INSERT per row. Fix: executemany() or multi-row INSERT with batch sizes 100-500.
close_reason: Closed

title: N separate VE inference calls in intervention() for multi-node queries
description: bayesian.py:2926-2943 intervention() loops over query nodes calling infer.query(variables=[qn]) separately. Same model, same evidence, repeated VE. Fix: call infer.query(variables=safe_query_nodes) once, extract marginals from joint.
close_reason: Replace N per-node infer.query() calls with single batch infer.query(variables=all_nodes) + marginalize() to extract per-node marginals. Same fix applied to both causal_intervention() and BayesianContext.intervention(), covering both the do-operator and observation comparison paths. Fallback to individual queries if batch fails.

title: N+1 queries in generate_voi_tasks per ranking entry
description: bayesian.py:2413-2429 generate_voi_tasks does reader.get_nodes(ids=[id]) per ranking entry + fetches ALL concept edges per entry. Fix: batch node lookups before loop, fetch concept edges ONCE and reuse.
close_reason: Batch node lookups via single get_nodes(ids=...) call before loop; move get_edges for concept labels outside loop and index by from_node. Eliminates N SQL roundtrips per ranking entry.

title: query_stale_edges loads all edges, should compute decay in SQL
description: queries/__init__.py:1436-1485 query_stale_edges loads ALL edges into Python then iterates to compute decay. DuckDB supports power(), date arithmetic, CASE WHEN. Fix: single SQL query with CASE per layer computing effective_confidence, WHERE filter.
close_reason: Replace Python-loop decay computation with single SQL CTE using power(), date_diff(), and CASE WHEN. Configurable half_life_days via dynamic CASE clauses. Filters soft-deleted edges. Adds regression test for custom half-life overrides.

title: N+1 queries in compute_voi observation counts
description: bayesian.py:2145 obs_counts dict comprehension calls get_observations() once per candidate node. Fix: single SQL SELECT node_id, COUNT(*) FROM ohm_observations WHERE node_id IN (?) AND deleted_at IS NULL GROUP BY node_id
close_reason: Replace N per-candidate get_observations() calls with single SQL GROUP BY node_id query using IN clause. Zero-observation nodes default via setdefault().

title: Add HTTP endpoint tests for granger, edge_stability, and policy
description: The three new GET endpoints (/granger, /edge_stability, /policy) have no server test coverage. Add TestInferenceHTTPEndpoints class to test_server.py covering: endpoint returns 200 with valid params, 400 on missing required params, correct response structure.
close_reason: Closed

description: The three new CLI subcommands (granger, edge-stability, policy) from recent sessions have no test_cli.py coverage. Add parametrized test_graph_args entries for each.

Acceptance: /policy?target=NODE&horizon=1 returns {action: observe|act, evpi, observation_cost, expected_utility}.

title: Migration: Convert observation source strings to source node references
description:
