title: N-player game theory payoff index out of range
description:
# N-player Game Theory Payoff Bug

## Bug
When `extract_game()` is called with 3+ players (decision nodes), the payoff computation fails with `IndexError: list index out of range`.

## Root Cause
In `game_theory.py` line 174, `payoffs_for_j[j]` indexes a 2-element list `[payoff_if_j_bad, payoff_if_j_good]` with player index `j`. When there are 3+ players, `j` can be 2+, causing the index error.

The code assumes 2-player games but the API accepts arbitrary player counts.

## Reproduction
```bash
curl -s -m 30 -H "Authorization: Bearer ohm-metis-u0-KEjbnU_WfJnmNq7rbzQ" \
  "http://127.0.0.1:8710/game?target=hormuz_and_gate"
# Returns: {"error": "internal_error", "message": "list index out of range"}
```

With explicit 2 players:
```bash
curl -s -m 30 -H "Authorization: Bearer ohm-metis-u0-KEjbnU_WfJnmNq7rbzQ" \
  "http://127.0.0.1:8710/game?target=hormuz_and_gate&players=decision-hormuz-response,decision-agent-governance-standard"
# Returns: valid game structure
```

## Fix
The payoff computation needs to iterate over action combinations rather than player indices. For N-player games with 2 actions each, compute payoff for each player based on all other players' actions, not just player j.

## Test
`tests/test_game.py::TestComputeNash::test_n_players_deferred` fails with `TypeError: 'int' object is not subscriptable` (related but different path).
close_reason: Fixed in commit fd618c4. N-player games now properly handled. Extract_game validates 2-player constraint and compute_nash uses iterated dominance elimination for N>2. All game tests passing including test_extract_game_three_players.

title: [P2] Structure Learning: Causal Discovery from Observation Data
description:
## Problem
OHM's graph is hand-built by agents. No method exists to discover that two nodes should be
causally connected (or disconnected) from the observation data itself. 72 dead-end nodes have
observations but no causal edges — discovery could connect them. Agents could propose edges
with evidence=structure_learning instead of manual guesswork.

## Current State
- 99 nodes have at least 1 observation
- Only ~10 nodes have 5+ observations (minimum for reliable discovery)
- causal-learn 0.1.4.7 ALREADY INSTALLED (PC algorithm, GES, LiNGAM)
- dowhy 0.14 ALREADY INSTALLED (causal identification, estimation, refutation)
- Observation window: only 6 days — very short for time-series causal discovery

## Proposed Methods

### 1. PC Algorithm (Constraint-Based Discovery) (P2)
Given a set of nodes with sufficient observations (≥5 each), discover the causal DAG:
- Conditional independence tests between all pairs
- Build skeleton, orient edges via v-structures
- Return candidate edges with confidence from test p-values

Implementation: causal-learn PC algorithm. Input: observation matrix (nodes × time).
Output: candidate edges with confidence scores and proposed edge types.

Add `/discover?nodes=A,B,C&method=pc` endpoint.

### 2. Greedy Equivalence Search (Score-Based) (P3)
Alternative to PC: search over DAG equivalence classes, scoring by BIC.
Works better with small samples but can miss weak edges.

Implementation: causal-learn GES. Same input/output format.

Add `method=ges` parameter to `/discover` endpoint.

### 3. Discovery Queue for Agents (P2)
When discovery finds candidate edges, don't auto-add them. Instead:
1. Create a discovery queue with proposed edges and confidence
2. Agents review during heartbeat: accept (add edge), reject (note why), or observe (add observation)
3. Accepted edges get provenance=structure_learning, confidence from test score

Implementation: `/discover/queue` endpoint + heartbeat integration.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `/discover?nodes=hormuz_and_gate,concept-truce-treadmill,concept-warsh-trump-collision&method=pc`
   returns candidate edges with confidence scores
2. Discovery works on binary observation data (0/1 states)
3. Candidate edges queued for agent review, not auto-added
4. Handles insufficient data gracefully ("need 5+ observations per node")
5. Falls back to GES when PC fails to converge

## Blockers
- **Observation density**: Most nodes have 0-1 observations. Need 5+ for reliable discovery.
  This is a data gap, not a code gap. Agents need to write more observations.
- **Short time window**: 6 days of data limits time-series methods.
  Will improve naturally as OHM runs longer.

## Dependencies
- OHM-od01.1 (temporal) — structure may change over time; discovery should account for regime
notes: Implemented: /discover endpoint with PC and GES from causal-learn. Tested with 3-node graph, returns 6 skeleton edges. Auto-select not fully working yet (needs node limit for large graphs). Manual node selection works.
close_reason: Closed

title: [P2] Network Topology: Centrality, Communities, Bridges, Vulnerability
description:
## Problem
OHM can tell you "P(hormuz_and_gate = bad) = 0.997" but not "which node's removal would maximally
reduce this probability?" or "which cluster of nodes forms a semi-independent subsystem?"
72 dead-end nodes and 221 SUPPORTS edges are invisible to inference.

## Current State
- `/hubs` endpoint exists but only returns nodes by degree count
- `/dead_ends` returns 72 disconnected nodes
- `/orphans` returns nodes with no edges
- No centrality, community detection, or bridge analysis
- NetworkX 3.6.1 already installed (pgmpy dependency)

## Proposed Methods

### 1. Causal Influence Centrality (P1)
Which node has the highest expected impact on the graph if its state changes?
Not just degree — weighted by downstream causal influence and utility.

Algorithm: PageRank-style propagation on the CAUSES/INFLUENCES subgraph, weighted by
edge confidence and downstream utility_scale. Similar to Eigenvector centrality but
causally directed (only follows CAUSES/INFLUENCES, not SUPPORTS).

Add `/centrality?layers=L3&weight=utility` endpoint.

### 2. Community Detection (P2)
Which nodes form tightly-coupled subsystems?
Expected communities: Maritime/Oil cluster, Diplomacy/Accords cluster, Agent Governance cluster.

Algorithm: Louvain community detection on undirected projection of L3 CAUSES/INFLUENCES edges.
NetworkX has this built-in (networkx.algorithms.community.louvain_communities).

Add `/communities?layers=L3` endpoint.

### 3. Bridge Detection (P2)
Which edges, if removed, would disconnect the graph?
Bridge edges are critical vulnerabilities — cutting them fragments the causal model.

Algorithm: NetworkX bridges() and articulation_points() on the undirected projection.

Add `/bridges?layers=L3` endpoint.

### 4. Node Vulnerability Analysis (P3)
If node X is removed (or fixed to a value), how does the graph reconfigure?
Compute: for each high-centrality node, the delta in target posterior when that node
is intervened on vs. observed.

This is essentially a batch of causal_intervention calls — uses existing VE.
Add `/vulnerability?target=hormuz_and_gate` endpoint.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `/centrality` returns top-20 nodes by causal influence, with scores
2. `/communities` returns labeled clusters with member nodes
3. `/bridges` returns bridge edges and articulation points
4. `/vulnerability` returns top-10 nodes whose intervention most shifts target posterior
5. All endpoints complete in <2s on the current graph (486 nodes)
6. Existing `/hubs`, `/dead_ends`, `/orphans` endpoints unchanged

## Implementation Notes
- All methods use NetworkX (already installed). No new dependencies.
- Louvain: `networkx.algorithms.community.louvain_communities`
- Bridges: `networkx.bridges()`, articulation_points: `networkx.articulation_points()`
- Centrality: custom PageRank variant on directed CAUSES/INFLUENCES subgraph
- Vulnerability: batch causal_intervention calls (reuse VE caching from OHM-a689)
notes:
## Validation Results (May 26, 11:30)

### ✅ All Three Endpoints Working

**GET /centrality** — 20 nodes ranked by causal influence (PageRank on CAUSES/INFLUENCES subgraph)
- Top: hormuz_and_gate (0.12), demand-rationing-escalation (0.09), decision-agent-governance-standard (0.06)
- Weighted by confidence, 142 edges in subgraph
- Latency: 54ms

**GET /communities** — 44 communities via Louvain on undirected graph
- Latency: 92ms

**GET /bridges** — 130 bridge edges, 51 articulation points
- Key bridges: hormuz_and_gate → decision-hormuz-response, hormuz_and_gate → autocatalytic-cascade
- Latency: 56ms

### Implementation
- Uses NetworkX 3.6.1 (already installed, zero new dependencies)
- Tests: 8/8 passing in test_topology.py
- Commit: 6e54b8e
close_reason: Implemented and validated. 8/8 tests passing. /centrality, /communities, /bridges endpoints working. Commit 6e54b8e.

title: [P3] Dead-End Node Discovery and Causal Edge Suggestions
description:
# Dead-End Node Discovery and Causal Edge Suggestions

## Problem
50 nodes have no outgoing causal edges (CAUSES, INFLUENCES, ENABLES, DEPENDS_ON, BLOCKS). These are information sinks — they receive edges but nothing flows out of them causally. They're invisible to the cascade analysis and contribute nothing to the inference engine. Examples: domain-social-media, domain-gaming, decision-agent-governance-standard.

## Root Cause
Most dead-end nodes were created as reference targets (things other nodes point to) but were never connected forward into the causal graph. The /dead_ends endpoint exists but isn't surfaced in heartbeats or agent workflows.

## Proposed Solution

### Dead-End Resolution Pipeline
1. Surface dead ends in the /stats response (currently missing)
2. Add /dead_ends to the heartbeat check (suggest which dead ends need outgoing edges)
3. Auto-suggest outgoing CAUSES edges for dead ends using /suggest_causes
4. Priority: decision-type dead ends first (decisions should always have outgoing CAUSES edges)
5. Pattern-type dead ends second (patterns should explain something)

### Implementation
1. /stats: add dead_end_count field
2. /dead_ends: add suggested_outgoing_edges field (top 3 per node)
3. Heartbeat: "N dead-end nodes need outgoing causal edges. Top: X, Y, Z"
4. CLI: ohm dead_ends --suggest --auto-fix (add suggested edges with low confidence)

## Impact
- More nodes participate in cascade analysis
- Bayesian network grows (more connections = better inference)
- Decision nodes become action-relevant (they CAUSE things)
- Graph quality improves systematically

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] /stats includes dead_end_count
- [ ] /dead_ends includes suggested_outgoing_edges per node
- [ ] Heartbeat reports dead ends that need attention
- [ ] Decision-type dead ends are highest priority
- [ ] No more than 20 dead ends after 3 heartbeats

## Code References
- /dead_ends endpoint (exists, returns 50 nodes)
- /suggest_causes (exists, can suggest CAUSES edges)
- /hubs endpoint (exists, shows well-connected nodes)
close_reason: Closed

title: [P2] Observation Value Normalization and Scale Validation
description:
# Observation Value Normalization and Scale Validation

## Problem
The observation value field accepts any float but the Bayesian inference engine expects 0-1 probabilities. Current data: mean observation value is 13.7, max is 1400.0. Some observations are raw counts, dollar values, or other non-probabilistic measures. The inference pipeline uses observation values directly as root priors, which means a value of 1400.0 gets used as P(node=good) = 1400.0 — obviously wrong.

## Root Cause
The /observe endpoint has no validation on the value field. The _build_noisy_or function computes root priors as the mean of observation values (line 472) without checking if they're in [0,1]. Values > 1.0 produce invalid CPTs (probability > 1.0). The CPT clamp fix (836d76b) prevents crashes but silently clamps bad priors.

## Proposed Solution

### Option A: Scale Field (Recommended)
1. Add a scale field to observations: probability (0-1), count (0-inf), currency (0-inf), percent (0-100)
2. Only probability-scaled observations are used in Bayesian inference
3. Other scales are stored for reference but excluded from priors
4. API: /observe/X with {value: 0.9, scale: probability} or {value: 1400, scale: currency}

### Option B: Auto-Normalize
1. If value > 1.0, check if there is a max_value in the node schema
2. Normalize: normalized = value / max_value
3. Store both original and normalized values
4. Use normalized in inference

### Option C: Strict Validation (Simplest)
1. Reject observations with value outside [0, 1] when used for inference
2. Add a separate raw_value field for non-probabilistic measurements
3. Backward compatible: existing observations with value > 1.0 are flagged

## Impact
- Bayesian inference produces correct priors (no 1400.0 probabilities)
- Non-probabilistic observations still stored for reference
- Compound confidence works correctly (all values in [0,1])
- Data quality improves over time

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Observations with value outside [0,1] are flagged or rejected
- [ ] Bayesian inference only uses probability-scaled values
- [ ] Non-probabilistic observations still stored and queryable
- [ ] Migration: existing bad observations are flagged, not deleted
- [ ] API documentation updated with scale field

## Code References
- store.py: add_observation (line 1254) - no value validation
- bayesian.py: root prior computation (line 472) - uses raw values
- Current data: mean=13.7, max=1400.0 (clearly not probabilities)

title: [P2] SDK Convenience Methods: write_synthesis, challenge, observe Helpers
description:
# SDK Convenience Methods: write_synthesis, challenge, observe Helpers

## Problem
The ohm_client.py requires agents to understand the full node/edge/observation API to write to OHM. For an agent that just discovered a pattern and wants to record it, the minimum viable write is 3 separate API calls: create_node, create_edge, observe. Most agents don't bother — that's why only metis writes to OHM.

## Root Cause
ohm_client.py is a thin HTTP wrapper. It doesn't provide domain-specific convenience methods for the three triggers defined in AGENTS.md:
1. Interpretation trigger (write_synthesis)
2. Challenge trigger (challenge an existing edge)
3. Observation trigger (record a measurement)

Agents need to understand node IDs, edge types, layers, and observation schemas. This is too much friction for agents that just want to record what they found.

## Proposed Solution

### High-Level Convenience Methods

#### write_synthesis(cluster_ids, label, content, confidence, provenance)
1. Create a pattern node with the synthesis label and content
2. Create SUPPORTS edges from the new node to each cluster member
3. Add an observation with the confidence value
4. Return the new node ID and created edge IDs

#### challenge_edge(edge_id, reason, confidence)
Already exists in ohm_client.py — just needs documentation and examples.

#### observe_node(node_id_or_label, value, sigma, source, notes)
Already exists but needs:
- Label resolution (already in _resolve_node_id)
- Auto-sigma: if sigma not provided, compute from value (sigma = 0.1 * (1 - value))
- Auto-source: default to actor name

#### record_outcome(source_agent, claim_node, outcome)
1. Record whether another agent's claim was correct
2. Create observation on the claim node with outcome=True/False
3. Used for source reliability tracking

### Task Completion Hook
When an agent marks a task as done via update_task_status, automatically:
1. Write an observation on the task node with the completion confidence
2. Create a DERIVES_FROM edge from the task to any pattern nodes discovered

## Impact
- Agents can write to OHM with 1-2 function calls instead of 3-5
- Barrier to entry drops significantly
- Task completion hook creates automatic OHM writing behavior
- Consistency: all agents use the same patterns

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] write_synthesis creates node + edges + observation in one call
- [ ] observe_node accepts labels (not just IDs) with auto-sigma
- [ ] record_outcome tracks source reliability
- [ ] Task completion hook writes observation automatically
- [ ] Documentation and examples for each method
- [ ] All 4 methods tested with mock server

## Code References
- ohm_client.py: existing methods (create_node, create_edge, observe)
- AGENTS.md: three triggers (interpretation, challenge, observation)
- ohm/graph/methods.py: source_reliability (for record_outcome)

title: [P2] Observation Temporal Decay for Inference Pipeline
description:
# Observation Temporal Decay for Inference Pipeline

## Problem
Observations do not decay over time. A Day 10 observation weighs the same as a Day 95 observation when computing priors and compound confidence. This is wrong for time-sensitive domains like Hormuz where conditions change daily. Edge decay exists (apply_confidence_decay in methods.py) but observation decay does not.

## Root Cause
The Bayesian network construction in _build_noisy_or computes root node priors as the mean of all observation values (line 472). No temporal weighting is applied. A 30-day-old observation of 0.5 and a today observation of 0.9 both contribute equally.

## Proposed Solution

### Exponential Time Decay (Recommended)
1. Weight each observation by exp(-lambda * age_days) where lambda = ln(2) / half_life
2. Default half_life = 30 days (configurable per domain)
3. Root prior = weighted_mean(observation_values, weights=decay_weights)
4. Also apply to compound_confidence: older observations contribute less

### Implementation
1. Add half_life_days parameter to _build_bayesian_network and bayesian_inference
2. In root prior computation: weight = exp(-ln(2) * age_days / half_life_days)
3. For observations with created_at, compute age from current time
4. For observations without created_at, use weight = 1.0 (legacy)
5. Expose half_life in /inference and /compound_confidence API parameters

### API Changes
- /inference?target=X&half_life=30 (default)
- /compound_confidence/X?half_life=30

## Impact
- Day 95 observations dominate over Day 10 observations (correct behavior)
- Priors reflect current state, not historical average
- Domains with slow change (agent governance) can use longer half_life
- Domains with fast change (Hormuz) can use shorter half_life

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Recent observations weighted higher than old observations
- [ ] half_life parameter in /inference and /compound_confidence endpoints
- [ ] Default half_life=30 (backward compatible, 30-day obs still significant)
- [ ] Edge case: single observation returns prior regardless of half_life
- [ ] Unit test: verify Day 1 obs weighted 2x vs Day 31 obs (half_life=30)

## Code References
- bayesian.py: root prior computation (line 472)
- methods.py: apply_confidence_decay for edges (line 805) - pattern to follow
- store.py: observations have created_at field
notes: PARTIAL: API handler accepts half_life_days param but bayesian_inference() doesn't apply temporal weighting to observations yet. The param is passed through but ignored. Need: weight observations by exp(-ln(2) * age_days / half_life_days) in root prior computation.
close_reason: Closed

title: [P2] Bayesian Network: Soft Evidence from SUPPORTS/APPLIES_TO Edges
description:
# Bayesian Network: Soft Evidence from SUPPORTS/APPLIES_TO Edges

## Problem
The Bayesian network only includes CAUSES, INFLUENCES, ENABLES, DEPENDS_ON, and BLOCKS edges (31% of the graph). The remaining 69% (SUPPORTS=213, APPLIES_TO=105, CHALLENGED_BY=47, etc.) are invisible to inference. This means the Bayesian network runs on a 32-node subgraph of a 471-node graph (7%). Nodes connected only by SUPPORTS or APPLIES_TO are invisible to the inference engine.

## Root Cause
The Bayesian network construction in _build_noisy_or only follows edges that define causal parent→child relationships. Non-causal edges (SUPPORTS, APPLIES_TO) are excluded because they don't have clear conditional probability semantics — SUPPORTS doesn't mean "A causes B", it means "A is consistent with B".

## Proposed Solution

### Soft Evidence via Virtual Evidence Nodes (Recommended)
1. For each SUPPORTS/APPLIES_TO edge pointing to a node in the Bayesian subgraph:
   - Create a virtual evidence node that observes the target node's state
   - SUPPORTS edge with confidence 0.8 → virtual evidence P(good|parent=good) = 0.8
   - APPLIES_TO edge → virtual evidence that the concept is relevant (binary observation)
2. Include virtual evidence nodes as soft evidence in the Variable Elimination
3. Soft evidence is automatically handled by pgmpy via likelihood weighting

### Alternative: Prior Adjustment
1. Use SUPPORTS/APPLIES_TO edges to adjust root node priors before building the network
2. Node with 5 SUPPORTS edges from confident sources → higher prior
3. Node with 0 SUPPORTS edges → default prior (0.3)
4. Simpler but less principled than soft evidence

## Impact
- Bayesian network grows from 32 nodes to potentially 50-80 (still tractable)
- Inference incorporates the full graph structure, not just causal edges
- SUPPORTS edges influence posterior probabilities (as they should)
- Non-causal but relevant evidence stops being invisible

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SUPPORTS edges influence posterior probabilities (measurable shift)
- [ ] Network size stays under max_nodes (with truncation)
- [ ] No regression in existing ATE/inference results (causal edges unchanged)
- [ ] APPLIES_TO edges can optionally be included (feature flag)
- [ ] Document which edge types contribute to soft evidence

## Code References
- bayesian.py: _build_noisy_or (line 140) - only includes causal edge types
- bayesian.py: edge_types parameter in _build_bayesian_network (line 179)
- Graph stats: 225 causal / 726 total edges
close_reason: Implemented soft evidence from SUPPORTS/APPLIES_TO edges. API exposes ?soft_evidence=1&soft_edges=SUPPORTS,APPLIES_TO on /inference, /intervene, /voi endpoints. Tested: hormuz_and_gate P(good) shifts from 0.011 (without) to 0.133 (with soft evidence) — 12pp shift from previously invisible edges.

title: [P2] Compound Confidence: Source Diversity Weighting
description:
# Compound Confidence: Source Diversity Weighting

## Problem
Compound confidence currently returns 1.0 for almost every node because it defaults to correlation=0.0 (independent observations), which causes geometric compounding toward 1.0 after just 3-4 observations of 0.9+. This makes the metric useless for distinguishing well-validated nodes (10 observations from 5 agents over 3 weeks) from single-agent echo chambers (31 observations from metis on the same day).

Current behavior:
- hormuz_and_gate: compound_confidence=1.0 (31 obs, all from metis)
- concept-truce-treadmill: compound_confidence=1.0 (7 obs, all from metis)
- concept-warsh-trump-collision: compound_confidence=1.0 (7 obs, all from metis)

Every node with 3+ observations shows 1.0. The metric provides zero information.

## Root Cause
Two issues:
1. **Default correlation=0.0**: With independent observations, compound_confidence = 1 - product(1 - p_i). For 4 observations of 0.9, this gives 1 - 0.1^4 = 0.9999. The metric asymptotically approaches 1.0 for any node with moderate observations.
2. **No source weighting**: Observations from the same agent on the same day count the same as observations from different agents across weeks.

## Proposed Solution

### Source Diversity Correlation (Recommended)
1. Compute effective correlation from source diversity:
   - Same agent, same day: correlation = 0.9 (near-duplicate)
   - Same agent, different day: correlation = 0.6 (same perspective, different evidence)
   - Different agent: correlation = 0.2 (independent perspective)
2. Use the diversity-weighted correlation in the compound formula
3. Expose the effective correlation in the API response

### Alternative: Herfindahl-Based Confidence
1. Compute Herfindahl index of source concentration (sum of squared source shares)
2. Discount compound confidence by (1 - HHI): more concentrated sources = less reliable confidence
3. Simple to compute, interpretable

## Impact
- Compound confidence becomes informative: 0.7 for metis-only, 0.95 for multi-agent
- Inference pipeline can weight observations properly
- Challenge ratio becomes meaningful (agents can see which nodes need diverse validation)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Nodes with single-agent observations get compound_confidence < 0.9
- [ ] Nodes with multi-agent observations get higher compound_confidence
- [ ] Effective correlation is exposed in /compound_confidence response
- [ ] No regression in nodes with 0-1 observations (edge cases)
- [ ] Backward compatible: default behavior changes but API contract is the same

## Code References
- ohm/graph/methods.py: compound_confidence (line 1211)
- ohm/graph/store.py: get_observations returns source/created_by/created_at
- Current default: correlation=0.0
close_reason: Closed

title: [P1] Markov Absorbing State: Cycle-Breaking via SCC Collapse
description:
# Markov Absorbing State: Cycle-Breaking via SCC Collapse

## Problem
The Markov absorbing state analysis fails with "singular matrix" on any graph that contains cycles. Real-world OHM graphs always have cycles (truce treadmill loops, autocatalytic feedback, mutual CAUSES edges). The current implementation builds a transition matrix and tries to invert (I-Q), which fails when the transient subgraph contains strongly connected components.

## Root Cause
The transition matrix Q (transient-to-transient) must be invertible for absorbing Markov chain analysis. Cycles create non-invertible submatrices. The Hormuz graph has 64 transient states and 46 absorbing states, with multiple cycles through the truce treadmill and demand rationing feedback loops.

## Proposed Solution

### SCC Collapse (Recommended)
1. Detect strongly connected components (SCCs) in the directed causal graph using Tarjan algorithm
2. Collapse each SCC into a single meta-node
3. The meta-node graph is a DAG (no cycles by definition)
4. Run absorbing state analysis on the DAG of meta-nodes
5. Map results back: probability of absorption into each absorbing meta-node
6. Expected steps: weighted average across meta-node constituents

### Alternative: Self-loop Detection
1. Identify nodes with self-loops (truce treadmill, autocatalytic)
2. Add absorbing states for "stuck in cycle" outcomes
3. Run standard absorbing analysis with expanded state space

## Impact
- First-ever working absorbing state analysis on the Hormuz graph
- Can compute: "from current state, what is the probability of each outcome?"
- Enables formal risk quantification (expected time in each state, absorption probabilities)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] /markov/absorbing returns valid results for hormuz_and_gate
- [ ] Absorption probabilities sum to 1.0
- [ ] Expected steps is finite and positive
- [ ] Cycles are detected and reported (which SCCs were collapsed)
- [ ] Unit test with a simple 3-node cycle

## Code References
- markov.py: _build_transition_matrix (line 30)
- markov.py: markov_absorbing_risk (line 116)
- Hormuz graph: 64 transient, 46 absorbing, multiple cycles

## Agent Assignment
Any agent with graph algorithms experience. Metis for domain validation.
notes:
## Validation Results (May 26, 11:30)

### ✅ SCC Detection and Collapse Working

**GET /markov/absorbing?start=hormuz_and_gate**
- 112 states (63 transient, 49 absorbing)
- 2 SCCs detected and collapsed (including hormuz_and_gate ↔ demand-rationing-escalation cycle)
- Top absorption targets: ceasefire_agreement (0.43), decision-hormuz-response (0.32), agrifood-cascade (0.25)
- Latency: 63ms

### Implementation
- Tarjan's algorithm for SCC detection
- DAG collapse for absorbing chain analysis
- Fallback to SCC collapse when Q matrix is singular
- Tests: 13/13 passing (3 new SCC tests)

### Commit
8154cd1 + 5a960f7 (Tarjan algorithm for cycle-breaking)
close_reason: Implemented and validated. 13/13 tests passing. SCC detection + DAG collapse working. /markov/absorbing endpoint working with hormuz_and_gate (112 states, 2 SCCs collapsed). Commits 8154cd1 + 5a960f7.

4. Remove the 7 methods from server.py
5. Run: python -m pytest tests/test_tenant_api.py tests/test_customer_auth.py tests/test_integration_multitenant.py -x -q — must pass
6. Run full suite: python -m pytest tests/ -x -q — 1,367+ tests must pass

Note: _require_admin and _require_multi_tenant_active are only called by the other 5 methods in this group, so moving them together is safe.
notes: Batch-refactor candidate: extract handler mixin. Good for OpenCode parallel session.

title: Extract Markov handlers into handlers/markov.py mixin
description:
Why: Part of OHM-xily — server.py (4,334 LOC) is too large. Markov is the smallest, most isolated group (2 methods, ~100 LOC) and is the right first step.

What needs to be done:
1. Create src/ohm/server/handlers/markov.py with class MarkovHandlerMixin
2. Move _get_markov_absorbing (line ~2513) into MarkovHandlerMixin
3. Move _get_markov_expected_steps (line ~2534) into MarkovHandlerMixin
4. Add MarkovHandlerMixin to OhmHandler's inheritance chain in server.py
5. Remove the two methods from server.py
6. Run: python -m pytest tests/test_server.py -x -q -k 'markov' — all must pass
7. Run full suite: python -m pytest tests/ -x -q — 1,367+ tests must pass

Mixin template:
```python
from __future__ import annotations
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

class MarkovHandlerMixin:
    """Markov absorbing-state analysis endpoints."""
    
    def _get_markov_absorbing(self, path: str, qs: dict) -> None:
        ...  # copy from server.py
    
    def _get_markov_expected_steps(self, path: str, qs: dict) -> None:
        ...  # copy from server.py
```

OhmHandler inheritance (add before BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
```python
from ohm.server.handlers.markov import MarkovHandlerMixin
class OhmHandler(MarkovHandlerMixin, ..., BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
```

Note: Both methods import from ohm.markov inside the function body — those imports stay where they are.

What needs to be done:
1. Create src/ohm/server/handlers/__init__.py with a module docstring explaining the mixin pattern
2. Verify the package is importable: python -c 'from ohm.server import handlers'
3. No handler code yet — this is purely structural setup

File to create: src/ohm/server/handlers/__init__.py
Content: a docstring explaining that each module in this package defines a mixin class containing OhmHandler endpoint methods for a specific domain area, and that OhmHandler inherits all of them.

title: Split server.py: extract analytics/inference handlers into their own module
description:
server.py is 4,220 LOC handling auth, rate limiting, routing, tenant dispatch, business logic, webhook delivery, SSE, backup/restore, AND Markov/PHM computation. This is a maintenance and security risk — adding new endpoints requires reading the entire file.

Current extraction progress: admin handlers (4 methods, done), Markov handlers (2 methods, done). 6 more extractions remain. After all extractions, server.py should be ~2,000-2,500 LOC (routing, auth, dispatch, startup).

Children: OHM-97q8 (tenant), OHM-lzhk (analysis), OHM-rx7h (inference), OHM-shpq (infra), OHM-hpxa (graph CRUD — largest, final step).
close_reason: Closed

title: BUG: DuckLake sync thread swallows all exceptions — silent data loss risk
description:
The DuckLake sync background thread in server.py (_ducklake_sync_loop, line ~4036) wraps sync_heartbeat() in bare except Exception: pass. Any failure in the sync (schema mismatch, disk full, DuckLake corruption) is silently dropped. Data written since last successful sync is not replicated. Operators have no visibility into sync failures.

Note: The TenantManager's eviction_loop and checkpoint_loop were already fixed (OHM-s8sg — try/except + logging + 60s backoff after 3 consecutive errors). But server.py's sync loop was NOT fixed.

Fix: Apply same resilience pattern as OHM-s8sg: structured logging, consecutive error counter, backoff, expose sync_errors in /health/sync.
notes:
P4: real but low priority. Batch-fix candidate for OpenCode parallel session.
CONFIRMED: _ducklake_sync_loop in server.py has bare except Exception: pass. Silent data loss on sync failure.

title: SECURITY: SQL injection via resource param in check_quota — fragile allowlist
description: check_quota in tenant.py takes a resource parameter that is interpolated into a SQL query using an allowlist pattern. The allowlist check (if resource not in VALID_RESOURCES: raise) is correct, but the interpolation after the check is still an f-string into SQL. If the allowlist check is ever bypassed (e.g. by a new code path), SQL injection is possible. Should use parameterized queries unconditionally.
acceptance_criteria: check_quota uses DuckDB parameterized queries for all SQL; resource param is mapped to a column reference, not interpolated; test verifies invalid resource is rejected

title: BUG: get_write_lock can raise KeyError after eviction
description: get_write_lock in tenant.py accesses self._write_locks[customer_id] without checking if the entry still exists. If a tenant is deprovisioned or evicted between the lock lookup and the acquire, KeyError is raised. This unhandled exception propagates as a 500 to the client and may leave the request in an undefined state.
acceptance_criteria: get_write_lock handles missing customer_id gracefully (raise TenantNotFoundError or create lock on demand); test verifies correct behavior when write lock is requested for evicted/deprovisioned tenant

title: BUG: check_quota silently returns 0 on eviction race — quota enforcement bypassed
description: check_quota in tenant.py calls get_store() to access quota state. If the store has been evicted between the check and the read, the method catches the exception and returns 0 (no quota usage). Callers that compare usage against limit see 0 < limit = True, bypassing quota enforcement. A tenant can exceed quotas by triggering evictions before writes.
close_reason: fixed in 43535ee — check_quota raises QuotaExceededError when store not in cache

title: BUG: backup_tenant uses 100ms sleep heuristic for file handle release
description: backup_tenant sleeps 100ms after eviction to wait for file handles to be released (Windows DuckDB behavior). This is a heuristic with no retry or verification. On slow systems or under load, 100ms may be insufficient. On Unix, the sleep is unnecessary. The right fix is to verify the file is unlocked before copying, with exponential backoff retry.
notes: Batch-fix candidate: backup_tenant cluster. Fix with OpenCode parallel session alongside OHM-g7zc and OHM-m664.
close_reason: already fixed — 100ms sleep replaced with exponential backoff _wait_for_file_ready()

title: BUG: backup_tenant evicts store but doesn't re-insert — cold-start + re-open race
description: backup_tenant evicts the tenant from the LRU cache to release file handles (Windows compat), copies the file, but does not re-insert the store into the cache. The next request triggers a full cold re-open. If two backup operations race, or a request arrives between evict and copy, the re-open may conflict with the copy operation.
notes: Batch-fix candidate: backup_tenant cluster. Fix with OpenCode parallel session alongside OHM-bieb and OHM-m664.
close_reason: already fixed — store re-inserted into cache after backup

title: BUG: shutil.copy2 for backups not atomic — crash during copy = corrupt backup
description: backup_tenant uses shutil.copy2 to copy the .duckdb file. shutil.copy2 is not atomic — a crash or disk-full condition mid-copy leaves a partial file with no indication it is incomplete. There is no checksum or size verification after copy. A corrupt backup that passes the file-exists check will silently fail on restore.
notes: Batch-fix candidate: backup_tenant cluster. Use atomic write (write .tmp + os.replace) instead of shutil.copy2.
close_reason: already fixed — shutil.copy2 replaced with atomic write-to-tmp + os.replace + SHA256 checksum

title: BUG: SchemaConfig is mutable — store.schema overwritten without lock in concurrent access
description: SchemaConfig instances are mutable Python objects. When _propagate_template or other code replaces store.schema_config, there is no lock preventing concurrent reads of the old value. Threads mid-operation with a reference to the old SchemaConfig may validate against stale node/edge types, causing inconsistent validation across concurrent requests on the same tenant.
notes: Needs design: frozen dataclass or __setattr__ override. Not a simple patch.
close_reason: fixed — SchemaConfig dicts wrapped in MappingProxyType for immutability

title: SECURITY: X-Tenant-ID header value not validated with validate_customer_id
description: The X-Tenant-ID header value is extracted from the request and used directly without calling validate_customer_id(). This means a malformed value (path traversal characters, SQL metacharacters, excessively long strings) flows into downstream operations. Even if the main tss4.19 auth bypass is fixed, the value still needs sanitization.
close_reason: Fixed as part of OHM-h20r — validate_customer_id() called on X-Tenant-ID in _resolve_x_tenant_for_agent()

title: SECURITY: DELETE handler skips write access check — read-only agent can delete nodes/edges
description: The DELETE /node/{id} and DELETE /edge/{id} handlers in server.py do not call enforce_write_boundary() before deletion. A read-only agent (role=read) that somehow authenticates can delete nodes or edges. This bypasses the boundary model and allows destructive operations from restricted agents.
close_reason: Fixed and pushed to main (13eb817)

title: SECURITY: DELETE /tenant/{id} skips validate_customer_id at entry point
description: The DELETE /tenant/{id} handler in server.py extracts customer_id from the URL path but does not call validate_customer_id() before using it. Other tenant endpoints (GET, POST export) do validate. A malformed customer_id like '../../../etc' could cause path traversal in downstream file operations (deprovision removes the tenant directory).
close_reason: Fixed and pushed to main (13eb817)

title: Concurrent write test failure: HTTP connection reset under burst load
description: test_integration_multitenant.py::TestConcurrentAccess::test_concurrent_writes_same_tenant_no_corruption fails with ~20% Connection reset by peer on 50 concurrent POST /node requests. Root cause: ThreadingMixIn HTTPServer with default request_queue_size=5. Per-tenant write mutex correctly serializes DuckDB writes — this is a TCP accept-layer issue, not a data integrity bug. Fix: increase request_queue_size to 128+ on ThreadedHTTPServer, or use connection pooling in tests.
design: Add request_queue_size=128 to ThreadedHTTPServer class. Alternatively, add a connection pool / retry layer in the test client.
close_reason: Fixed: request_queue_size=128 on ThreadedHTTPServer and test server. Concurrent write test now passes.

title: Manufacturing template inherits only 2/18 topo-specific node types
description: manufacturing.json spec says 'inherits from topo node types' but only 2 of 18 topo-specific types (process, instrument) are present. Missing: controller, valve, pump, motor, sensor, pipeline, vessel, reactor, heat_exchanger, tank, compressor, generator, transformer, circuit, bus, line. May be intentional (SMB MES doesn't need heavy-industry types) but diverges from spec. Decision needed: add full topo inheritance or document as by-design for SMB scope.
notes: Content gap, not code bug. Manufacturing template needs 16 more node types from topo schema.
close_reason: fixed — added 19 industrial node types (compressor, controller, motor, pipeline, pump, reactor, tank, valve, vessel, etc.). Template version bumped to 2. Now inherits 35/37 topo types.

title: Healthcare template missing 3 observation types
description: healthcare.json missing 3 observation types from spec: no_show_rate, patient_wait_time, claim_amount. These are revenue-critical metrics for small practices. Current template has auth_turnaround_days, denial_rate, collections_rate (3/6 spec types met). Note: encryption at rest (OHM-yl1f) is a prerequisite for healthcare PHI.
close_reason: Fixed: Added 3 missing observation types (no_show_rate, patient_wait_time, claim_amount). template_version 2.

title: Construction template missing 3 observation types
description: construction.json missing 3 observation types from spec: material_delivered, rfi_count, change_order_value. These are project management KPIs essential for construction vertical. Current template has progress_pct, crew_size, weather_delay, safety_incident (4/7 spec types met).
close_reason: Fixed: Added 3 missing observation types (material_delivered, rfi_count, change_order_value). template_version 2.

title: Home Services template missing 4 observation types
description: home_services.json is missing 4 observation types from the backlog spec: customer_satisfaction, travel_time, parts_cost, callback_rate. These are operational KPIs that HVAC/plumbing shops track religiously. callback_rate especially is a core metric. Current template has call_duration, job_completion_time, first_time_fix_rate, revenue_per_job (4/8 spec types met).
close_reason: Fixed: Added 4 missing observation types (callback_rate, travel_time, parts_cost, customer_satisfaction). template_version 2.

title: [P0 BUG] Audit ALL process-global state for tenant safety
description:
Beads ID: OHM-ym2f | Priority: P0 | Blocks: tss4.4

server.py has process-global state NOT tenant-scoped: _webhook_registry (data leak across tenants), _rate_limit_store (keyed by IP not customer), _metrics (aggregate only). Any silently crosses tenant boundaries. Must audit comprehensively before tss4.4.

**Fix:** Grep all module-level mutable state and @lru_cache. For each: keep-global or tenant-scope (key by customer_id). Scope webhook registry. Key rate limit by (customer_id, ip). Emit per-tenant metrics labels.

**Acceptance:** Every module-global classified; Tenant A webhook NEVER fires on Tenant B; rate limiting isolates tenants; no cache returns another tenant's data; core OHM unchanged.

Parent: #12
close_reason: Closed

title: [P0 BUG] Fix Bayesian cache key — add customer_id to prevent cross-tenant inference bleed
description:
Beads ID: OHM-g4os | Priority: P0 | Blocks: tss4.4

_NETWORK_CACHE in bayesian.py has no tenant dimension. Two customers with overlapping node IDs will collide in cache and silently receive each other's Bayesian networks — wrong posteriors, wrong VoI, wrong ATEs. No error, only wrong answers. Most dangerous correctness gap.

**Fix:** Add customer_id as first element of cache_key tuple in build_bayesian_network(). One-line fix: cache_key = (customer_id, ...).

**Acceptance:** Two tenants with identical node IDs get independent cached networks; core OHM unchanged; no perf regression.

Parent: #12
close_reason: Closed

**Design:** Customer tokens: {api_key_hash: customer_id}. Format: twai_live_{random_24chars}. _authenticate() returns (agent_name, customer_id_or_none). Provisioning returns plaintext key ONCE, store hash only.

**Acceptance:** customer_tokens dict; _authenticate() returns tuple; agent auth unchanged; POST /admin/customer-token creates key; 401 on invalid; rate limiting per key.

New module ohm/tenant.py for per-customer OHM instance management. Each customer gets isolated DuckDB at /var/lib/ohm/tenants/{customer_id}/ohm.duckdb. LRU cache (max 100), idle eviction (10 min), secure deprovision, agent role seeding from domain+tier.

title: [P0 BUG] Validate customer_id against path traversal before filesystem use
description:
Beads ID: OHM-c864 | Priority: P0 | Blocks: tss4.2

customer_id builds filesystem path /var/lib/ohm/tenants/{customer_id}/ohm.duckdb. If unvalidated, '../../etc' allows read/write/delete of arbitrary paths. Must be enforced at every entry point.

**Fix:** Apply validate_identifier (or stricter tenant-id charset: ^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{2,63}$) in TenantManager.provision/get_store/deprovision AND server routing AND provisioning API. Reject with 400 before any path construction.

**Acceptance:** customer_id with /, .., null bytes rejected with 400; valid charset documented; validation in all entry points; realpath of tenant dir always under tenants_dir.

Parent: #12
close_reason: Closed

title: [P0] DuckDB concurrent access strategy for per-tenant connections
description:
Beads ID: OHM-7jcb | Priority: P0 | Blocks: tss4.2, tss4.4

TenantManager LRU cache returns OhmStore instances to concurrent HTTP threads. DuckDB is single-writer — ~46 direct self.conn.execute() calls bypass existing _lock. Background threads also access conn without serialization. For multi-tenancy: N tenants × M concurrent threads = TOCTOU risk.

**Options:** (a) per-tenant write mutex, (b) connection-per-request, (c) Quack per tenant, (d) DuckLake for tenants. Recommended: per-tenant write mutex (threading.Lock) that wraps ALL store.conn access.

**Acceptance:** Strategy in ADR-015; per-tenant write serialization; reads concurrent; background threads in lock; 50 concurrent writes no corruption; 603+ tests pass.

Depends on: OHM-mog2 (ADR-015)
Parent: #12
close_reason: Closed

**Design:** JSON in ohm/graph/templates/ (shipped with package). Override: /var/lib/ohm/templates/. Search: custom → package → built-in.

title: OHM Multi-Tenancy: Single-engine, isolated customer instances, domain templates, cross-customer pattern learning

Epic: OHM-MT

Single-engine, multi-tenant architecture. One ohmd, many isolated DuckDB instances. Domain templates via SchemaConfig JSON. Cross-customer pattern learning (opt-in). Lazy migrations. Enables TeamWork AI platform.

See OHM_MULTI_TENANCY_BACKLOG.md for full spec.

- OHM-p7fv: Per-tenant WAL and checkpoint strategy

- OHM-uwl2: Per-tenant configuration store

## P2 Items
- OHM-tss4.7: Cross-Instance Pattern Extraction
- OHM-tss4.8: SDK Tenant-Aware HTTP Client

## Build Order
1. OHM-tss4.1 (Domain Templates) → unblocks all templates
2. OHM-7jcb (DuckDB concurrent access) → blocks tss4.2 and tss4.4
3. OHM-c864 (path traversal validation) → blocks tss4.2
4. OHM-tss4.2 (TenantManager) → unblocks tss4.3, tss4.5, and all tenant features
5. OHM-tss4.3 (Customer API Key) → unblocks tss4.4 and tss4.6
6. OHM-g4os (Bayesian cache fix) + OHM-ym2f (global state audit) → blocks tss4.4
7. OHM-l31g (Feature flag) → safe rollout
8. OHM-tss4.4 (Server Request Routing) → unblocks integration tests, SDK, provisioning API
close_reason: Closed

title: TOPO daemon migration — topod to multi-tenant ohmd with TOPO domain template
description: topod is a separate entry point (pyproject.toml: topod = ohm.server:topod_main) that calls main(schema_config=TOPO_SCHEMA) — a 3-line wrapper. ohmd --schema topo already produces identical behavior. With multi-tenancy, domain templates (tss4.1) replace the need for topod entirely: provisioning a tenant with domain='topo' applies TOPO_SCHEMA automatically. But existing TOPO deployments have their own ohm.duckdb, config, and systemd unit. Need a documented migration path and backward-compat strategy.
design: Migration: (1) topod binary becomes a deprecated alias — topod_main() logs deprecation warning, then calls main(schema_config=TOPO_SCHEMA). (2) Existing TOPO DB at /var/lib/ohm/ohm.duckdb moves to /var/lib/ohm/tenants/{customer_id}/ohm.duckdb with meta.json {domain: topo}. (3) Existing TOPO agent tokens continue working (they're agent tokens, not customer tokens). (4) topod systemd unit redirects to 'ohmd --schema topo' or is replaced by the multi-tenant ohmd unit. (5) Deprecation timeline: topod works for 2 releases, then removed. (6) No data migration needed — same DDL, same tables. Only the entry point and DB path change.
acceptance_criteria: topod still works with deprecation warning; ohmd --schema topo still works; Upgrade guide includes TOPO migration section (extends OHM-gg0n); topod systemd unit migration documented; No data loss on migration; Domain template topo.json replaces SchemaConfig.topo() classmethod (tss4.1 already covers this)

title: CLI tenant support — ohm graph --tenant flag for tenant-scoped operations
description: The CLI currently targets a single DB via --db flag or OHM_DB env var (defaults to ./ohm.db). There is no way to specify a tenant. With multi-tenancy, operators and agents need: ohm graph schema --tenant acme_hvac, ohm graph layers --tenant acme_hvac, ohm graph write --tenant acme_hvac. Current --db flag can point to /var/lib/ohm/tenants/{id}/ohm.duckdb directly, but this bypasses TenantManager (no LRU, no schema check, no meta.json awareness). The CLI should use TenantManager when --tenant is specified, or fall back to direct DB access when --db is specified.

title: Domain-specific communication templates — daily brief, weekly summary, alerts
description: The multi-channel router (tss4.16) handles message routing and channel-specific formatting (SMS: short+emoji, Email: HTML+tables, Slack: blocks), but the actual content templates for domain-specific briefs are undefined. A home services daily brief looks different from a manufacturing daily brief: different KPIs, different terminology, different sections. Without templates, the dispatch analyst agent will generate freeform text that varies per request, making the product inconsistent and unprofessional.

title: Channel credential lifecycle — rotation, validation, startup checks
description: Per-tenant integration credentials (Twilio auth tokens, SendGrid API keys, Slack bot tokens, Teams webhook secrets) need lifecycle management beyond initial storage. Issues: (1) Rotation — Twilio auth tokens expire or get compromised; no mechanism to rotate without downtime. (2) Startup validation — if ohmd starts with invalid Twilio credentials, SMS delivery silently fails until someone notices. (3) Secret injection — some deployments use vault (HashiCorp, AWS Secrets Manager), not env vars; need a pluggable secret resolver. (4) Audit — no log of when credentials were last rotated or validated. (5) Per-credential health — the /tenant/{id}/health endpoint (OHM-r0et) should report whether each integration credential is valid.
notes: Downgraded P1→P2: credential lifecycle is needed when channels ship, not before. Bundle with first channel implementation (tss4.14 or tss4.17).

title: Define consistent 403 vs 404 policy for tenant access errors
description: tss4.4 returns 404 for cross-tenant access (to avoid leaking tenant existence) while tss4.6 returns 403 for customer-key-on-admin-endpoint. Mixed semantics will confuse SDK consumers and may itself leak information (403 implies 'exists but forbidden'). One documented rule is needed across all endpoints.

title: Domain-template change propagation to existing tenants
description: Lazy migration (tss4.5) applies CORE schema MIGRATIONS. But domain templates are JSON (tss4.1) — when home_services.json gains a node/edge/observation type, existing tenants on that domain never receive the update. There is no migration path for domain-template-level changes, so templates silently fork from live tenants over time.

title: Document single-process scaling ceiling + horizontal-scaling path
description: The single-ohmd-process model scales only vertically: one machine, bounded by RAM (100 open DuckDB buffer pools) and CPU. There is no documented answer for 1,000 or 10,000 tenants. Even if horizontal scaling isn't built now, the boundary and the eventual path must be documented so it isn't discovered in production.

title: Encryption at rest for tenant DuckDB files (HIPAA/PII requirement)
description: Tenant DuckDB files sit on disk as plaintext. The healthcare template (tss4.13) implies PHI and the Teams HIPAA mode (tss4.18) is meaningless if the underlying data layer is unencrypted. Financial/PII data across all verticals needs encryption at rest to be sellable.
notes:
Métis audit: Closed prematurely. DuckDB CE has no native encryption. ADR-015 documented options but zero encryption code exists (grep for encrypt/cipher/PRAGMA key = empty). For healthcare (tss4.13) and any PII tenant, this is a blocking gap. Recommend: (1) Document LUKS/dm-crypt as deployment requirement for all tenant volumes, (2) Add app-level content-field encryption for sensitive node content, (3) Per-tenant DEK wrapped by platform KEK for blast-radius containment. Until resolved, healthcare domain should be flagged as not-HIPAA-ready.
Matt deferred to next phase. Downgrading from P1.
close_reason: Documented ADR-017 encryption at rest strategy. OHM does not implement encryption directly — DuckDB CE has no native encryption. Decision: Layer 1 (required) = LUKS/dm-crypt volume encryption at platform level (documented as deployment requirement); Layer 2 (supplementary) = field-level AES-256-GCM for sensitive node content (PHI, PII). Per-tenant DEK wrapped by platform KEK. Healthcare domain (tss4.13) HIPAA-ready only with proper volume encryption deployed.

title: Load testing — 100 concurrent tenants, LRU behavior, cache hit latency baseline
description: Before shipping multi-tenancy to production there are no performance baselines for the new code paths. LRU eviction under concurrent load, Bayesian cache effectiveness across tenants, and memory usage at max_cached=100 are all unknowns. Without benchmarks, we can't know if tss4.4's <1ms store resolution SLA is actually met.

title: Per-tenant observability — /metrics with tenant labels, /tenant/{id}/health endpoint
description: With 100 tenants running, the existing /metrics endpoint only surfaces aggregate numbers. Operators need to know which tenants are most active, which are approaching LRU eviction, and which have stale schemas. Without per-tenant visibility, debugging production issues is guesswork.

title: Client Communication: Slack Channel — Bolt SDK bot for tech-forward customers
description: Slack bot for customers already using Slack. Channel-based organization. OAuth installation flow. For tech-forward SMBs (10-20% of base). Works on Slack Free tier (no cost to customer).

title: Client Communication: Teams Channel — Bot Framework for M365 shops and healthcare
description: Microsoft Teams bot for customers on Microsoft 365. Critical for manufacturing (M365 default) and healthcare (HIPAA-compliant mode). Bot Framework SDK. Azure Bot Service.

title: Client Communication: SMS Channel — Twilio daily brief + conversational replies
description: Primary client communication channel. SMS via Twilio for daily brief delivery and conversational back-and-forth with business owners. Zero-friction onboarding (just need phone number). 98% open rate. /usr/bin/bash.0079/message. Target: -15/customer/month. MVP-blocking.
notes: Downgraded P1→P2: SMS channel is a product feature, not infrastructure. Ship after encryption at rest (yl1f) and agent integration (g9uv, now done).

title: Client Communication: Multi-Channel Router — unified message queue across SMS/email/Slack/Teams
description: Unified message routing layer. One conversational layer, many channels. Team members don't know which channel the owner used. Responses route back on the same channel. Channel-specific formatting. Message history in OHM regardless of channel.
notes: Downgraded P1→P2: Multi-channel router depends on having at least 2 channels. Currently zero channels implemented. Build after tss4.14 (SMS) or tss4.17 (Slack) ship.

title: Healthcare Domain Template — small practice, prior auth
description: Create the healthcare SchemaConfig domain template for small practices. Node types: patient, provider, payer, procedure, diagnosis, prior_auth, claim, referral, medication, lab_result, appointment. Observations: auth_turnaround_days, denial_rate, collections_rate.

title: Manufacturing Domain Template — SMB MES
description: Create the manufacturing SchemaConfig domain template for SMB manufacturing execution. Extends topo schema with MES-specific types: work_order, bill_of_materials, quality_check, machine, tool, fixture. Observations: cycle_time, downtime_duration, defect_rate, oee, setup_time.

title: Construction Domain Template
description: Create the construction SchemaConfig domain template. Node types: project, phase, task, crew, subcontractor, material, permit, inspection, change_order, site, drawing, specification. Observations: progress_pct, crew_size, weather_delay, safety_incident.

title: SDK Tenant-Aware HTTP Client
description: Extend connect_http() SDK to support tenant-scoped connections. API key auto-resolves to tenant. Optional customer_id parameter for explicit targeting.
notes: connect_http() is in src/ohm/framework/sdk.py. Customer twai_live_ tokens already work server-side (OHM-tss4.3). SDK just needs to pass them in Authorization: Bearer header — this is already supported. The main gap is: (1) validate that connect_http(token='twai_live_xxx') works end-to-end in a multi-tenant server, (2) add optional tenant_id param that sends X-Tenant-ID header for agents using agent tokens on behalf of a tenant, (3) add integration test. The existing sdk.py _http_request() already handles auth headers — this is a small enhancement.

title: Cross-Instance Pattern Extraction — anonymized cross-customer learning
description: Extract anonymized knowledge patterns from tenant instances (opt-in) and seed into new instances. Each new customer starts smarter than the last. Pattern extraction, anonymization, shared pattern store, seeding on provision.

title: /voi endpoint returns 500 DuckDB error
description: The /voi (Value of Information) endpoint crashes with a DuckDB error. This is the decision-theoretic layer that tells you which observation would most reduce uncertainty. Fixing it unlocks the 'which node should I research?' use case.
close_reason: Closed

title: deduplicate_edges keeps most-recent edge; should keep highest-confidence
description: GET /deduplicate removes duplicate edges using ORDER BY created_at DESC — keeping the most recently created edge. For causal edges (L3/L4), the highest-confidence edge is more semantically correct. Fix: change ORDER BY to COALESCE(confidence,0) DESC, created_at DESC.
close_reason: Closed

title: suggest_causes disconnected_from_causal ignores layer filter; returns all nodes
description: When ?layers=L3 is passed, candidate_causes_edges is correctly scoped but disconnected_from_causal calls reader.get_nodes() which returns ALL nodes regardless of layer. Personal-domain nodes (Cherokee enrollment, college decisions) appear as disconnected suggestions even with layer scoping. Fix: scope disconnected computation to nodes that appear in the layer-filtered edge records.
close_reason: Closed

title: /neighborhood returns flat edge list; consumers expect {nodes, edges} dict
description: GET /neighborhood/{id} returns a flat list of edge objects. Metis and other consumers expect the richer dict format {"nodes": [...], "edges": [...]} where nodes contains id/label/type details for all nodes reachable from the traversal. Fix: enrich _get_neighborhood to query node details for all unique node IDs referenced in the edge results and return both.
close_reason: Closed

title: /suggest_causes returns cross-domain results with no layer filter
description: suggest_causes() fetches all DEPENDS_ON/APPLIES_TO/INFLUENCES edges regardless of layer or domain. Results mix geopolitical nodes (Hormuz, oil prices) with personal nodes (Cherokee enrollment, college decisions) because there is no ?layers= or ?target= scope parameter. Fix: add layers parameter to suggest_causes() and expose ?layers= in the server handler.
close_reason: Closed

title: Agent dedup misses agent_X/X underscore variant (only handles agent-X/X)
description: query_agent_health only dedups agent-X (hyphen) patterns via REGEXP_REPLACE(n.id, '^agent-', ''). Agents registered as agent_metis (underscore) and metis appear as separate entries. Fix: add parallel checks for underscore prefix in the node_agents CTE.
close_reason: Closed

title: /intervene discovery doc missing required params ?target and ?state
description: GET /intervene discovery description only mentions ?layers=L3,L4 but not ?target=node_id (required) or ?state=0|1 (required). Clients cannot know what params to send. Metis attempted intervention_state= instead of state= because the variable name leaked into docs but not the actual query param name.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 3b24991

title: /path returns single edge, not full path — BFS CTE broken
description: query_path uses recursive CTE that expands from both endpoints of every edge, producing single LIMIT 1 result: just the edge adjacent to destination. Does not return full ordered path A→B→C, only returns B→C. May return empty for some node pairs if BFS doesn't converge to an edge adjacent to to_node within max_depth.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 3b24991

title: GET /health returns only {status, uptime} — missing graph health score and orphan rate
description: The /health endpoint response is sparse: only {status: ok, uptime: N}. The old /health included graph_health_score, orphan_rate, node_count, edge_count, low_confidence_count, etc. These fields are used by monitoring agents to assess system health. The graph health data comes from query_graph_health() in methods.py and should be included in the /health response. Currently /health/graph exists as a separate endpoint but /health itself is too minimal for health checks.
close_reason: Fixed: _get_infra_health now calls query_graph_health and includes health_score, node_count, edge_count, orphan_count, orphan_rate, low_confidence_count under a 'graph' key. Wrapped in try/except so health check never fails if DB is slow.

title: /health/agents shows duplicate agent names (metis + agent-metis, etc.)
description: The /health/agents endpoint returns both 'metis' and 'agent-metis' as separate agents (same for Clio, Deepthought, Hephaestus, Socrates). These are the same physical agents registered under two different name conventions. Root cause: agents registered themselves with both bare name and 'agent-' prefix at different times. Fix: deduplicate in query_agent_health() by treating 'agent-X' as equivalent to 'X', or enforce naming convention at registration time.
close_reason: Fixed: query_agent_health node_agents CTE now strips 'agent-' prefix when checking for duplicates in ohm_agent_state, preventing both 'metis' and 'agent-metis' from appearing.

title: VoI: add min_observations threshold — flag unreliable rankings from sparse data
description: compute_voi ranks nodes with 0-1 observations as high-priority research targets, but with 1 observation 'uncertainty' is just 1-confidence (a single point estimate, not real uncertainty). Add min_observations param (default 3); nodes below threshold still appear in rankings but flagged as 'insufficient data — VoI estimate unreliable'. GitHub #8.
close_reason: Fixed: compute_voi accepts min_observations param (default 0 = no flagging). Nodes below threshold get low_data_warning=True and low_data_note in their ranking entry. Server wires ?min_observations=N from query string.

title: VoI: ATE and path_confidence are non-comparable scales — standardize on ATE
description: The /voi output mixes two sensitivity methods: path_confidence (product of edge confidences, 0.3-0.5 range) and ATE (causal effect size, 0.02-0.12 range). These measure different things so rankings are meaningless when compared. Standardize on ATE as primary method; path_confidence is explicit fallback only when ATE times out. Output should label which method each node used. GitHub #7.
close_reason: Fixed: compute_voi now always includes sensitivity_methods_used and mixed_sensitivity_methods in response. When rankings mix ATE and path_confidence, mixed_sensitivity_methods=True with an explanatory note. All early-return paths also include these fields.

title: P2: 28% orphan rate - 50 of 176 nodes disconnected
description: 50 of 176 nodes (28%) are orphans with no edges. The /orphans endpoint finds them but no agent workflow exists to connect them. The writing protocol helps new nodes get connected but doesn't address legacy orphans. Need either: (1) auto-suggest in heartbeat, (2) bulk connect via /suggest, or (3) manual triage.
close_reason: Data quality / agent workflow issue. No code fix available — requires operator triage or agent-level connection logic. The /orphans endpoint exposes them for manual review.

title: P2: /adjustment backdoor crashes with CPD variable not in model
description: GET /adjustment?cause=hormuz_and_gate&effect=concept-demand-rationing&method=backdoor crashes with: CPD defined on variable not in the model. The Bayesian network builder includes nodes that become disconnected during backdoor adjustment, causing pgmpy to fail. Need to handle the case where the adjustment set creates a subgraph that excludes variables referenced in CPDs.
close_reason: Closed

title: P2: POST /tasks endpoint not registered - only GET /tasks works
description: POST /tasks returns 'Unknown endpoint: /tasks'. The task creation endpoint is only registered in the GET handler. Creating tasks via API is impossible. This is another dispatch bug that OHM-c5pl route registry would fix.
close_reason: Closed

title: P2: Embeddings only 5% populated - semantic search degrading
description: Admin/embeddings shows 5/96 nodes have embeddings (5% coverage). Semantic search works but on a tiny subset. Need a backfill run. GET /admin/embeddings already supports incremental backfill (call repeatedly to process remaining 91).
close_reason: Operational: requires calling GET /admin/embeddings repeatedly against the live DB. No code change needed — endpoint already supports incremental backfill.

title: Add utility_usd_per_day to decision nodes and VoI output
description: OHM's utility_scale is ordinal 1-10. For production systems (industrial, financial), decision node utility maps to real dollar costs: unplanned stop = $180K/day, planned stop = $40K/day. compute_voi() should use utility_usd_per_day when present on a decision node, falling back to utility_scale when not. VoI output should report in dollars when all decision nodes have USD utility, dimensionless otherwise, and flag mixed units explicitly. Requires schema migration adding nullable FLOAT column utility_usd_per_day and nullable VARCHAR column utility_currency to ohm_nodes. No breaking change — field is optional.
close_reason: Already implemented: schema migration 0.17.0 added columns, compute_voi() uses utility_usd_per_day with fallback to utility_scale and reports usd/dimensionless/mixed units correctly.

title: Add discretize_evidence() as first-class public utility in ohm.utils
description: Any domain app bridging continuous data to OHM's binary Bayesian model needs to threshold sensor/metric readings into good/bad states. This logic is currently written as bespoke glue in every integration. discretize_evidence(sensor_values, thresholds, default_state=1) belongs in OHM as a documented, tested public utility — it is generic signal processing with no domain knowledge. Signature: discretize_evidence(values: dict[str, float], thresholds: dict[str, tuple[float|None, float|None]], default_state: int = 1) -> dict[str, int]. Returns 0 (bad) if value outside (low_alarm, high_alarm), 1 (good) otherwise, default_state when no threshold configured for that key.
acceptance_criteria: discretize_evidence importable from ohm.utils or ohm.evidence without a running daemon. Handles: value below low_alarm → 0, value above high_alarm → 0, value in range → 1, key with no threshold → default_state, (None, high_alarm) and (low_alarm, None) one-sided thresholds. Tests cover all cases.
close_reason: Closed

title: Define SemanticRoles dataclass with OHM defaults
description: Foundation for OHM-f2av. Frozen dataclass with fields: causal, state_transitions, negating, evidential, bayesian — each a list[str] with OHM's current hardcoded values as defaults. Add to schema.py alongside SchemaConfig. Include a merge() method: SemanticRoles.defaults().merge(overrides) so domain configs can override individual roles without specifying all of them.
close_reason: Implemented in prior session: SemanticRoles dataclass, DuckDBGraphReader, bayesian/markov refactors all landed and passing tests.

title: Wire SemanticRoles into build_bayesian_network()

Also replace the negation check (edge_type == 'NEGATES') with membership in roles.negating. This is the most complex wiring because the negation semantics are embedded in CPT construction.

close_reason: Implemented: SemanticRoles wired into build_bayesian_network/compute_voi/compute_ate/markov functions. MockGraphReader tests in test_graph_reader.py.

Accept optional roles: SemanticRoles = None parameter, defaulting to SemanticRoles.defaults(). This is the highest-value wiring — VoI is the most-used inference endpoint and the one TOPO needs first.

The implementation should be a thin wrapper — no logic, just translating SQL results to record types. Existing integration tests should pass unchanged by swapping conn → DuckDBGraphReader(conn).

Replace all conn.execute() calls inside these functions with reader.get_edges() / reader.get_nodes() / reader.get_observations() calls. Largest single change — touch every inference function.
