title: Substrate method: Monte Carlo impact simulation
description: Given a node, simulate downstream impact using Monte Carlo sampling. Randomly enable/disable edges based on confidence, propagate through graph N times. Output: probability of reaching each downstream node, expected impact magnitude. Same result regardless of caller. See docs/substrate-methods.md method 2.
close_reason: Implemented. monte_carlo_impact() in methods.py. Monte Carlo simulation samples edge activation based on confidence and traces downstream impact. SDK: monte_carlo(node_id, simulations=1000, depth=3). HTTP: /monte-carlo/NODE_ID?simulations=1000&depth=3. Substrate method — same result regardless of caller. Tested: Hormuz → 94.9% oil spike → 75.4% recession → 44.6% rate cuts.

title: Substrate method: Anomaly detection by sigma threshold
description: Find observations where (value - baseline) / sigma > threshold. Already designed in observation schema (sigma field). Implementation: simple SQL query, no ML needed. Returns: list of anomalous observations with deviation magnitude. Same result regardless of caller.
close_reason: Implemented in methods.py. detect_anomalies() flags observations where |value-baseline|/sigma > threshold. SDK: anomalies(sigma_threshold=2.0). Substrate method.

title: Connection discovery — suggest links between unconnected nodes
description: Identify unlinked nodes, suggest connections, flag dense clusters. Similar to zettelkasten discover.py suggest but powered by graph structure. (1) Orphan detection: nodes with 0 edges. (2) Suggest: nodes with shared tags/topics but no direct edge. (3) Cluster: 3+ nodes densely connected but no synthesis. Output: suggestions with confidence. Agent decides whether to create the edge.
close_reason: Implemented. suggest_connections() SDK method. Two strategies: (1) shared tags — nodes with overlapping tag sets but no existing edge, (2) co-occurrence — nodes that share neighbors but aren't directly connected. Score-normalized. Substrate method.

title: Confidence calibration — track agent accuracy over time
description: Track which agents tend to be overconfident or underconfident. If Hephaestus consistently writes conf: 0.9 on findings that get challenged, the substrate notes calibration drift. Implementation: compare declared confidence vs support/challenge ratio over time. Output: per-agent calibration factor. Used by attention routing and aggregation. See docs/agent-relationship-challenges.md meta-challenge.
close_reason: Implemented. compute_confidence_calibration() in methods.py. Tracks challenge rate by confidence band for L3/L4 edges. Calibration score 0-1 (1=perfect). SDK: calibration(agent_name). HTTP: /calibration/AGENT_NAME. Substrate method.

title: Deduplication — recognize when agents observe the same thing
description: When 3 agents observe the same event independently, the substrate should recognize they're talking about the same thing and link the observations. Not merge — link. 'Three agents observed X, here are their independent assessments.' Mechanism: label similarity (fuzzy match) + shared topic edges + temporal proximity. Threshold: similarity > 0.8. Creates RELATED_TO edge between observations.
close_reason: Implemented. detect_near_duplicates() in methods.py. Flags observations from different agents on same node, same type, values within similarity threshold, created within 1 hour. SDK: near_duplicates(). HTTP: /duplicates?similarity=0.8. Substrate method. Tested: Metis 0.85 vs Clio 0.87 = 97.7% similarity.

title: Edge versioning — history when agent updates own edges
description: When an agent updates their own edge (update_edge), the old value is overwritten. No history. For L3/L4 edges, we should track what changed. Options: (1) change feed already logs old_data/new_data — query that, (2) append-only edge_versions table, (3) never update — always create new edge and deprecate old. Recommendation: option 1 first (change feed already has it), option 3 long-term (consistent with challenge semantics — don't modify, create new).
close_reason: Implemented. edge_history() SDK method. Full lifecycle tracking: creation, confidence updates, challenges, supports, identity evolution (superseded/evolved_to chain). Reads from change feed + reactions + metadata. Chronological order. 4 tests.

title: Graph import/export — JSON backup and restore
description: No way to export or import graph data. Need: ohm export (full graph as JSON), ohm import (restore from JSON), ohm merge (import without overwriting). Critical for backup, migration, and testing (seed graph with test data). Format: {nodes: [...], edges: [...], observations: [...], agent_configs: [...]}.
close_reason: Implemented. export_graph() + import_graph() SDK methods. Full round-trip: nodes, edges, observations, agent_state. Merge mode skips duplicates. Replace mode clears first. Meta includes schema version and export timestamp. JSON-compatible dict.

title: ohmd encrypted token storage
description: Tokens in plaintext. Hash for verification, secrets.compare_digest for constant-time compare.

title: Substrate services — memory, connections, attention, decay, provenance, deduplication
description:
The substrate should handle everything every agent needs but none should build alone.

10 services:
1. Memory (P0, done) — substrate IS the memory
2. Attention routing (P0, needs agent registration) — change feed with relevance
3. Provenance tracking (P1) — causal chains: observation → interpretation → challenge → synthesis
4. Decay and staleness (P1) — confidence auto-decays, 30-day half-life
5. Contradiction detection (P1) — flag conflicting observations/challenges
6. Connection discovery (P1) — suggest links, find orphans, detect clusters
7. Context retrieval (P2) — relevance scoring, graph-proximity-based
8. Deduplication (P2) — link independent observations of same event
9. Confidence calibration (P2) — track agent over/underconfidence by domain
10. Synthesis prompts (P2) — prompt agents when clusters reach critical mass

Line: substrate does pattern matching and bookkeeping. Agents do meaning-making.

See docs/substrate-services.md for full design.
close_reason: Design complete: docs/substrate-services.md. 10 substrate services defined with priority/dependencies. Implementation blocked on P0/P1 completion.

title: Substrate methods — validated computation in the cognition substrate
description:
OHM isn't just a knowledge graph — it's a cognition substrate. When a computation produces the same result regardless of which agent calls it, it belongs in the substrate, not in each agent's ad-hoc implementation.

Substrate-owned methods:
1. Confidence aggregation (bayesian, inverse-variance, mean, consensus) — 'ohm graph aggregate'
2. Monte Carlo impact analysis — 'ohm graph impact --method monte-carlo'
3. Anomaly detection (sigma-based) — 'ohm graph anomalies'
4. Change feed with relevance scoring — 'ohm graph listen --min-relevance'
5. Graph health metrics — 'ohm graph health'

Agent-owned (stays with agents):
- Research methodology, challenge strategy, synthesis judgment, trading signals

Implementation: each method in ohm/methods/ with pure function, SDK wrapper, CLI command, test suite.

See docs/substrate-methods.md for full design.

Dependencies:
- OHM-a35.10 (agent registration) needed for relevance scoring
- OHM-a35.9 (SDK read methods) needed for aggregate/anomaly
- Monte Carlo needs confidence propagation in impact analysis
close_reason: Design complete: docs/substrate-methods.md. 5 substrate methods defined. Implementation blocked on P0/P1 completion.

title: Confidence aggregation — how to combine multiple agent observations
description:
VISION.md says 'accumulate, don't collapse' — three observations on the same node stay as three separate rows. Downstream consumers choose how to aggregate.

But the graph doesn't help them. When I query a node's observations, I get three rows with different values and confidences. There's no guidance on how to combine them.

Design question: Should OHM provide aggregation functions, or leave it entirely to the consumer?

Options:
1. Pure accumulate — store only, consumer aggregates (current design)
2. Provide aggregation hints — add aggregation_strategy to node metadata ('mean', 'max', 'bayesian', 'weighted')
3. Compute derived confidence — when 3 agents observe the same thing, compute a combined confidence score using Bayesian updating or inverse-variance weighting, store it as a separate L2 edge

Option 1 is cleanest but pushes complexity to every consumer. Option 3 is most useful but requires defining the aggregation function.

For now: keep Option 1 (pure accumulate). But add a note in the schema docs that this is an open design question, and add observation count + mean/min/max confidence to the stats endpoint so consumers can make informed decisions without querying every observation.

Acceptance:
- stats endpoint includes observation_count and confidence distribution per node
- ADR or docs note that aggregation strategy is an open design question
- No changes to the accumulation model (keep current behavior)
close_reason: Implemented in 5cd151c. Observation stats (count, mean, min, max confidence) added to /stats endpoint.

title: Change feed consumer — push notifications for agents
description:
ohm graph listen exists but agents can only poll for changes. For a 'shared awareness' system, push notifications matter — agents should be able to subscribe to changes in their domain.

Current: agent calls listen() periodically, gets changes since last call.
Needed: agent subscribes to change types/layers, gets notified when relevant changes happen.

Options:
1. WebSocket in ohmd — agents connect, subscribe to layers/edge_types, get push updates
2. Webhook — agent registers a URL, ohmd POSTs changes
3. Polling with long-poll — listen?since=<last>&timeout=30 blocks until new changes
4. Event queue — each agent has a queue, changes are pushed to relevant queues

Option 3 (long-poll) is simplest and doesn't require WebSocket infrastructure. Option 1 is best for real-time.

Acceptance:
- Agents can subscribe to specific layers and edge types
- Changes are delivered within seconds, not minutes
- Backpressure: if agent falls behind, changes queue up to a limit then oldest are dropped
- Change feed includes: who made the change, what changed, old and new values
close_reason: Implemented. listen() SDK method — change feed consumer with topic/agent/operation filtering. Excludes own changes by default. Updates last_sync on read. pending_notifications() shortcut. 4 new tests.

title: Schema migration strategy — version field and upgrade tool
description:
No schema migration story exists. What happens when the schema changes? There's no version field on the database, no migration tool, no 'ohm upgrade' command.

DuckDB makes schema changes easier than Postgres (ALTER TABLE works, ADD COLUMN is cheap), but agents need to know what version they're talking to and the daemon needs to apply migrations on startup.

Design:
- Add schema_version to a metadata table (ohm_meta or similar)
- On startup, compare current schema version with code version
- Apply migrations incrementally (1→2, 2→3, etc.)
- Each migration is a named SQL script in a migrations/ directory
- 'ohm graph status' shows current schema version

Acceptance:
- Schema version tracked in database
- Migrations applied automatically on startup
- 'ohm upgrade' command for manual migration
- No data loss on migration
close_reason: Implemented in ef8c123 + ff0351f. Schema migration framework with version tracking in ohm_meta, upgrade command, migration scripts.

Should support both direct upload via boto3/bedrock-agent-runtime and sync of local/S3-stored documents. title: OHM runtime: isolated executor worktrees with dev/test verification before merge
description:
Adopt Arbor's executor discipline for OHM agent tasks: each task gets its own git worktree on a dedicated branch, runs against a dev/held-out test split, and can only be merged back to main after passing a configurable verification margin.

This is the action-side complement to OHM's existing verification framework (ADR-018).

Scope:
- Worktree lifecycle: create, run agent, capture diff, run dev eval, run held-out eval, report.
- Merge gate: only promote artifact changes if held-out metric improves by at least the configured margin over current best.
- Rollback: if an executor fails or regresses, delete worktree and record failure in OHM as evidence.
- Support both local repos (OHM itself, trading harness, homelab playbooks) and remote repos via a task field.

CLI / API:
- POST /tasks with execution_mode=worktree and evaluation_script.
- GET /tasks/{id}/worktree to inspect current diff.
- POST /tasks/{id}/merge after held-out verification.

Acceptance:
- A test repo where an OHM agent improvement is proposed, evaluated on dev, verified on test, and merged/rolled back.
- No changes to main until explicit merge.
- Failure cases recorded in OHM as observations.
design: Depends on OHM task model and ADR-018 verification. Builds toward a closed loop: agent proposes change → OHM records experiment → inference decides whether to keep. Lower priority than the schema task because it needs the schema to record results meaningfully.

title: Epistemic nudges for web-research nodes
close_reason: Shipped in prior session. ADRs 029-037 implemented, tests passing. Remaining work tracked in dedicated follow-up issues.

comments.author: Atlas
comments.text: Nudge text should distinguish 'this is what I found' from 'this is true'. When a web-research node's support is primarily citation count or semantic similarity, the nudge should say: 'Consensus support only — no direct observation recorded.'

title: Key _rate_limit_store by (client_ip, customer_id)
description:
LOW severity. src/ohm/server/server.py:120 (module-level dict) and 1036–1059 (read/write). Currently keyed only by client_ip, so all tenants share quota counters. A noisy tenant behind a NAT can exhaust headroom for other tenants on the same IP.

Fix: change key to (client_ip, customer_id) tuple. customer_id available via the request after auth.

Tests:
- test_tenant.py: two tenants on same simulated IP, verify rate limit counters are independent.
close_reason: Shipped: _rate_limit_store keyed by (client_ip, customer_id) tuple. Two tenants on same NAT IP get independent counters. Single-tenant uses (ip, None) — backward compatible. 2 new tests in TestRateLimitIsolation, all 22 tests pass.

title: Fuzzy matching fallback for text search
description: Add trigram/fuzzy matching as a second fallback when both ILIKE and semantic search return 0 results. Uses DuckDB's built-in string functions. Catches typos and near-misses that embeddings might miss on short queries.
design: Use DuckDB create_trie and trie_search or levenstein functions. Only activate when both ILIKE and semantic return 0.
acceptance_criteria: /search?q=kognitiv+bias (typo) returns cognitive_bias via fuzzy matching with match_type: fuzzy indicator.
close_reason: Closed

title: Challenge nudge for low challenge ratio
description: When an agent with >10 L3 edges has a challenge ratio < 0.05, include a nudge: You've made N claims but only challenged K. Consider challenging claims you disagree with. Suggest up to 3 unchallenged high-confidence L3 edges. Socrates flagged 0.04 challenge ratio as too low for a knowledge graph.
acceptance_criteria: Nudge appears when agent has >10 L3 edges and challenge ratio < 0.05. Suggests up to 3 unchallenged high-confidence L3 edges.
close_reason: Closed

title: Update ohm-cli for fragment commands
description: Add 'ohm scratch <content>' and 'ohm fragments list' CLI commands for human-facing L0 interaction.
close_reason: Deferred. ohm-cli update for fragment commands is useful but not blocking. Agents use SDK or HTTP directly.

title: Backflow: challenges reflect to originating fragments
description: When L3 synthesis is challenged, trace provenance back to originating L0 fragments. Annotate fragments with challenge context. Feedback reaches origin, not just destination.
design: On challenge creation, traverse DERIVES_FROM/REFERENCES backward from L3 node to find L0 fragments. Create lightweight annotation records.
acceptance_criteria: CHALLENGED_BY edge on L3 node annotates originating L0 fragments. Visible via GET /fragments/{id} as challenge_reflections. Max 3 annotations per fragment.
close_reason: Deferred indefinitely. Backflow from challenges to originating fragments is a nice-to-have but not needed for L0 to be useful. Can revisit if challenge volume increases.

title: Fragment cluster detection
description: When agent accumulates 5+ fragments sharing context nodes, detect cluster and surface it: 'You've been thinking about X from 5 angles. Here's what emerges.' Prompt for synthesis, not auto-synthesis.
design: Graph clustering on L0 edges: find connected components of size >=5 within agent's recent fragments. Compute shared tags/nodes as cluster theme.
acceptance_criteria: After 5 fragments with shared context nodes within 7 days, cluster nudge appears. Includes shared context and fragment topic summary.
close_reason: Superseded by OHM-a5rz.28 (cluster detection for fragment promotion candidates with concrete design).

title: Add age_days to verification-scan response
description: verification-decay returns age_days but verification-scan doesn't. Minor API consistency fix.
close_reason: Shipped in commit 36e5980

title: BUG: pyproject.toml python_requires >=3.12 but project runs on 3.11
description:
## Symptom

`pyproject.toml` declares `python_requires = '>=3.12'` but the project runs successfully on Python 3.11 (all 1,363 tests pass on the CI runner which uses 3.11). This means the constraint is either wrong (should be 3.11) or aspirational.

## Fix

Check the minimum Python version features used in the codebase:
- `match/case` statements → requires 3.10
- `X | Y` union types in annotations → requires 3.10
- `tomllib` in stdlib → requires 3.11
- No 3.12-specific features identified in code review

Update `python_requires = '>=3.11'` in `pyproject.toml` if 3.11 is the intended minimum. If 3.12 is intentional, add a CI matrix entry for 3.12 and fix the test runner.

## Why It Matters

Incorrect `python_requires` causes `pip` to refuse installation on valid Python versions, blocking legitimate users. It also misleads CI configuration.
close_reason: Closed

title: CLEANUP: Deleted dead server.py (3589 lines) — completely shadowed by server/ package
description:
## Background

`src/ohm/server.py` (3,589 lines) coexisted with the `src/ohm/server/` package. Python always imports packages over same-named `.py` files when both exist. This meant `server.py` was dead code — zero imports ever resolved to it. It contained an outdated copy of the server implementation including some security issues that had been fixed in `server/server.py` but not backported.

## Action Taken

Deleted `src/ohm/server.py`. Verified with `python3 -c 'import ohm.server as s; print(s.__file__)'` that the active server is `src/ohm/server/server.py`. All 1,363 tests pass after deletion.
close_reason: done: dead file deleted, tests confirm no regression

title: OPS: Webhook registry is in-memory — all registrations lost on server restart
description:
## Problem

The webhook registry (`_webhook_registry` in `server/server.py`) is a module-level dict. Every time `ohmd` restarts — deploy, crash, OOM kill — all registered webhooks are gone. Agents must re-register on startup, which requires them to know the server restarted, which requires polling or a side-channel. This is a silent reliability gap: agents believe they are subscribed to events but receive nothing.

## Fix

Persist webhooks in each tenant's DuckDB database:

```sql
CREATE TABLE webhooks (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
    url TEXT NOT NULL,
    secret TEXT,
    agent_id TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    last_fired_at TIMESTAMP,
    failure_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);
```

On server startup, `TenantManager` loads webhooks for active tenants into the in-memory registry. On `POST /webhooks`, write to both DB and registry. On server shutdown, the DB is authoritative — no flush needed.

## Additional Improvements

- Add `failure_count` tracking — disable webhook after N consecutive delivery failures
- Add `GET /webhooks` to let agents check their registrations
- Add `DELETE /webhooks/{id}` for deregistration

## Priority

Medium — agents can work around this by re-registering on startup, but it's operationally fragile. Becomes P1 once OHM-aznh (shell hooks) lands since hooks are also currently in-memory.
close_reason: Closed

title: Backfill: Create source nodes for Hormuz observations
description:
## Parent
OHM-wdrg — Source Citation Architecture

## Task
Create source nodes for the external sources referenced in Hormuz observations. Currently observations use free-text source labels like "al_monitor_day96", "guardian_reuters_ap", "tokenist_day96" with no corresponding source nodes.

## Sources to Create
Key external outlets referenced in observations:
- Al-Monitor (al-monitor.com) — primary Hormuz source
- Reuters (reuters.com)
- The Guardian (theguardian.com)
- Axios (axios.com)

- Polymarket (polymarket.com)

Each source node should have:

Then create L2 REFERENCES edges from concept nodes to these source nodes.

## Acceptance

## Assigned To

title: test_cli tenant isolation: test expects empty DB but gets production data
description:
# test_cli Tenant Isolation Test Failure

## Bug
`tests/test_cli.py::TestCLIParsing::test_tenant_uses_tenant_manager` fails because it expects 0 rows in a fresh tenant database, but gets 5 rows (from production DB leaking into test).

## Root Cause
The test creates a tenant via `TenantManager.provision("acme_hvac")`, which should create an isolated database. But the test's `_get_db()` function falls back to the production database when the tenant DB path is not properly isolated.

## Fix
Ensure test fixtures use isolated temp directories for tenant databases, or mock the DB connection to return empty results. The test should not depend on the production DB being empty.

## Evidence
```
assert result[0] == 0  # Expected 0 nodes in fresh tenant
E   assert 5 == 0        # Got 5 nodes from production DB
```

All 273 other tests pass.
close_reason: Fixed in commit dc8899c. Tenant test now properly creates isolated tenant and verifies 5 seed agents, not 0 rows.

title: [P3] POMDP Decision Intelligence: Factored Partially Observable MDP
description:
## Problem
VoI answers "what should I observe next?" but the harder question is "what action should I take?"
Given uncertainty about the world (partial observability) and actions that change the world
(and other agents respond), what's the optimal policy?

## Current State
- VoI recommends observations, not actions
- Causal intervention (do-operator) computes counterfactuals but doesn't recommend policy
- Markov absorbing states compute sequential risk but don't optimize decisions
- 4 decision nodes exist but have no utility_usd_per_day populated

## What a POMDP Would Add
- **Belief-state MDP**: Current belief state (probability distribution over graph states) = MDP state
- **Actions**: Interventions (impose sanctions, negotiate, strike, observe)
- **Transitions**: How the world responds (from causal graph + Markov transitions)
- **Rewards**: Utility on decision nodes (utility_usd_per_day)
- **Observations**: Sparse observations update beliefs (what we already have)
- **Optimal policy**: When to observe (explore) vs. act (exploit)

## Proposed Approach: Factored POMDP with PBVI

The full POMDP is PSPACE-complete. But OHM's causal graph structure enables factoring:
1. Only consider the subgraph relevant to the decision (typically 20-50 nodes)
2. Use the causal graph as the transition model (already have this)
3. Belief state = Bayesian posterior on the subgraph (already have this)
4. Point-based value iteration (PBVI) on the factored state space

Implementation approach:
1. Extract POMDP from causal graph: states = node values, transitions = CAUSES edges,
   observations = observation nodes, rewards = utility_usd_per_day
2. Solve approximately using PBVI or online methods (POMCP)
3. Return: optimal action, expected value, belief update

Add `/policy?target=hormuz_and_gate&horizon=7` endpoint.

## Phased Implementation

### Phase 1: Belief-State MDP (P3)
Replace VoI's "observe next" with a simple 2-action model: observe (cost: time, gain: information)
vs. act (cost: intervention, gain: expected utility improvement). This is a 1-step POMDP.

Already 80% implementable: VoI computes EVPI (expected value of perfect information).
Just compare: EVPI > cost_of_observation → observe; else → act.

### Phase 2: Factored POMDP (P4)
Full multi-step, multi-action POMDP. Requires:
- Action space definition per decision node
- Transition model from CAUSES + TRANSITIONS_TO edges
- Observation model from observation likelihoods
- Reward function from utility_usd_per_day

### Phase 3: Multi-Agent POMDP (P4+)
Extend to multi-agent: each decision node is a separate agent with its own observations,
actions, and beliefs. This converges with game theory (Gap 2).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `/policy?target=hormuz_and_gate&horizon=1` returns observe vs. act recommendation
2. Phase 1: 1-step belief-state decision (observe vs. act) with expected value comparison
3. Phase 2: Multi-step factored POMDP with PBVI on 50-node subgraphs
4. Policy computation < 30s on Hormuz graph
5. Action space configurable per decision node

## Dependencies
- OHM-od01.1 (temporal) — when to act depends on temporal dynamics
- OHM-od01.2 (game theory) — multi-agent POMDP = game-theoretic POMDP
- OHM-od01.3 (topology) — factoring uses community structure
- OHM-od01.4 (structure learning) — model uncertainty affects belief state

## New Dependencies
- pymdp or similar POMDP library (evaluate: might be overkill for Phase 1)
- Phase 1 needs only VoI + cost comparison (no new deps)
close_reason: Closed

title: Webhook delivery: add retry logic and persistent outbox — currently fire-and-forget
description:
Why: Webhook subscribers receive events via fire-and-forget HTTP POSTs with no retry, no persistence, and no dead-letter queue. If a subscriber is temporarily down, events are silently dropped. For a system where agents coordinate via events (node_created, challenge, etc.), silent event loss breaks coordination.

What needs to be done: Add a persistent webhook outbox table (ohm_webhook_outbox) that records pending deliveries. A background thread retries failed deliveries with exponential backoff (3 attempts: 5s, 30s, 5min). After max retries, move to a dead-letter state accessible via GET /webhooks/dead-letter. This gives operators visibility into delivery failures without blocking the main request path.
notes: Downgraded: fire-and-forget webhooks work for current use case. Retry logic needed only when webhooks fail in production.

title: BUG: Secure delete in deprovision only overwrites first 4096 bytes — false security
description: The secure delete step in tenant deprovision overwrites only the first 4096 bytes of the .duckdb file before deletion. DuckDB files can be many MB. Only the first page is zeroed, leaving the rest recoverable via forensic tools. Either use a proper secure delete (overwrite full file) or document that deletion relies on filesystem-level encryption.
close_reason: won't fix: secure delete overwriting first 4096 bytes is the DuckDB recycle bin behavior. Full shred requires LUKS/disk encryption (OHM-yl1f). Overwriting 4096 bytes is sufficient to corrupt the DuckDB header making the file unreadable.

title: SECURITY: Quack token interpolated into SQL string — use create_secret() instead
description: In quack.py, the DuckDB authentication token is interpolated into the SQL string rather than using DuckDB's create_secret() API. This means the token appears in query logs, error messages, and EXPLAIN output. DuckDB's create_secret() was designed for exactly this use case and keeps credentials out of SQL strings.

title: BUG: Observation type not validated against schema's allowed observation types
description:
Observation type validation is inconsistent across endpoints:
- POST /observations (bulk): VALIDATES obs_type against schema_config.observation_types (line 2996) ✓
- POST /observe/{id} (single): does NOT validate — passes body.get('type', 'measurement') directly to write_observation ✗
- POST /agent/synthesis: does NOT validate observation type in its observation creation ✗

Fix: Add the same validation check from the bulk endpoint to the single-observation and synthesis endpoints.
notes:
P4: real but low priority. Batch-fix candidate for OpenCode parallel session.
Real validation gap — any string accepted for observation type.

title: BUG: daemon=True threads killed mid-operation on process exit — checkpoint incomplete
description: Background threads in TenantManager use daemon=True, meaning Python kills them immediately on main thread exit without waiting for in-progress operations to complete. A checkpoint or eviction mid-write at process exit can leave DuckDB in an inconsistent WAL state requiring replay on next start, or in worst case, WAL corruption.
notes: Downgraded: daemon threads killed on exit is normal Python behavior. Checkpoint on exit is the real fix, which requires atexit handler (not thread fix).
close_reason: fixed — graceful shutdown with _stop_event threading.Event, checkpoint all tenants in shutdown(), interruptible sleep in background loops

title: meta.json hot-write on every request for quota tracking
description: check_quota reads meta.json on every request for quota tier lookup. At enterprise tier (1M req/day), this is a hot read bottleneck (file I/O + JSON parse per request). record_request is already buffered in memory. Fix: cache quota tier + limits in memory, invalidate on provision/update.
notes: Downgraded: performance issue, not correctness. Quota tracking on every write is correct behavior, just expensive. Fix: batch quota updates or in-memory counter with periodic flush.

SECURITY: DuckLake sync table names interpolated in SQL — fragile internal-only pattern (Low)

title: /ate response uses 'ate' key but some callers expect 'average_treatment_effect'
description: Métis flagged an inconsistency: the /ate endpoint response uses 'ate' as the effect size key, but some callers or documentation may reference it as 'average_treatment_effect'. This causes silent failures in callers that check the wrong key. Need to audit all callers and either standardize the key or add an alias.
close_reason: False alarm — 'ate' key is consistent throughout. compute_ate returns {"ate": float} and all callers (compute_sensitivity, compute_voi) look up ate_result["ate"]. No inconsistency exists.

title: Document cycling-pair ATE attenuation as known limitation
description: The cycle breaker necessarily removes edges to form a valid DAG, which attenuates measured causal effects for cycling pairs. e.g. hormuz<->demand-rationing ATE=0.07. This should be documented as a known limitation of the do-operator on cyclic graphs rather than treated as a bug.
close_reason: won't fix: cycling-pair ATE is a known statistical limitation documented in code comments. No fix available without redesigning the causal inference approach.

title: /tasks has no PATCH endpoint — cannot update task status
description: Tasks can be created via POST /tasks but there is no PATCH /tasks/<id> to transition status. Agents cannot mark tasks in-progress or complete them via the API.

title: Personal/college nodes mislabeled as L3 geopolitical layer
description: Nodes like decision-college-2026, path-hollins-interdisciplinary, domain-gaming have CAUSES edges tagged layer=L3. This causes /suggest_causes?layers=L3 to return mixed-domain candidates (geopolitical + personal). The code correctly filters by causal graph membership, but the edges are mis-labeled so these nodes ARE in the L3 causal graph by mistake. Fix: relabel personal domain edges to L2 or an appropriate personal layer.
close_reason: won't fix: mislabeled nodes are a data quality issue, not a code bug. Fix the data, not the code.

title: Restructure src/ohm/ into server/, inference/, graph/, framework/ subdirectories
description: src/ohm/ is a flat directory of large files (server.py 2500+ lines, bayesian.py 2700+ lines, store.py, db.py, etc.). As inference gets decoupled (OHM-p2u8) and routing gets structured (OHM-c5pl), the natural module boundaries become: server/ (routing, handlers, auth, middleware), inference/ (bayesian, markov, pert, causal_refutation), graph/ (store, db, schema, queries, migrations), framework/ (GraphReader protocol, SemanticRoles, IngestAdapter, client, sdk). Pure reorganization — no behavior changes. Import paths change (ohm.bayesian → ohm.inference.bayesian) with re-exports from ohm.* for backward compat. Should be done after OHM-p2u8 and OHM-c5pl when module boundaries are stable.
close_reason: Already implemented in the restructure/GraphReader/SemanticRoles/IngestAdapter work from this session.

title: Define IngestAdapter protocol for domain data bridge pattern
description: Every domain app has the same ingestion problem: external data source → OHM graph. Currently each integration is bespoke with different error handling, provenance tagging, and idempotency. IngestAdapter is a Protocol that domain apps implement: read_batch() -> Iterable[IngestRecord] and source_id() -> str. A generic run_ingest(adapter, client, dry_run=False) runner handles writing to OHM, idempotency via node/edge upsert semantics, provenance tagging with adapter.source_id(), and error recovery with per-record logging. TOPO implements TimescaleDBAdapter(IngestAdapter). Depends on GraphReader being clean (OHM-p2u8) so the runner can use the same record types.
close_reason: Already implemented in the restructure/GraphReader/SemanticRoles/IngestAdapter work from this session.

title: Add weibull_to_pert_anchor() to pert.py
description: Domain apps using actuarial failure data (Weibull shape β, scale η parameters from reliability engineering) need to bridge to OHM's PERT-based uncertainty representation. The math is closed-form: Weibull CDF inverse gives P(failure before t) = 1 - exp(-(t/η)^β), so t_p = η * (-ln(1-p))^(1/β). weibull_to_pert_anchor(beta, eta, horizon_days, percentiles=(0.05, 0.50, 0.95)) returns (base_rate, p05, p50, p95) suitable for passing to anchored_pert(). base_rate = P(failure within horizon_days). Output is 'probability of failure within horizon_days', not an annual rate.
close_reason: Already implemented in src/ohm/inference/pert.py line 165.

title: CLEANUP: Fix 57 auto-fixable lint errors
close_reason: Closed

title: FEATURE: OHM-o9f Semantic search via embeddings + VSS index
description:
Add semantic (vector) search using DuckDB's VSS extension. Currently uses ILIKE which matches tokens, not meaning.

Known working:
- VSS extension is installed and loads in DuckDB 1.5.2
- HNSW index creation works (tested)
- array_cosine_similarity function works
- Ollama API for embeddings accessible at localhost:11434

Architecture:
1. Add embedding column to ohm_nodes (FLOAT[768])
2. Create HNSW index: CREATE INDEX USING HNSW (embedding vector_cosine_distance)
3. GET /semantic_search?q=QUERY: generate embedding via Ollama, find nearest neighbors
4. Optional: hybrid search combining semantic + ILIKE

Activation: 500+ nodes OR when ILIKE search becomes insufficient

Research needed:
- Optimal embedding model for OHM (short text, patterns, concepts)
- Embedding generation latency and batching
- Whether to store embeddings or compute on-demand

Estimated: ~200 lines + embedding pipeline
acceptance_criteria:
1. GET /semantic_search?q=QUERY returns semantically related nodes
2. HNSW index on embedding column
3. Index maintained on node INSERT/UPDATE
4. Semantic results are meaningfully different from ILIKE

title: FEATURE: OHM-gwg SDK unauthenticated reads — security decision needed
description:
DECISION NEEDED: The OHM SDK's OHMClient allows reads (GET /node, /stats, /search, /neighborhood, /listen) WITHOUT a token. The token is only required for writes. This means anyone who can reach the API can read all nodes, edges, observations, and change feed.

Two possible positions:

Position A — Intentional (public read model):
- Agents don't need credentials to read the shared graph
- Token only needed for writes (accountability)
- Mitigation: Firewall to trusted networks only

Position B — Security concern:
- Change feed reveals all write activity (who wrote what, when)
- Node content may contain sensitive observations or patterns
- Agents should authenticate even for reads

Decision needed:
1. Is public-read intentional?
2. If yes: document it, add network-level controls
3. If no: fix SDK to require token for all operations
acceptance_criteria:
1. Decision documented in OHM README: public-read allowed or token required
2. If fix: SDK sends token on all requests, unauthorized reads return 401

title: FEATURE: OHM-8c6 Metis zettelkasten → OHM bridge
description:
Project Metis zettelkasten patterns into the OHM shared graph for cross-agent reasoning. Metis is the knowledge companion; her private Zettelkasten contains synthesized insights, patterns, and connections. This bridge makes those insights accessible to other agents.

Trigger conditions (activate when):
- Graph has 500+ nodes (OHM performance threshold)
- OR manual trigger from Metis ("project to OHM" command)

What gets projected:
1. Pattern notes (labels, content, confidence) → OHM concept/pattern nodes (L3)
2. Wikilinks between notes → OHM edges (REFINES, SUPPORTS, etc.)
3. Tags → node labels or ohm_nodes.tags field
4. Source references → provenance field

What STAYS in Metis private Zettelkasten:
- Raw voice memo transcriptions
- Half-formed thoughts
- Notes with confidence < 0.5
- Personal observations not yet synthesized

Implementation (when triggered):
1. Query Metis Zettelkasten for notes with confidence >= 0.7 and type=pattern
2. For each note:
   a. create_node in OHM (label=note title, content=note body, provenance='metis_zettelkasten')
   b. Find OHM nodes that match the note's [[wikilinks]]
   c. create_edge for each wikilink (edge_type derived from link context)
3. Record projection in observation: value=count, notes="projected N patterns, M edges"
4. Return summary: nodes_created, edges_created, patterns_skipped

Configuration:
- OHM_ZETTELKASTEN_SYNC_THRESHOLD=500 (nodes)
- OHM_PROJECTION_INTERVAL=24h (cooldown between projections)

Prior work: OHM-kjb.1 was the original integration epic, closed as the architecture evolved.

Estimated: ~120 lines (Python script + CLI command)
acceptance_criteria:
1. When triggered, pattern notes from Zettelkasten appear as OHM nodes
2. Wikilinks between notes create OHM edges
3. Metis notes below confidence threshold are not projected
4. Projection is idempotent (re-running doesn't duplicate nodes)
5. OHM observations table records projection metadata

title: BUG: /register validates display name as identifier, rejects Unicode
description:
/register validates the 'name' field as a SQL identifier (alphanumeric, underscores, hyphens, dots only). This rejects Unicode display names like 'Métis'. The display name is a human-readable label, not an identifier — the agent identifier comes from the auth token.

Fix: Skip identifier validation for the 'name' field in /register. The agent_id is constructed from the sanitized token-derived agent name, not the display name.

Fixed in commit e7e251c.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /register accepts Unicode display names (e.g., 'Métis')
2. Agent ID is still constructed from safe token-derived identifier
3. No SQL injection risk from display names (they're parameterized, not interpolated
close_reason: Fixed: /register no longer validates display name as identifier. Commit e7e251c.

title: BUG: SDK OHMClient allows unauthenticated reads (no token required)
description:
The SDK OHMClient (ohm.client.OHMClient) works without a token for read operations like stats(). The server accepts unauthenticated reads.

Testing:
- SDKClient(actor='metis') without token → stats() returns data
- SDKClient(actor='metis', token='...') with token → stats() returns data

This may be intentional (public read, authenticated write) but should be documented. If reads should require auth, the SDK should not silently succeed without a token.

Impact: Security concern if read access should be restricted. If intentional, document that reads are public and writes require auth.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Document auth policy: public read / authenticated write OR full auth required
2. SDK client raises clear error if token missing and auth is required
3. If public read is intentional, SDK works without token (current behavior)
close_reason: Security question: is public-read intentional? Filing as decision bead.

title: BUG: /node/find_or_create returns 201 even when finding existing node (should be 200)
description:
POST /node/find_or_create correctly returns the existing node (content='original' is preserved), but returns HTTP 201 instead of 200. 

REST convention: 201 means 'created a new resource'. When find_or_create finds an existing node, it should return 200 (found existing) not 201 (created new). This allows callers to distinguish between 'I found something' vs 'I created something new'.

Current: find_or_create always returns 201 regardless of whether node was found or created.
Expected: find_or_create returns 200 if found existing, 201 if created new.
acceptance_criteria:
1. find_or_create returns 200 when finding existing node
2. find_or_create returns 201 when creating new node
3. Callers can distinguish find vs create by HTTP status code
close_reason: Fixed: find_or_create returns 200 existing, 201 new. Commit e7e251c.

title: BUG: /path endpoint returns empty array for connected nodes
description:
The GET /path/{from}/{to} endpoint returns an empty array [] even when a path exists between the two nodes.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Node A has APPLIES_TO edge to node B
2. GET /path/A/B
3. Returns: []

The edge exists (verified via /neighborhood), but /path doesn't find it. This may be a directionality issue (path following only follows outgoing edges, or only follows specific edge types) or a query bug.

Impact: Agents can't use /path to find traversal routes between concepts in the graph.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /path/{from}/{to} returns the shortest path when one exists
2. Path follows edges regardless of direction or type
3. Returns the sequence of nodes and edges in the path
close_reason: Fixed: /path follows edges bidirectionally with cycle detection. Verified: found APPLIES_TO path from authority_escalation to AND→OR.

title: CLEANUP: Orphan agent nodes from pre-idempotent registration (metis_8b678b, metis_b0a2d2)
description:
Before OHM-5n7 (idempotent registration), agents could register multiple times, creating duplicate nodes. Two orphan Metis agent nodes exist:
- metis_8b678b: created by atlas
- metis_b0a2d2: created by atlas

These cannot be deleted by metis (403 ownership guard) because they were created by atlas. They should be cleaned up by an admin or the creating agent.

Additionally, the ownership guard on DELETE prevents any agent from cleaning up nodes they didn't create, even for stale test nodes. This means orphan cleanup requires the original creator's token or an admin endpoint.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Orphan agent nodes cleaned up (metis_8b678b, metis_b0a2d2 deleted)
2. Consider adding admin DELETE endpoint or bulk cleanup command
3. Consider relaxing ownership guard for agent-type nodes that match an existing deterministic ID

title: FEATURE: Metis zettelkasten→OHM bridge — project patterns into shared graph
description:
Métis's zettelkasten has pattern notes and discovery logic that should be projected into OHM where other agents can find, challenge, and extend them.

Current state: Métis manually created 4 pattern nodes in OHM (AND→OR conversion, autocatalytic text system, evaluation trap, external cognition) with edges to existing value/pattern nodes.

Desired state: An automated bridge that:
1. Reads pattern/synthesis notes from the zettelkasten
2. Projects them into OHM as pattern/concept nodes with L3 APPLIES_TO edges
3. Projects source notes as L2 REFERENCES edges
4. Records observations (confidence, sigma) for pattern strength
5. Runs periodically during heartbeat or on zettelkasten sync

The bridge should be one-directional (zettelkasten → OHM) — OHM is for shared awareness, not replacing local memory. Challenges from other agents flow back via /listen().

Architecture: A script at /root/olympus/metis/graph/ohm_bridge.py that:
- Reads from graph/zettelkasten.kuzu
- Uses ohm_client.OHMClient to write to OHM
- Tracks projected notes in a local mapping file to avoid duplicates
- Runs as part of heartbeat or on-demand

Activation: When OHM has 500+ nodes or agents report search relevance issues.
close_reason: Deferred to 500+ nodes, but improving description.

title: RESEARCH: Semantic search via embeddings and VSS index
description:
OHM currently uses ILIKE for text search, which works at <1K nodes but won't scale semantically. DuckDB's VSS extension supports HNSW indexes and cosine similarity search — both tested and working.

The bigger question: should OHM generate embeddings, or should agents provide them?

Option A — Agent-side embedding:
- Agents generate embeddings via Ollama (nomic-embed-text, 768-dim) when creating nodes
- POST /node accepts optional 'embedding' field (FLOAT[] or base64)
- OHM stores it and indexes with HNSW
- GET /search?mode=semantic&q_vector=... or POST /search with content that gets embedded server-side

Option B — Server-side embedding:
- OHM calls an embedding model on node creation/update
- Requires embedding service (Ollama API endpoint in config)
- More automatic but adds a dependency and latency

Option C — Hybrid:
- Agents CAN provide embeddings, but OHM also has an optional embedding service
- If no embedding provided and service configured, generate one
- Best flexibility, most complexity

Métris's zettelkasten already uses embedding-based similarity for discovery (discover.py suggest). The convergence point: OHM becomes the shared graph where embedding-based semantic search complements hop-based neighborhood traversal.

Activation trigger: When OHM hits 500+ nodes or agents report search relevance issues.

DuckDB VSS extension verified working:
- HNSW index creation: CREATE INDEX USING HNSW (embedding) WITH (metric = 'cosine')
- Cosine similarity: array_cosine_similarity(embedding, query_vector)
- Tested on DuckDB 1.5.2

FTS extension note: DuckDB FTS v1.5.2 has a PRAGMA syntax issue (treats index name as column ref). ILIKE is sufficient at current scale. Revisit FTS when search performance degrades or when DuckDB fixes the syntax.
close_reason: Research needed first — improving description.

title: FEATURE: Label substring filtering on list_nodes endpoint
description:
Current behavior: GET /nodes supports filtering by node_type and exact label match. No substring/pattern matching on labels.

Use case: Deepthought wants list_nodes(node_type='concept', label_contains='china') to find all concept nodes with 'china' in the label. Currently must use /search which only does text matching on a single query term.

Proposed API extension:
  GET /nodes?type=concept&label_contains=china&limit=20&offset=0
  
  Also support label_prefix:
  GET /nodes?label_prefix=hormuz → matches 'Hormuz Ceasefire', 'Hormuz Analysis', etc.

Implementation notes:
- DuckDB LIKE '%china%' for substring, LIKE 'hormuz%' for prefix
- Case-insensitive matching (ILIKE)
- Add indexes if needed for performance at scale
- Can be combined with existing type filter

Verified need: Deepthought integration testing 2026-05-18.
acceptance_criteria:
1. GET /nodes?label_contains=<term> returns nodes with matching labels (case-insensitive)
2. GET /nodes?label_prefix=<term> returns nodes with matching label prefix
3. Works with existing type, limit, offset parameters
4. Performance: <50ms on 1000+ node graph

title: ohmd: SSE /events filter by layer and agent
description: Currently /events streams all changes. An agent that only cares about L3 changes from Vegetation Agent must receive the entire feed and filter client-side. Add query params to /events: ?layer=L3,L4&agent=vegetation_agent&node_type=PASTURE. This reduces noise for agents that don't need full coverage. Implementation: the SSE subscriber registry already exists (from OH-M-9hx.2); just add filtering logic before pushing events to each subscriber.
acceptance_criteria: GET /events?layer=L3&agent=vegetation_agent returns only L3 events from vegetation agent; multiple filters combinable (?layer=L1,L2&node_type=ANIMAL); no auth required for filter params; backward compatible (no filter = full feed)

title: Follow-on: extract PATCH handlers into GraphHandlerMixin
description:
Why: _do_PATCH in server.py currently handles PATCH inline — the node, edge, and task mutation logic is embedded directly in the dispatch method rather than in separate _patch_node/_patch_edge/_patch_task methods like every other verb. This is inconsistent with the mixin pattern established by OHM-xily and makes the PATCH code invisible to the handler groupings.

What needs to be done:
1. Extract the PATCH /node/ block from _do_PATCH into _patch_node(path, body, agent) on GraphHandlerMixin
2. Extract the PATCH /edge/ block from _do_PATCH into _patch_edge(path, body, agent) on GraphHandlerMixin
3. Extract the PATCH /tasks/ block from _do_PATCH into _patch_task(path, body, agent) on GraphHandlerMixin
4. _do_PATCH becomes a thin dispatcher calling the extracted methods
5. Run: python -m pytest tests/test_server.py -x -q -k 'patch' — must pass
6. Run full suite: python -m pytest tests/ -x -q — 1,367+ tests must pass

Depends on OHM-xily being complete (GraphHandlerMixin must exist first).
notes: P4: follow-on, only relevant after all handler extractions complete.
close_reason: subset of OHM-hpxa (GraphHandlerMixin extraction) — PATCH handlers will be extracted as part of that work

title: Quack concurrent multi-writer: define production readiness criteria and activation path
description:
Why: The Quack extension (DuckDB concurrent multi-writer) is implemented in src/ohm/graph/quack.py and tested, but is not active in production. The current single-writer mutex per tenant is a throughput ceiling for multi-agent workloads. The architecture anticipates Quack as the solution but there is no stated criteria for when it is safe to activate.

What needs to be done: Define production readiness criteria for Quack mode: (1) extension stability in DuckDB core_nightly, (2) token auth and TLS proxy requirements, (3) test coverage for concurrent writes under the existing test suite, (4) ohmd config flag to opt-in (quack_mode: true). Document the activation path in CLAUDE.md or an ADR. This issue is a scoping/decision task — implementation follows once criteria are agreed.
notes: P4: production readiness criteria is a design doc, not code. Quack works for current use case.
close_reason: Created ADR-016 documenting Quack production readiness criteria: (1) extension stability requires DuckDB stable release with Quack (currently only core_nightly), (2) security requires 32+ char tokens via env vars and TLS proxy, (3) test coverage requires passing concurrent write test (currently failing — blocks activation), (4) ohmd config flag quack:true to opt-in, (5) 4-phase activation path from mutex to Quack. Also added Quack notes to CLAUDE.md.

title: Make embedding backend pluggable — currently hardwired to Ollama mxbai-embed-large
description:
Why: Node embeddings are generated via Ollama running mxbai-embed-large locally. This is wired directly into OhmStore. Swapping to a different embedding provider (OpenAI, Cohere, sentence-transformers, or disabling embeddings entirely) requires code changes. Operators in airgapped environments, cost-sensitive deployments, or with existing embedding infrastructure need a clean configuration path.

What needs to be done: Define an EmbeddingBackend protocol/ABC in src/ohm/graph/embeddings.py with a single embed(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]] interface. Provide three built-in implementations: OllamaBackend (existing), OpenAIBackend, and NullBackend (returns zero vectors, used when embeddings not needed). OhmStore accepts an optional embedding_backend= parameter; defaults to NullBackend if Ollama is not reachable. Backend selection configurable via ohmd config file.
notes: P4: nice-to-have. Ollama embeddings work fine for current use case.

title: SECURITY: Public-read model exposes all graph data by default — risky for medical/cyber
description:
The default require_read_auth=False configuration means all graph data is publicly readable without authentication. This is appropriate for personal/single-tenant use but dangerous for multi-tenant deployments in medical, cybersecurity, or financial domains where tenants expect their graph to be private.

The code does NOT auto-force require_read_auth=True in multi-tenant mode (line 3901). The operator must explicitly set OHM_REQUIRE_READ_AUTH=1 or --require-read-auth flag. If they don't, every tenant's graph is world-readable.

FIX: In multi-tenant mode (TenantManager active), default require_read_auth=True. Single-tenant mode keeps the current False default.
notes: Downgraded P2→P4: public-read is correct default for internal research graphs. Domain-specific access control only needed when paying customers in regulated industries onboard. Revisit when tss4.14/16 ship.
close_reason: Multi-tenant mode now defaults to require_read_auth=True. When --multi-tenant is set and require_read_auth is not explicitly configured, all reads require authentication. This protects multi-tenant deployments from public exposure. Single-tenant mode continues to default to public-read.

title: SECURITY: DuckLake sync table names interpolated in SQL — fragile internal pattern
description: DuckLake sync in store.py or tenant.py interpolates table names into SQL using f-strings. While these are internal table names (not user-supplied), the pattern is fragile — any future refactor that makes the table name configurable would immediately become a SQL injection. Should use identifier quoting (double-quote escaping) for all table names in SQL.
notes: P4: real but low priority. Batch-fix candidate for OpenCode parallel session.
close_reason: fixed — DuckLake sync table names now use quoted identifier helper. No more raw f-string interpolation of table names in SQL.

title: BUG: all_edge_types property recomputes on every access — should be cached
description: The all_edge_types property on SchemaConfig (or equivalent) runs a SQL query or iterates all edges on every property access. High-frequency callers (validation, boundary checks) pay this cost repeatedly. Should be cached at construction time and invalidated only on schema update.
notes: P4: real but low priority. Batch-fix candidate for OpenCode parallel session.
close_reason: fixed — all_edge_types property now cached with _all_edge_types_cache

title: BUG: DELETE node/edge uses inline ownership check instead of boundary module
description: DELETE /node/{id} and /edge/{id} in server.py implement their own inline ownership check instead of calling the boundary module (enforce_write_boundary or equivalent). This creates two divergent implementations that can drift. ADR-003 specifies boundary module as the single source of truth for write access decisions.
notes: P4: real but low priority. Batch-fix candidate for OpenCode parallel session.

title: BUG: _write_meta has no fsync between write and replace — crash leaves corrupt .tmp
description: _write_meta in tenant.py writes to a .tmp file then does atomic rename. But there is no fsync/flush before the rename. On a crash between write and rename, the .tmp file is left behind. On next start, if .tmp is detected and used, it may be partially written. fsync() should be called before rename to guarantee durability.
notes: P4: real but low priority. Batch-fix candidate for OpenCode parallel session.

title: BUG: tenant_health accesses entry.store.conn after lock release — may be closed
description: tenant_health in tenant.py reads WAL size or other metrics via entry.store.conn after releasing _cache_lock. If a concurrent eviction closes the store between lock release and the conn access, this raises on a closed connection. Low frequency race but can produce spurious 500s on health checks.
notes: P4: real but low priority. Batch-fix candidate for OpenCode parallel session.

title: Provision seeds 0 agent role nodes — spec says domain agents should be pre-seeded
description: OHM_MULTI_TENANCY_BACKLOG.md says TenantManager.provision() should 'seed initial agent role nodes based on domain + tier (e.g., dispatch_analyst for home_services scout)'. Currently provision creates 0 nodes. The domain templates define types and provenances but no pre-seeded agent data. Acceptable for v1 but should be tracked.
notes: P4: seeding agent role nodes is cosmetic. Tenants work without pre-seeded agents.
close_reason: Added seed_agents to SchemaConfig and _seed_domain_agents() function. When a tenant is provisioned with a domain template (home_services, healthcare, manufacturing, construction, ohm), the specified agent role nodes are pre-seeded in both ohm_nodes and ohm_agent_state tables. Templates updated with appropriate roles: dispatch_analyst/compliance_planner/operations_manager/parts_broker/schedule_coordinator for home_services; billing_specialist/clinical_coordinator/practice_manager for healthcare; process_engineer/maintenance_supervisor/shift_supervisor for manufacturing; project_manager/safety_officer/estimator for construction; metis/clio/socrates/hephaestus/deepthought for OHM.

title: OHM Client Communication: Multi-channel owner engagement for TeamWork AI
description: Separate epic for client-facing communication channels. These are product features, not multi-tenancy isolation infrastructure. The multi-tenancy epic (OHM-tss4) should focus on core isolation concerns (TenantManager, auth, routing, migration). Communication channels depend on multi-tenancy infrastructure but are a parallel product track. Channels: SMS (Twilio), Email (SendGrid), Multi-Channel Router, Slack (Bolt SDK), Teams (Bot Framework). Also covers: channel credential management, message queue backing store, and domain-specific communication templates.
design: Queue: ohm_messages DuckDB table (id, customer_id, channel, payload_json, status, created_at, sent_at, error, retry_count) in core store. Background dispatcher thread polls every 60s — no external queue needed. Channel credentials read from tenant meta.json integrations field (built by OHM-uwl2). Implementation sequence: (1) tss4.15 Email/SendGrid first — simplest REST API, most universal. jb2x credential lifecycle implemented as part of tss4.15. (2) tss4.14 SMS/Twilio only if a customer actively requests it — carrier compliance (opt-in/out, 10DLC) adds significant complexity. (3) tss4.17 Slack and tss4.18 Teams deferred — require OAuth app registration and admin approval flows. Router (tss4.16) extracted from tss4.15 implementation, not built first.
notes:
Do not start tss4.14/tss4.16/tss4.17/tss4.18 until tss4.15 is shipped and a real customer needs the next channel. Build one channel well rather than scaffolding all channels at once.
DEPRIORITIZED: Ship security before features. Encryption at rest (OHM-yl1f) must land before customer PII hits this platform.
Premature per Matt's direction — ship security/infra before comms.
DEMOTED P3→P4: Way too early for client comms. No tenants using OHM yet. Ship infra and agent integration first.
close_reason: won't fix — client communications handled by OpenClaw, not OHM. OpenClaw owns channel routing (Signal, Telegram, Discord, email). OHM owns the knowledge graph. No duplication.

title: Client Communication: Email Channel — SendGrid weekly summaries + rich content
description: Secondary client communication channel. Email via SendGrid for weekly summary reports, document attachments, and customers who prefer email. HTML templates. Monthly performance reviews. Compliance documents.
notes:
START HERE: (1) Add ohm_messages table to OhmStore schema in src/ohm/graph/db.py — CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ohm_messages (id VARCHAR PRIMARY KEY, customer_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, channel VARCHAR NOT NULL, payload_json JSON, status VARCHAR DEFAULT 'pending', created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(), sent_at TIMESTAMP, error VARCHAR, retry_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0). (2) Add TenantManager.enqueue_message(customer_id, channel, payload) that INSERTs into ohm_messages in the core store. (3) Add _MessageDispatcher background thread to server.py (like _checkpoint_loop) that polls SELECT * FROM ohm_messages WHERE status='pending' AND retry_count<3 LIMIT 10 every 60s, dispatches via requests.post to SendGrid /v3/mail/send using integrations.sendgrid.api_key from tenant meta.json, marks sent/error. (4) Wire POST /tenant/{id}/message admin endpoint in server.py. (5) Use monkeypatch/responses to mock SendGrid in tests — no real API calls.
DEPRIORITIZED: Blocked on security. Client communication before encryption = PII on insecure platform.
Parent epic (OHM-glni) deprioritized. Downgrade follows.
DEMOTED P3→P4: Parent epic (OHM-glni) deprioritized. Follows.
close_reason: won't fix — parent epic (OHM-glni) closed. Client comms handled by OpenClaw.

title: 28 orphan nodes in graph including test artifacts — need cleanup and prevention
description: The graph has 28 orphan nodes (no edges) including test artifacts: 'xxx...' (very long ID), 'val-task-1', 'test-node-api-check', 'obs-*' observation nodes. Test artifacts should be cleaned up after tests, and the test suite should use isolated DBs to avoid polluting shared state. Need: (1) DELETE orphan test artifacts from production graph, (2) ensure test fixtures use in-memory or temp DBs, (3) add periodic orphan GC or surfacing in /health.
notes: Data maintenance, not code. Run cleanup script and add test-artifact prevention to CI.
close_reason: won't fix — data maintenance, not code. Clean up test nodes in running instance and add CI prevention separately.

title: PERT elicitation automation from agent knowledge
description: PERT elicitation currently requires manual p05/p50/p95 entry. Agents have knowledge graphs with confidence values, observation histories, and edge probabilities that could inform PERT estimates automatically. Build: (1) auto_pert_from_observations() — derive PERT from observation history, (2) auto_pert_from_edges() — derive from incoming/outgoing edge probability distributions, (3) CLI/SDK wrappers for agent-driven PERT elicitation workflows.
notes: Downgraded P3→P4: PERT automation blocked on agent OHM usage (g9uv). Now that g9uv is addressed, PERT automation becomes useful but is still speculative — no agents currently provide PERT values. Merge with agent maturity.
close_reason: Implemented PERT elicitation automation (OHM-8fg9): (1) auto_pert_from_observations() derives PERT from observation history using empirical percentiles, (2) auto_pert_from_edges() derives PERT from incoming/outgoing edge probability distributions, (3) CLI command: ohm graph pert-auto <node_id> [--source observations|edges], (4) SDK wrappers graph.auto_pert_from_observations() and graph.auto_pert_from_edges(), (5) 23 new test cases covering all auto-PERT functions and scale_pert_variance. Fixed circular import in bayesian.py by using relative imports.

title: PERT elicitation automation — populate p05/p50/p95 on edges from agent knowledge
description: AGENT WORK — not a dev-team task. The pert.py module and API endpoints exist. Agents (metis, clio, socrates) populate PERT estimates on edges via POST /edge with probability_p05/p50/p95. Need: (1) Agent prompts for PERT estimates, (2) Bulk PERT update endpoint (already: POST /observations for nodes, need PATCH /edge for PERT fields), (3) Mixture-of-experts solicitation for high-utility_scale decisions.
notes: P4: merge with OHM-8fg9 (same feature, different scope).
close_reason: Closed
